title: Robust Transmission Design for RIS-Assisted High-Speed Train Communication Coverage Enhancement With Imperfect Cascaded Channels
summary: Reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) has recently been gained attention as an effective technique improving the coverage and performance of communication systems by creating additional communication links. Deployment of RIS is crucial for overcoming signal coverage limitations, especially in high-speed train (HST) scenarios. Considerable research has been performed assuming perfect channel state information (CSI). However, due to the rapidly time-varying fading channels and feedback delays, achieving perfect CSI at the base station (BS) is not feasible in the HST scenarios. To tackle this problem, this paper investigates a robust design strategy for RIS-aided HST communication coverage enhancement, particularly focusing on cascaded BS-RIS-user channels at BS (CBRUB). The study explores the optimization problem under two types distinct of models: centered on minimizing transmit power subject to worst-case rate constraints within the bounded CSI error (BCSIE) model, and the other focusing on outage probability (OP) constraints under the statistical CSI error (SCSIE) model. We use the S-procedure to approximate the non-convex (NC) constraints, converting the worst-case rate constraints into linear matrix inequalities. Additionally, the Bernstein-type inequality is applied to transform the OP constraints into second-order cone constraints and linear inequalities. The simulation analysis results show that CBRUB errors have a significant effect on system performance compared to direct CSI errors.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes robust RIS design for high-speed train communications under imperfect CSI, addressing two error models: bounded CSI errors with worst-case rate constraints and statistical CSI errors with outage probability constraints.
AI.motivation: RIS deployment is crucial for HST communications but perfect CSI is infeasible due to rapidly time-varying channels and feedback delays, necessitating robust design strategies under imperfect CSI conditions.
AI.method: The study investigates two optimization problems: 1) minimizing transmit power under worst-case rate constraints using bounded CSI error model with S-procedure for constraint approximation, and 2) focusing on outage probability constraints under statistical CSI error model using Bernstein-type inequality for constraint transformation.
AI.result: Simulation results show that cascaded BS-RIS-user channel errors at the BS (CBRUB errors) have a more significant impact on system performance compared to direct CSI errors.
AI.conclusion: The proposed robust design strategies effectively handle imperfect CSI in RIS-aided HST communications, with particular attention needed for cascaded channel errors which are more critical than direct channel errors.

title: Five Constructions of Asymptotically Optimal Aperiodic Doppler Resilient Complementary Sequence Sets with New Parameters
summary: Sequences exhibiting favorable ambiguity function characteristics play a critical role in radar detection systems and modern mobile communication applications. As a newly developed sequence family, Doppler resilient complementary sequence sets (DRCSSs) can effectively suppress ambiguity function sidelobes by coherently combining the ambiguity functions of their constituent subsequences. The objective of this paper is to present five classes of asymptotically optimal aperiodic DRCSSs with novel parameters based on trace functions over finite fields and column orthogonal complex matrices. Compared with existing asymptotically optimal aperiodic DRCSSs in the literature, the proposed aperiodic DRCSSs deliver superior or novel parameters. Notably, for three families of the constructed aperiodic DRCSSs, the column sequence peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR) is upper bounded by p by selecting suitable column orthogonal complex matrices.

AI.tldr: This paper presents five new classes of asymptotically optimal aperiodic Doppler resilient complementary sequence sets (DRCSSs) with novel parameters using trace functions over finite fields and column orthogonal complex matrices.
AI.motivation: Sequences with good ambiguity function properties are crucial for radar detection and mobile communications. DRCSSs can effectively suppress ambiguity function sidelobes by coherently combining subsequence ambiguity functions, but existing asymptotically optimal aperiodic DRCSSs have limitations in parameters.
AI.method: The authors construct five classes of asymptotically optimal aperiodic DRCSSs using trace functions over finite fields and column orthogonal complex matrices. They leverage mathematical structures from finite fields and complex matrix theory to design sequences with desired properties.
AI.result: The proposed aperiodic DRCSSs achieve superior or novel parameters compared to existing ones. Notably, three families of the constructed sequences have column sequence peak-to-average power ratio (PAPR) upper bounded by p (where p is likely a prime or field characteristic) through appropriate selection of column orthogonal complex matrices.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully presents five new classes of asymptotically optimal aperiodic DRCSSs with improved parameters and bounded PAPR, advancing the design of sequences with favorable ambiguity function characteristics for radar and communication applications.

title: Lossless Compression via Chained Lightweight Neural Predictors with Information Inheritance
summary: This paper is dedicated to lossless data compression with probability estimation using neural networks. First, we propose a probability estimation architecture based on a chain of neural predictors, so that each unit of the chain is defined as a neural network with the minimum possible number of weights, which is sufficient for efficient compression of data generated by Markov sources of a given order. We show that this architecture allows us to minimize the overall number of weights participating in the probability estimation process depending on the statistical properties of the input data. Second, in order to improve compression efficiency, we introduce an information inheritance mechanism, where the probability estimate obtained by a low-order unit is used at the next higher-order unit. Experimental results show that the proposed lossless data compressor equipped with the chained probability estimation architecture provides compression ratios close to the state-of-the-art PAC compressor. At the same time, it outperforms PAC by a factor of 1.2 to 6.3 in encoding throughput and by a factor of 2.8 to 12.3 in decoding throughput on a consumer GPU.

AI.tldr: Neural network-based lossless compression with chained probability estimation architecture that achieves near-state-of-the-art compression ratios while significantly outperforming PAC in encoding/decoding throughput.
AI.motivation: To develop efficient lossless data compression with neural probability estimation that balances compression ratio with computational performance, addressing the need for fast encoding/decoding while maintaining competitive compression efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes a chained neural predictor architecture where each unit is a minimal neural network for Markov sources of given order, with information inheritance mechanism where lower-order probability estimates feed into higher-order units.
AI.result: Compression ratios close to state-of-the-art PAC compressor, with 1.2-6.3x faster encoding throughput and 2.8-12.3x faster decoding throughput on consumer GPU.
AI.conclusion: The chained probability estimation architecture provides an effective balance between compression efficiency and computational performance, making neural-based lossless compression more practical for real-world applications.

title: Rate-Distortion Theory for Deductive Sources under Closure Fidelity
summary: We study lossy compression of a finite statement source generated in a fixed deductive environment. The source symbols are statements in a knowledge base endowed with a proof system, and reconstruction fidelity is measured by preservation of deductive closure rather than by symbolwise equality. This induces, once the proof system and canonical order are fixed, a decomposition of the source into an irredundant core and redundant stored consequences. Under a natural disjointness condition on zero-distortion reconstruction sets, we show that the minimum zero-distortion rate equals the source mass of the core times the entropy of the source conditioned on that core. For reconstruction alphabets contained in the deductive closure of the source knowledge base, we further prove that the full rate-distortion function depends only on the core, so redundant states are invisible to both rate and distortion. When the decoder is limited to a bounded number of inference steps, we obtain an exact fixed depth rate-delay-distortion characterization. Under an additional order-robustness assumption identifying the chosen core with the order-free essential set, this characterization interpolates between classical symbolwise compression and unconstrained deductive compression. These results formulate deductive compression as a structured source coding problem and quantify how shared inference structure changes the fundamental limits of communication.

AI.tldr: Deductive compression treats statements in a knowledge base as source symbols, measuring fidelity by preservation of deductive closure rather than symbolwise equality, revealing that redundant consequences are invisible to rate-distortion tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: Traditional lossy compression measures fidelity by symbolwise equality, but for deductive systems (like knowledge bases with proof systems), what matters is preservation of logical consequences - the deductive closure. The paper aims to understand how shared inference structure changes fundamental communication limits.
AI.method: The paper studies lossy compression of finite statement sources in deductive environments. It decomposes sources into irredundant core and redundant consequences, analyzes zero-distortion rates under disjointness conditions, and characterizes rate-distortion functions for reconstruction alphabets contained in deductive closure. It also examines bounded inference steps (rate-delay-distortion) and order-robustness assumptions.
AI.result: Minimum zero-distortion rate equals source mass of core times conditional entropy. Redundant statements are invisible to both rate and distortion when reconstruction is within deductive closure. With bounded inference steps, exact fixed depth rate-delay-distortion characterization is obtained. Under order-robustness, characterization interpolates between classical symbolwise compression and unconstrained deductive compression.
AI.conclusion: Deductive compression is formulated as a structured source coding problem where shared inference structure fundamentally changes communication limits. The core of a knowledge base determines compression performance while redundant consequences don't affect rate-distortion tradeoffs, showing how logical structure enables more efficient communication.

title: Portable Medical Imaging in Modern Healthcare: Fundamentals, AI-Based Taxonomy, Image Quality, and Open Challenges
summary: Portable medical imaging (PMI) has emerged as an important solution for point-of-care diagnosis in emergency, rural, and resource-limited settings where conventional imaging infrastructure is not readily available. Modalities such as portable computed tomography, portable magnetic resonance imaging, portable ultrasound, and wireless capsule endoscopy improve access to timely diagnosis, but they remain highly vulnerable to image-quality degradation caused by motion artifacts, environmental interference, hardware limitations, and unstable acquisition conditions. This review provides a systematic and quality-centered synthesis of recent advances in PMI. It introduces a taxonomy of AI-based PMI methods spanning machine learning, deep learning, transfer learning, and Transformer-based approaches, and examines their roles in image enhancement, reconstruction, quality assessment, detection, and classification. The review also analyzes PMI devices, sensing pipelines, modality-specific distortions, evaluation metrics, and publicly available datasets. In contrast to existing surveys that are mainly modality-driven or application-focused, this work emphasizes the relationship between image quality, AI robustness, and clinical usability in portable settings. Finally, it identifies current research gaps and outlines future directions toward reliable, interpretable, and clinically deployable PMI systems.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews AI-based methods for improving portable medical imaging quality, focusing on how AI robustness relates to clinical usability in resource-limited settings.
AI.motivation: Portable medical imaging is crucial for point-of-care diagnosis in emergency, rural, and resource-limited settings, but suffers from quality degradation due to motion artifacts, environmental interference, hardware limitations, and unstable acquisition conditions.
AI.method: The review provides a systematic synthesis of AI-based PMI methods, introducing a taxonomy spanning machine learning, deep learning, transfer learning, and Transformer-based approaches. It examines their roles in image enhancement, reconstruction, quality assessment, detection, and classification, while also analyzing PMI devices, sensing pipelines, modality-specific distortions, evaluation metrics, and datasets.
AI.result: The review emphasizes the relationship between image quality, AI robustness, and clinical usability in portable settings, contrasting with existing surveys that are mainly modality-driven or application-focused.
AI.conclusion: The paper identifies current research gaps and outlines future directions toward reliable, interpretable, and clinically deployable portable medical imaging systems.

authors: Arash Ajoudani adn Georgios Th. Papadopoulos
title: Foundation Models in Robotics: A Comprehensive Review of Methods, Models, Datasets, Challenges and Future Research Directions
summary: Over the recent years, the field of robotics has been undergoing a transformative paradigm shift from fixed, single-task, domain-specific solutions towards adaptive, multi-function, general-purpose agents, capable of operating in complex, open-world, and dynamic environments. This tremendous advancement is primarily driven by the emergence of Foundation Models (FMs), i.e., large-scale neural-network architectures trained on massive, heterogeneous datasets that provide unprecedented capabilities in multi-modal understanding and reasoning, long-horizon planning, and cross-embodiment generalization. In this context, the current study provides a holistic, systematic, and in-depth review of the research landscape of FMs in robotics. In particular, the evolution of the field is initially delineated through five distinct research phases, spanning from the early incorporation of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Computer Vision (CV) models to the current frontier of multi-sensory generalization and real-world deployment. Subsequently, a highly-granular taxonomic investigation of the literature is performed, examining the following key aspects: a) the employed FM types, including LLMs, VFMs, VLMs, and VLAs, b) the underlying neural-network architectures, c) the adopted learning paradigms, d) the different learning stages of knowledge incorporation, e) the major robotic tasks, and f) the main real-world application domains. For each aspect, comparative analysis and critical insights are provided. Moreover, a report on the publicly available datasets used for model training and evaluation across the considered robotic tasks is included. Furthermore, a hierarchical discussion on the current open challenges and promising future research directions in the field is incorporated.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive review of Foundation Models (FMs) in robotics, covering their evolution, taxonomy, datasets, and future directions.
AI.motivation: Robotics is shifting from fixed, single-task systems to adaptive, general-purpose agents. Foundation Models (large-scale neural networks trained on massive datasets) are driving this transformation by enabling multi-modal understanding, planning, and cross-embodiment generalization.
AI.method: The study provides a systematic review by: 1) delineating the field's evolution through five research phases, 2) conducting granular taxonomic analysis of FM types (LLMs, VFMs, VLMs, VLAs), architectures, learning paradigms, knowledge incorporation stages, robotic tasks, and application domains, 3) analyzing publicly available datasets, and 4) discussing challenges and future directions.
AI.result: A holistic review that maps the research landscape of FMs in robotics, providing comparative analysis and critical insights across all examined aspects, along with comprehensive dataset reporting and hierarchical discussion of challenges.
AI.conclusion: Foundation Models are revolutionizing robotics by enabling adaptive, multi-function agents for complex environments. The review systematically organizes the field's evolution and current state while identifying open challenges and promising research directions for future development.

title: DeepER-Med: Advancing Deep Evidence-Based Research in Medicine Through Agentic AI
summary: Trustworthiness and transparency are essential for the clinical adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) in healthcare and biomedical research. Recent deep research systems aim to accelerate evidence-grounded scientific discovery by integrating AI agents with multi-hop information retrieval, reasoning, and synthesis. However, most existing systems lack explicit and inspectable criteria for evidence appraisal, creating a risk of compounding errors and making it difficult for researchers and clinicians to assess the reliability of their outputs. In parallel, current benchmarking approaches rarely evaluate performance on complex, real-world medical questions. Here, we introduce DeepER-Med, a Deep Evidence-based Research framework for Medicine with an agentic AI system. DeepER-Med frames deep medical research as an explicit and inspectable workflow of evidence-based generation, consisting of three modules: research planning, agentic collaboration, and evidence synthesis. To support realistic evaluation, we also present DeepER-MedQA, an evidence-grounded dataset comprising 100 expert-level research questions derived from authentic medical research scenarios and curated by a multidisciplinary panel of 11 biomedical experts. Expert manual evaluation demonstrates that DeepER-Med consistently outperforms widely used production-grade platforms across multiple criteria, including the generation of novel scientific insights. We further demonstrate the practical utility of DeepER-Med through eight real-world clinical cases. Human clinician assessment indicates that DeepER-Med's conclusions align with clinical recommendations in seven cases, highlighting its potential for medical research and decision support.

AI.tldr: DeepER-Med is an AI framework for medical research that uses explicit, inspectable workflows for evidence-based generation, outperforming existing platforms and showing practical utility in clinical cases.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems for medical research lack explicit evidence appraisal criteria, risking compounding errors and making reliability assessment difficult. Existing benchmarks also fail to evaluate performance on complex real-world medical questions.
AI.method: DeepER-Med frames medical research as an explicit workflow with three modules: research planning, agentic collaboration, and evidence synthesis. The authors also created DeepER-MedQA, a dataset of 100 expert-level medical questions curated by 11 biomedical experts for realistic evaluation.
AI.result: DeepER-Med consistently outperforms widely used production-grade platforms across multiple criteria, including generating novel scientific insights. In eight real-world clinical cases, human clinicians found its conclusions aligned with clinical recommendations in seven cases.
AI.conclusion: DeepER-Med demonstrates potential for trustworthy and transparent AI in medical research and clinical decision support by providing explicit, inspectable evidence-based workflows that align with clinical practice.

title: Zoom Consistency: A Free Confidence Signal in Multi-Step Visual Grounding Pipelines
summary: Multi-step zoom-in pipelines are widely used for GUI grounding, yet the intermediate predictions they produce are typically discarded after coordinate remapping. We observe that these intermediate outputs contain a useful confidence signal for free: zoom consistency, the distance between a model's step-2 prediction and the crop center. Unlike log-probabilities or token-level uncertainty, zoom consistency is a geometric quantity in a shared coordinate space, making it directly comparable across architecturally different VLMs without calibration. We prove this quantity is a linear estimator of step-1 spatial error under idealized conditions (perfect step-2, target within crop) and show it correlates with prediction correctness across two VLMs (AUC = 0.60; Spearman rho = -0.14, p < 10^{-6} for KV-Ground-8B; rho = -0.11, p = 0.0003 for Qwen3.5-27B). The correlation is small but consistent across models, application categories, and operating systems. As a proof-of-concept, we use zoom consistency to route between a specialist and generalist model, capturing 16.5% of the oracle headroom between them (+0.8%, McNemar p = 0.19). Code is available at https://github.com/omxyz/zoom-consistency-routing.

AI.tldr: Zoom consistency - the distance between a model's step-2 prediction and crop center in multi-step GUI grounding pipelines - serves as a free confidence signal that correlates with prediction correctness across different VLMs.
AI.motivation: Multi-step zoom-in pipelines for GUI grounding discard intermediate predictions after coordinate remapping, wasting potentially useful confidence signals. The authors observe that these intermediate outputs contain valuable geometric information about prediction reliability.
AI.method: The paper introduces "zoom consistency" as a confidence metric: the distance between a model's step-2 prediction and the crop center. This geometric quantity is analyzed theoretically (proving it's a linear estimator of step-1 spatial error under ideal conditions) and empirically evaluated across two VLMs (KV-Ground-8B and Qwen3.5-27B).
AI.result: Zoom consistency correlates with prediction correctness across both VLMs (AUC = 0.60; Spearman rho = -0.14, p < 10^{-6} for KV-Ground-8B; rho = -0.11, p = 0.0003 for Qwen3.5-27B). The correlation is small but consistent across models, application categories, and operating systems. As proof-of-concept, using zoom consistency to route between specialist and generalist models captured 16.5% of the oracle headroom between them.
AI.conclusion: Zoom consistency provides a free, architecture-agnostic confidence signal for GUI grounding that doesn't require calibration and can be used for practical applications like model routing, despite having only modest correlation strength.

title: A methodology to rank importance of frequencies and channels in electromyography data with Decision Tree classifiers
comment: 16 pages, 9 figures, 1 table. Published in Russian Journal of Nonlinear Dynamics, 2024
summary: This study presents a methodology for identifying the most informative frequencies and channels in electromyography (EMG) data to evaluate muscle recovery using Decision Tree classifiers. EMG signals, recorded from the vastus lateralis muscle during squat exercises, were analyzed across varying rest intervals to assess optimal recovery periods. By employing single Decision Tree classifiers, the study enhances interpretability, offering insights into feature importance - essential for applications in medical and sports settings where transparency is critical. The experimental protocol utilized a grid search for hyperparameter tuning and cross-validation to address class imbalance, ultimately achieving a reliable classification of rest intervals based on power spectral density features. The results indicate that a limited subset of highly informative features provides sufficient accuracy, suggesting that streamlined, interpretable models are effective for the evaluation of muscle recovery. This approach can guide future research in developing compact, robust models adapted to EMG-based diagnostics.

AI.tldr: Decision Tree classifiers identify key EMG frequencies/channels for muscle recovery assessment, showing limited informative features provide sufficient accuracy for interpretable muscle recovery evaluation.
AI.motivation: Need interpretable methods to evaluate muscle recovery from EMG data for medical/sports applications where transparency is critical, requiring identification of most informative frequencies and channels.
AI.method: Analyzed EMG signals from vastus lateralis during squats with varying rest intervals; used single Decision Tree classifiers for interpretability, grid search for hyperparameter tuning, cross-validation for class imbalance, and power spectral density features.
AI.result: Achieved reliable classification of rest intervals; limited subset of highly informative features provides sufficient accuracy, demonstrating streamlined interpretable models are effective for muscle recovery evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Interpretable Decision Tree models with selected informative features can effectively assess muscle recovery, guiding development of compact robust models for EMG-based diagnostics in medical/sports settings.

title: The Spectral Geometry of Thought: Phase Transitions, Instruction Reversal, Token-Level Dynamics, and Perfect Correctness Prediction in How Transformers Reason
summary: We discover that large language models exhibit \emph{spectral phase transitions} in their hidden activation spaces when engaging in reasoning versus factual recall. Through systematic spectral analysis across \textbf{11 models} spanning \textbf{5 architecture families} (Qwen, Pythia, Phi, Llama, DeepSeek-R1), we identify \textbf{seven} core phenomena: (1)~\textbf{Reasoning Spectral Compression} -- 9/11 models show significantly lower $α$ for reasoning ($p < 0.05$), with larger effects in stronger models; (2)~\textbf{Instruction Tuning Spectral Reversal} -- base models show reasoning $α< $ factual $α$, while instruction-tuned models reverse this relationship; (3)~\textbf{Architecture-Dependent Generation Taxonomy} -- prompt-to-response shifts partition into expansion, compression, and equilibrium regimes; (4)~\textbf{Spectral Scaling Law} -- $α_\text{reasoning} \propto -0.074 \ln N$ across 4 Qwen base models ($R^2 = 0.46$); (5)~\textbf{Token-Level Spectral Cascade} -- per-token alpha tracking reveals local synchronization that decays exponentially with layer distance, and is weaker for reasoning than factual tasks; (6)~\textbf{Reasoning Step Spectral Punctuation} -- phase-transition signatures align with reasoning step boundaries; and (7)~\textbf{Spectral Correctness Prediction} -- spectral $α$ alone achieves AUC $= 1.000$ (Qwen2.5-7B, late layers) and mean AUC $= 0.893$ across 6 models in predicting correctness \emph{before} the final answer is generated. Together, these findings establish a comprehensive \emph{spectral theory of reasoning} in transformers, revealing that the geometry of thought is universal in direction, architecture-specific in dynamics, and predictive of outcome.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit spectral phase transitions in activation spaces: reasoning tasks show spectral compression vs factual recall, with architecture-dependent dynamics and predictive power for correctness.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental differences in how LLMs process reasoning versus factual recall tasks at the spectral level, and to establish a spectral theory of reasoning in transformers.
AI.method: Systematic spectral analysis across 11 models spanning 5 architecture families (Qwen, Pythia, Phi, Llama, DeepSeek-R1), examining spectral properties, scaling laws, token-level dynamics, and prediction capabilities.
AI.result: Identified seven core phenomena: reasoning spectral compression, instruction tuning spectral reversal, architecture-dependent generation taxonomy, spectral scaling law, token-level spectral cascade, reasoning step spectral punctuation, and spectral correctness prediction with high AUC scores.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a comprehensive spectral theory of reasoning in transformers, revealing universal directional geometry, architecture-specific dynamics, and predictive power for outcomes before final answer generation.

title: Adaptive Power Allocation and User Scheduling for LEO Satellites using Channel Predictions
comment: 6 Pages, 2 figures, correspondence paper. This article has been accepted for publication in IEEE TVT. This is the authors' version. The final published version will be available in IEEE Xplore
summary: Low earth orbit (LEO) satellites are a key technology to enable connectivity for rural and remote users. Communication satellites in LEO can provide coverage to much larger areas than terrestrial or aerial systems, while offering improved data rates when compared with geostationary systems. However, a major challenge with LEO satellite communications is the high mobility of the satellite, which results in a rapidly changing communication channel. Due to this, it is challenging to fairly allocate communication resources to multiple users in the system. This work proposes an Adaptive Power Allocation and Scheduling Scheme (APASS) to ensure user fairness in the downlink of a LEO satellite system serving mobile ground users. First, a novel channel and transmission model is introduced to capture the variability in channel statistics due to the satellite's trajectory. Then, a non-convex optimization problem is formulated to maximize the minimum rate across all ground users over a fixed set of time slots. To solve this problem, the proposed APASS dynamically allocates power and schedules transmissions based on predicted future channel gains. Numerical results show that APASS achieves strong performance even with substantial prediction errors, faring close to an upper bound that assumes perfect future channel knowledge. Furthermore, it improves the minimum user rate by a factor of 2.98 compared to equal-power allocation and maintains user fairness with a Jain's fairness index of well above 0.99.

AI.tldr: APASS: Adaptive Power Allocation and Scheduling Scheme for LEO satellite downlink to ensure user fairness by dynamically allocating power and scheduling transmissions based on predicted channel gains.
AI.motivation: LEO satellites provide better coverage and data rates than terrestrial/geostationary systems, but their high mobility causes rapidly changing channels, making fair resource allocation to multiple users challenging.
AI.method: Proposes APASS: 1) Novel channel/transmission model capturing channel variability from satellite trajectory, 2) Formulates non-convex optimization to maximize minimum user rate over time slots, 3) Dynamically allocates power and schedules transmissions using predicted future channel gains.
AI.result: APASS achieves near-optimal performance even with prediction errors, improves minimum user rate by 2.98× vs equal-power allocation, and maintains high fairness (Jain's index >0.99).
AI.conclusion: APASS effectively addresses LEO satellite fairness challenges through adaptive power allocation and scheduling based on channel predictions, ensuring robust performance in dynamic environments.

title: RelativeFlow: Taming Medical Image Denoising Learning with Noisy Reference
summary: Medical image denoising (MID) lacks absolutely clean images for supervision, leading to a noisy reference problem that fundamentally limits denoising performance. Existing simulated-supervised discriminative learning (SimSDL) and simulated-supervised generative learning (SimSGL) treat noisy references as clean targets, causing suboptimal convergence or reference-biased learning, while self-supervised learning (SSL) imposes restrictive noise assumptions that are seldom satisfied in realistic MID scenarios. We propose \textbf{RelativeFlow}, a flow matching framework that learns from heterogeneous noisy references and drives inputs from arbitrary quality levels toward a unified high-quality target. RelativeFlow reformulates flow matching by decomposing the absolute noise-to-clean mapping into relative noisier-to-noisy mappings, and realizes this formulation through two key components: 1) consistent transport (CoT), a displacement map that constrains relative flows to be components of and progressively compose a unified absolute flow, and 2) simulation-based velocity field (SVF), which constructs a learnable velocity field using modality-specific degradation operators to support different medical imaging modalities. Extensive experiments on Computed Tomography (CT) and Magnetic Resonance (MR) denoising demonstrate that RelativeFlow significantly outperforms existing methods, taming MID with noisy references.

AI.tldr: RelativeFlow is a flow matching framework for medical image denoising that learns from heterogeneous noisy references by decomposing absolute noise-to-clean mapping into relative noisier-to-noisy mappings, overcoming limitations of existing supervised and self-supervised approaches.
AI.motivation: Medical image denoising lacks absolutely clean images for supervision, creating a noisy reference problem. Existing methods (SimSDL, SimSGL) treat noisy references as clean targets causing suboptimal convergence, while self-supervised learning imposes restrictive noise assumptions not satisfied in realistic medical imaging scenarios.
AI.method: RelativeFlow reformulates flow matching by decomposing absolute noise-to-clean mapping into relative noisier-to-noisy mappings. It uses two key components: 1) consistent transport (CoT) - a displacement map that constrains relative flows to progressively compose a unified absolute flow, and 2) simulation-based velocity field (SVF) - constructs learnable velocity field using modality-specific degradation operators for different medical imaging modalities.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Computed Tomography (CT) and Magnetic Resonance (MR) denoising demonstrate that RelativeFlow significantly outperforms existing methods, effectively addressing medical image denoising with noisy references.
AI.conclusion: RelativeFlow successfully tames the medical image denoising problem with noisy references by learning from heterogeneous noisy references and driving inputs from arbitrary quality levels toward a unified high-quality target through relative flow matching.

title: Iterated Invariant EKF for Quadruped Robot Odometry
summary: Kalman filter-based algorithms are fundamental for mobile robots, as they provide a computationally efficient solution to the challenging problem of state estimation. However, they rely on two main assumptions that are difficult to satisfy in practice: (a) the system dynamics must be linear with Gaussian process noise, and (b) the measurement model must also be linear with Gaussian measurement noise. Previous works have extended assumption (a) to nonlinear spaces through the Invariant Extended Kalman Filter (IEKF), showing that it retains properties similar to those of the classical Kalman filter when the system dynamics are group-affine on a Lie group. More recently, the counterpart of assumption (b) for the same nonlinear setting was addressed in [1]. By means of the proposed Iterated Invariant Extended Kalman Filter (IterIEKF), the authors of that work demonstrated that the update step exhibits several compatibility properties of the classical linear Kalman filter. In this work, we introduce a novel open-source state estimation algorithm for legged robots based on the IterIEKF. The update step of the proposed filter relies solely on proprioceptive measurements, exploiting kinematic constraints on foot velocity during contact and base-frame velocity, making it inherently robust to environmental conditions. Through extensive numerical simulations and evaluation on real-world datasets, we demonstrate that the IterIEKF outperforms the vanilla IEKF, the SO(3)-based Kalman Filter, and its iterated variant in terms of both accuracy and consistency.

AI.tldr: Novel open-source state estimation algorithm for legged robots based on Iterated Invariant Extended Kalman Filter (IterIEKF) that outperforms existing methods in accuracy and consistency.
AI.motivation: Kalman filters are computationally efficient for state estimation but rely on linear/Gaussian assumptions that are hard to satisfy in practice. While previous work extended linear dynamics to nonlinear spaces via IEKF, and recent work addressed nonlinear measurement models via IterIEKF, there's a need for robust state estimation specifically for legged robots using these advancements.
AI.method: Developed an open-source state estimation algorithm based on IterIEKF for legged robots. The update step uses only proprioceptive measurements, exploiting kinematic constraints on foot velocity during contact and base-frame velocity, making it inherently robust to environmental conditions.
AI.result: Through extensive numerical simulations and real-world dataset evaluations, the IterIEKF-based algorithm outperforms vanilla IEKF, SO(3)-based Kalman Filter, and its iterated variant in terms of both accuracy and consistency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed IterIEKF-based state estimation algorithm provides superior performance for legged robots by effectively handling nonlinearities while maintaining computational efficiency and robustness to environmental conditions.

title: GIST: Multimodal Knowledge Extraction and Spatial Grounding via Intelligent Semantic Topology
summary: Navigating complex, densely packed environments like retail stores, warehouses, and hospitals poses a significant spatial grounding challenge for humans and embodied AI. In these spaces, dense visual features quickly become stale given the quasi-static nature of items, and long-tail semantic distributions challenge traditional computer vision. While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) help assistive systems navigate semantically-rich spaces, they still struggle with spatial grounding in cluttered environments. We present GIST (Grounded Intelligent Semantic Topology), a multimodal knowledge extraction pipeline that transforms a consumer-grade mobile point cloud into a semantically annotated navigation topology. Our architecture distills the scene into a 2D occupancy map, extracts its topological layout, and overlays a lightweight semantic layer via intelligent keyframe and semantic selection. We demonstrate the versatility of this structured spatial knowledge through critical downstream Human-AI interaction tasks: (1) an intent-driven Semantic Search engine that actively infers categorical alternatives and zones when exact matches fail; (2) a one-shot Semantic Localizer achieving a 1.04 m top-5 mean translation error; (3) a Zone Classification module that segments the walkable floor plan into high-level semantic regions; and (4) a Visually-Grounded Instruction Generator that synthesizes optimal paths into egocentric, landmark-rich natural language routing. In multi-criteria LLM evaluations, GIST outperforms sequence-based instruction generation baselines. Finally, an in-situ formative evaluation (N=5) yields an 80% navigation success rate relying solely on verbal cues, validating the system's capacity for universal design.

AI.tldr: GIST is a multimodal pipeline that converts mobile point clouds into semantically annotated navigation topologies for spatial grounding in cluttered environments, enabling semantic search, localization, zone classification, and natural language routing.
AI.motivation: Current Vision-Language Models struggle with spatial grounding in dense, cluttered environments like retail stores and warehouses where visual features become stale and semantic distributions are long-tailed, creating challenges for embodied AI navigation.
AI.method: GIST transforms consumer-grade mobile point clouds into semantically annotated navigation topologies by: 1) distilling scenes into 2D occupancy maps, 2) extracting topological layouts, and 3) overlaying lightweight semantic layers via intelligent keyframe and semantic selection.
AI.result: GIST achieves: 1) Semantic Search with categorical alternatives inference, 2) 1.04m top-5 mean translation error for semantic localization, 3) Zone classification of walkable floor plans, 4) Outperforms sequence-based instruction generation in LLM evaluations, and 5) 80% navigation success rate in formative evaluation.
AI.conclusion: GIST provides effective spatial grounding for human-AI interaction in complex environments through structured semantic-topological representations, demonstrating versatility across multiple downstream tasks and validating its capacity for universal design.

title: Weak-to-Strong Knowledge Distillation Accelerates Visual Learning
summary:
Large-scale visual learning is increasingly limited by training cost. Existing knowledge distillation methods transfer from a stronger teacher to a weaker student for compression or final-accuracy improvement. We instead investigate distillation to accelerate the training of strong students. We propose a generalizable plug-and-play recipe that freezes a weaker teacher, applies distillation only in early training, and turns it off once the student reaches and surpasses teacher-level performance. For ImageNet and CIFAR classification, this strategy reaches target thresholds much earlier, with up to 4.8 times speedup measured by epochs.
  We confirm that the method generalizes to other tasks and report 1.7 times epoch speedup for object detection on the COCO dataset, and 2.5 times earlier target-FID crossing for diffusion generation on the CIFAR-10 dataset, measured in steps. These findings validate our method as a universal speedup mechanism for visual learning.

AI.tldr: Early distillation from a weaker teacher accelerates training of strong students, achieving up to 4.8× epoch speedup on ImageNet/CIFAR, 1.7× on COCO detection, and 2.5× on CIFAR-10 diffusion generation.
AI.motivation: Large-scale visual learning is increasingly limited by training cost. Existing distillation methods focus on compression or final-accuracy improvement, but this work investigates distillation specifically for accelerating the training of strong students.
AI.method: Proposes a plug-and-play recipe: freeze a weaker teacher, apply distillation only in early training phases, and turn it off once the student surpasses teacher-level performance. This provides guidance during initial learning stages.
AI.result: Achieves up to 4.8 times epoch speedup for ImageNet and CIFAR classification, 1.7 times speedup for COCO object detection, and 2.5 times earlier target-FID crossing for CIFAR-10 diffusion generation.
AI.conclusion: The method serves as a universal speedup mechanism for visual learning across various tasks, demonstrating that early distillation from weaker teachers can significantly accelerate training of strong student models.

title: Direct and Ambient Backscatter Communications with a Dual-Function Radar Transmitter
summary: This work considers a system where a dual-function radar transmitter (source) performs direct communication with a reader while simultaneously enabling ambient backscatter communication from a tag. The source embeds its message into a coded pulse repeatedly transmitted over a frame, whereas the tag exploits the resulting environmental reverberation (clutter) as an ambient carrier to convey its own message. By leveraging the structure induced by the radar waveforms, we develop two signaling schemes. In the pilot-free scheme, the source and tag messages are conveyed through nonlinear vector modulation; the induced subspace structure enables both joint decoding, where all unknown quantities are simultaneously estimated, and disjoint decoding, where the tag codeword is recovered first, followed by the estimation of the source codeword and the channel vectors. In the pilot-aided scheme, pilot symbols and linearly modulated data symbols are embedded within each frame, enabling both non-iterative decoding based on pilot-derived channel estimates and iterative decoding via alternating channel estimation and data detection. We establish sufficient conditions on the source and tag codebooks that guarantee noiseless identifiability of the involved messages and channels. Finally, performance is evaluated in terms of source/tag error probabilities and channel-estimation accuracy, and the resulting system-level tradeoffs are discussed.

AI.tldr: A dual-function radar system enables simultaneous direct communication and ambient backscatter communication by leveraging radar waveform structure, with two signaling schemes (pilot-free and pilot-aided) for joint/disjoint decoding and iterative channel estimation.
AI.motivation: To develop an integrated system where a radar transmitter can simultaneously perform direct communication with a reader while enabling ambient backscatter communication from a tag, leveraging environmental reverberation as an ambient carrier for efficient spectrum and hardware utilization.
AI.method: Two signaling schemes: 1) Pilot-free scheme using nonlinear vector modulation where source and tag messages are jointly or disjointly decoded using induced subspace structure; 2) Pilot-aided scheme with pilot symbols and linearly modulated data enabling both non-iterative decoding (using pilot-derived channel estimates) and iterative decoding via alternating channel estimation and data detection.
AI.result: Established sufficient conditions on source and tag codebooks for noiseless identifiability of messages and channels; evaluated performance in terms of source/tag error probabilities and channel-estimation accuracy; discussed system-level tradeoffs between the two schemes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual-function radar communication system successfully enables simultaneous direct and ambient backscatter communication by leveraging radar waveform structure, with both pilot-free and pilot-aided schemes offering different tradeoffs in decoding complexity, channel estimation accuracy, and error performance.

title: Aletheia: Gradient-Guided Layer Selection for Efficient LoRA Fine-Tuning Across Architectures
comment: 11 pages, 5 figures, 2 frozen evidence campaigns, 81 experiment rows across 14 successful models and 8 architecture families, plus one documented failed Pythia/GPT-NeoX attempt
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has become the dominant parameter-efficient fine-tuning method for large language models, yet standard practice applies LoRA adapters uniformly to all transformer layers regardless of their relevance to the downstream task. We introduce Aletheia, a gradient-guided layer selection method that identifies the most task-relevant layers via a lightweight gradient probe and applies LoRA adapters only to those layers with asymmetric rank allocation. Across 81 experiment rows covering 14 successful models from 8 architecture families (0.5B-72B parameters, including dense and Mixture-of-Experts architectures), with one additional documented failed Pythia/GPT-NeoX attempt in Campaign 2, Aletheia achieves a 15-28% training speedup (mean 23.1%, p < 0.001) with bounded extra forgetting and broadly matched downstream behavior on the evaluated MMLU, GSM8K, and HumanEval benchmark pack. Across the tested families and scales, Campaign 1 shows a 100% per-model speed win rate and Campaign 2 shows broadly preserved downstream behavior within a bounded-degradation framing. Together these results support a practical model-economics claim: intelligent layer selection can make LoRA fine-tuning materially more efficient without introducing major downstream damage on the evaluated set.

AI.tldr: Aletheia is a gradient-guided layer selection method for LoRA fine-tuning that identifies task-relevant layers and applies adapters only to those layers with asymmetric rank allocation, achieving 15-28% training speedup with minimal downstream performance degradation.
AI.motivation: Standard LoRA practice applies adapters uniformly to all transformer layers regardless of their relevance to downstream tasks, which may be inefficient. The authors aim to make LoRA fine-tuning more efficient by selectively applying adapters only to task-relevant layers.
AI.method: Aletheia uses a lightweight gradient probe to identify the most task-relevant transformer layers, then applies LoRA adapters only to those selected layers with asymmetric rank allocation (different ranks for different layers based on their importance).
AI.result: Across 81 experiment rows covering 14 models (0.5B-72B parameters, including dense and MoE architectures), Aletheia achieves 15-28% training speedup (mean 23.1%, p < 0.001) with bounded extra forgetting and broadly matched downstream behavior on MMLU, GSM8K, and HumanEval benchmarks. Campaign 1 shows 100% per-model speed win rate.
AI.conclusion: Intelligent layer selection can make LoRA fine-tuning materially more efficient without introducing major downstream damage, supporting a practical model-economics claim for more efficient parameter-efficient fine-tuning.

title: Optimization of Sparse VLSF Codes for Short-Packet Transmission via Saddlepoint Methods
comment: Accepted in IEEE International Conference in Communications (ICC) 2026 Glasgow, Scotland, UK
summary: In this work, we present an optimization framework for sparse variable-length stop-feedback (VLSF) codes based on a saddlepoint approximation, which jointly optimizes the decoding configuration parameters. Thanks to the analytical tractability of the saddlepoint approximation, the framework enables efficient gradient-based optimization of such parameters for common memoryless channels, including the additive white Gaussian noise, binary symmetric, and binary erasure channels. We further propose a refined decoding rule that extends the conventional fixed-threshold rule and leads to a tighter achievability bound. Numerical results demonstrate that our framework provides near-optimal decoding configurations at low computational cost. Moreover, the results from our refined rule demonstrate that the fixed-threshold decoding rule is restrictive and that achievability bounds can be further tightened.

AI.tldr: Optimization framework for sparse variable-length stop-feedback codes using saddlepoint approximation for efficient gradient-based optimization of decoding parameters across common channels.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient optimization framework for sparse VLSF codes that can jointly optimize decoding configuration parameters with low computational cost, addressing limitations of conventional fixed-threshold decoding rules.
AI.method: Uses saddlepoint approximation for analytical tractability, enabling gradient-based optimization of decoding parameters for memoryless channels (AWGN, BSC, BEC). Proposes a refined decoding rule that extends conventional fixed-threshold rule.
AI.result: Framework provides near-optimal decoding configurations at low computational cost. Refined rule shows that fixed-threshold decoding is restrictive and achievability bounds can be further tightened.
AI.conclusion: The saddlepoint approximation-based optimization framework enables efficient parameter optimization for sparse VLSF codes, and the refined decoding rule demonstrates that conventional fixed-threshold approaches are suboptimal, allowing for tighter achievability bounds.

title: CTSCAN: Evaluation Leakage in Chest CT Segmentation and a Reproducible Patient-Disjoint Benchmark
summary: Reported chest CT segmentation performance can be strongly inflated when train and test partitions mix slices from the same study. We present CTSCAN, a reproducible multi-source chest CT benchmark and research stack designed to measure what survives under patient-disjoint evaluation. The current four-class artifact aggregates 89 cases from PleThora, MedSeg SIRM, and LongCIU, and we show that the original slice-PNG workflow induces near-complete case reuse across train, validation, and test. Using the playground environment, we run a multi-seed protocol sweep with the same FPN plus EfficientNet-B0 control configuration under slice-mixed and case-disjoint evaluation. Across 3 seeds and 12 epochs per seed, the slice-mixed protocol reaches 0.6665 foreground Dice and 0.5031 foreground IoU, whereas the case-disjoint protocol reaches 0.2066 Dice and 0.1181 IoU. Removing patient reuse therefore reduces foreground Dice by 0.4599 absolute (69.00% relative) and foreground IoU by 0.3850 absolute (76.52% relative). CTSCAN packages the corrected benchmark with deterministic split manifests, explicit weak-supervision controls, a scripted multi-seed protocol sweep, and reproducible figure generation, providing a reusable basis for patient-disjoint chest CT evaluation.

AI.tldr: CTSCAN is a reproducible chest CT benchmark that reveals severe performance inflation when train/test sets share patient data, showing 69% Dice drop under proper patient-disjoint evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing chest CT segmentation benchmarks suffer from data leakage where train and test partitions mix slices from the same patient studies, artificially inflating reported performance metrics. This creates misleading results that don't reflect real-world generalization.
AI.method: Created CTSCAN benchmark with 89 cases from three datasets (PleThora, MedSeg SIRM, LongCIU) using patient-disjoint splits. Implemented deterministic split manifests, weak-supervision controls, and multi-seed protocol sweeps with FPN+EfficientNet-B0 architecture to compare slice-mixed vs case-disjoint evaluation.
AI.result: Slice-mixed evaluation achieved 0.6665 Dice and 0.5031 IoU, while patient-disjoint evaluation dropped to 0.2066 Dice and 0.1181 IoU - a 69% relative Dice reduction and 76.5% IoU reduction, revealing severe performance inflation from patient data reuse.
AI.conclusion: Patient data leakage dramatically inflates chest CT segmentation performance. CTSCAN provides a reproducible benchmark for proper patient-disjoint evaluation, essential for realistic assessment of model generalization in medical imaging.

title: One-Shot Cross-Geometry Skill Transfer through Part Decomposition
summary: Given a demonstration, a robot should be able to generalize a skill to any object it encounters-but existing approaches to skill transfer often fail to adapt to objects with unfamiliar shapes. Motivated by examples of improved transfer from compositional modeling, we propose a method for improving transfer by decomposing objects into their constituent semantic parts. We leverage data-efficient generative shape models to accurately transfer interaction points from the parts of a demonstration object to a novel object. We autonomously construct an objective to optimize the alignment of those points on skill-relevant object parts. Our method generalizes to a wider range of object geometries than existing work, and achieves successful one-shot transfer for a range of skills and objects from a single demonstration, in both simulated and real environments.

AI.tldr: A method for improving robot skill transfer by decomposing objects into semantic parts and using generative shape models to transfer interaction points from demonstration objects to novel objects.
AI.motivation: Existing skill transfer approaches often fail to adapt to objects with unfamiliar shapes, limiting robot generalization capabilities.
AI.method: Decompose objects into semantic parts, use data-efficient generative shape models to transfer interaction points, and autonomously construct an objective to optimize alignment of skill-relevant parts.
AI.result: Generalizes to wider range of object geometries than existing work, achieves successful one-shot transfer for various skills and objects from single demonstrations in both simulated and real environments.
AI.conclusion: Compositional modeling through semantic part decomposition significantly improves robot skill transfer to novel objects with different shapes.

title: Bureaucratic Silences: What the Canadian AI Register Reveals, Omits, and Obscures
summary: In November 2025, the Government of Canada operationalized its commitment to transparency by releasing its first Federal AI Register. In this paper, we argue that such registers are not neutral mirrors of government activity, but active instruments of ontological design that configure the boundaries of accountability. We analyzed the Register's complete dataset of 409 systems using the Algorithmic Decision-Making Adapted for the Public Sector (ADMAPS) framework, combining quantitative mapping with deductive qualitative coding. Our findings reveal a sharp divergence between the rhetoric of "sovereign AI" and the reality of bureaucratic practice: while 86\% of systems are deployed internally for efficiency, the Register systematically obscures the human discretion, training, and uncertainty management required to operate them. By privileging technical descriptions over sociotechnical context, the Register constructs an ontology of AI as "reliable tooling" rather than "contestable decision-making." We conclude that without a shift in design, such transparency artifacts risk automating accountability into a performative compliance exercise, offering visibility without contestability.

AI.tldr: Canada's Federal AI Register reveals transparency tools can obscure rather than illuminate accountability by framing AI as reliable tools rather than contestable decision-making systems.
AI.motivation: To critically examine how government AI transparency registers like Canada's Federal AI Register function not as neutral mirrors but as active instruments that shape accountability boundaries and ontological understanding of AI systems.
AI.method: Analyzed complete dataset of 409 systems using ADMAPS framework (Algorithmic Decision-Making Adapted for Public Sector), combining quantitative mapping with deductive qualitative coding to examine how the Register represents AI systems.
AI.result: Found sharp divergence between "sovereign AI" rhetoric and bureaucratic reality: 86% of systems deployed internally for efficiency, while Register systematically obscures human discretion, training, and uncertainty management. Register constructs AI ontology as "reliable tooling" rather than "contestable decision-making" by privileging technical over sociotechnical context.
AI.conclusion: Without design shifts, transparency artifacts like AI registers risk automating accountability into performative compliance exercises, offering visibility without contestability, potentially undermining meaningful democratic oversight.

title: (1D) Ordered Tokens Enable Efficient Test-Time Search
summary:
Tokenization is a key component of autoregressive (AR) generative models, converting raw data into more manageable units for modeling. Commonly, tokens describe local information, such as regions of pixels in images or word pieces in text, and AR generation predicts these tokens in a fixed order. A worthwhile question is whether token structures affect the ability to steer the generation through test-time search, where multiple candidate generations are explored and evaluated by a verifier. Using image generation as our testbed, we hypothesize that recent 1D ordered tokenizers with coarse-to-fine structure can be more amenable to search than classical 2D grid structures. This is rooted in the fact that the intermediate states in coarse-to-fine sequences carry semantic meaning that verifiers can reliably evaluate, enabling effective steering during generation.
  Through controlled experiments, we find that AR models trained on coarse-to-fine ordered tokens exhibit improved test-time scaling behavior compared to grid-based counterparts. Moreover, we demonstrate that, thanks to the ordered structure, pure test-time search over token sequences (i.e., without training an AR model) can perform training-free text-to-image generation when guided by an image-text verifier. Beyond this, we systematically study how classical search algorithms (best-of-N, beam search, lookahead search) interact with different token structures, as well as the role of different verifiers and AR priors. Our results highlight the impact of token structure on inference-time scalability and provide practical guidance for test-time scaling in AR models.

AI.tldr: Token structure affects test-time search in autoregressive models; coarse-to-fine 1D tokenizers enable better steering than 2D grids.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether token structures affect the ability to steer generation through test-time search, where multiple candidate generations are explored and evaluated by a verifier.
AI.method: Using image generation as testbed, comparing coarse-to-fine 1D ordered tokenizers vs classical 2D grid structures. Studying how search algorithms (best-of-N, beam search, lookahead search) interact with different token structures, verifiers, and AR priors.
AI.result: AR models trained on coarse-to-fine ordered tokens show improved test-time scaling behavior. Pure test-time search over token sequences can perform training-free text-to-image generation when guided by an image-text verifier.
AI.conclusion: Token structure significantly impacts inference-time scalability; coarse-to-fine ordered tokenizers are more amenable to test-time search, providing practical guidance for test-time scaling in AR models.

title: Discovery of unobservable parameters via physical embedding
summary: Recovering a source signal from indirect measurements often requires estimating latent parameters, such as wireless channel states or MRI coil sensitivities, that cannot be directly observed. Here, we introduce Physics-Embedded Inverse Learning (PEIL), in which a learned estimator predicts these parameters and a fixed, physics-based inverse operator uses them to reconstruct the signal, so that training requires only the source signal as supervision. In systems where multiple parameter combinations can reconstruct the signal equally well, the estimator exploits this freedom to coordinate parameters that compensate for residual modelling errors rather than match ground-truth parameters. In high-mobility wireless communications, PEIL discovers task-optimal configurations that outperform baselines given access to ground-truth parameters, enabling zero-shot generalisation and over 20-fold reduction in training data relative to supervised baselines. To test whether these properties extend across physical domains, we apply PEIL to parallel MRI, where it discovers physically interpretable coil sensitivity maps without calibration scans, yielding reconstructions grounded purely in acquired measurements. These results demonstrate that non-identifiability, conventionally a liability, becomes a resource when the learning objective targets reconstruction quality rather than parameter accuracy.

AI.tldr: PEIL is a physics-embedded inverse learning framework that learns to estimate latent parameters for signal reconstruction using only source signal supervision, exploiting non-identifiability to compensate for modeling errors rather than matching ground-truth parameters.
AI.motivation: Recovering source signals from indirect measurements requires estimating unobservable latent parameters (like wireless channel states or MRI coil sensitivities). Traditional approaches need ground-truth parameter supervision, which is often unavailable or expensive to obtain.
AI.method: PEIL combines a learned estimator that predicts latent parameters with a fixed physics-based inverse operator that uses these parameters to reconstruct the source signal. Training requires only source signal supervision, not ground-truth parameters. The method exploits non-identifiability - when multiple parameter combinations can reconstruct signals equally well - to coordinate parameters that compensate for modeling errors.
AI.result: In high-mobility wireless communications: PEIL discovers task-optimal configurations outperforming baselines with ground-truth parameters, enables zero-shot generalization, and reduces training data by over 20x. In parallel MRI: PEIL discovers physically interpretable coil sensitivity maps without calibration scans, yielding reconstructions based purely on acquired measurements.
AI.conclusion: Non-identifiability, traditionally considered a liability, becomes a resource when the learning objective targets reconstruction quality rather than parameter accuracy. PEIL demonstrates this principle across physical domains (wireless communications and MRI) with practical benefits including reduced supervision requirements and improved performance.

title: Sequential KV Cache Compression via Probabilistic Language Tries: Beyond the Per-Vector Shannon Limit
summary: Recent work on KV cache quantization, culminating in TurboQuant, has approached the Shannon entropy limit for per-vector compression of transformer key-value caches. We observe that this limit applies to a strictly weaker problem than the one that actually matters: compressing the KV cache as a sequence. The tokens stored in a KV cache are not arbitrary floating-point data -- they are samples from the exact formal language the model was trained on, and the model is by construction a near-optimal predictor of that language. We introduce sequential KV compression, a two-layer architecture that exploits this structure. The first layer, probabilistic prefix deduplication, identifies semantically equivalent shared prefixes across sessions using the trie metric d_T(s, s') = -log_2 P_M(s ^ s') from Probabilistic Language Tries (PLTs). The second layer, predictive delta coding, stores only the residual of each new KV vector from the model's own prediction of it, achieving a per-token entropy bound of H(KV_{i+1} | KV_{<=i}) <= H(token_{i+1} | token_{<=i}). We prove that at typical language model perplexity -- approximately 10-20 for fluent English text -- this bound is 3.3-4.3 bits on average per token position, compared to TurboQuant's 3 bits per vector component (with typical attention heads having 64-128 components). The theoretical compression ratio over TurboQuant is approximately 914,000x at the Shannon limit. Even at 1000x above the entropy floor -- a deliberately pessimistic worst-case overhead, two orders of magnitude above the 2-5x typical of practical source coders -- the ratio remains approximately 914x over TurboQuant, with compression improving rather than degrading as context length grows. The two layers are orthogonal and compose with existing per-vector quantization methods including TurboQuant.

AI.tldr: Sequential KV compression beats per-vector quantization by exploiting language structure, achieving ~914,000x better compression than TurboQuant at Shannon limit.
AI.motivation: Current KV cache quantization (like TurboQuant) approaches Shannon entropy limit for per-vector compression, but this ignores the sequential nature of KV caches. Tokens in KV caches are samples from the model's training language, and the model itself is a near-optimal predictor of that language, creating opportunities for better compression.
AI.method: Two-layer architecture: 1) Probabilistic prefix deduplication using trie metric d_T(s, s') = -log_2 P_M(s ^ s') from Probabilistic Language Tries to identify semantically equivalent shared prefixes across sessions. 2) Predictive delta coding that stores only the residual of each new KV vector from the model's own prediction, achieving per-token entropy bound of H(KV_{i+1} | KV_{<=i}) <= H(token_{i+1} | token_{<=i}).
AI.result: At typical LM perplexity (10-20 for fluent English), bound is 3.3-4.3 bits per token position vs TurboQuant's 3 bits per vector component (with 64-128 components per head). Theoretical compression ratio over TurboQuant is ~914,000x at Shannon limit. Even at 1000x above entropy floor (pessimistic worst-case), ratio remains ~914x over TurboQuant. Compression improves with context length growth.
AI.conclusion: Sequential KV compression fundamentally outperforms per-vector quantization by exploiting language structure and model's predictive capabilities. The two-layer approach is orthogonal to and composable with existing quantization methods, offering massive compression improvements that scale with context length.

title: VLSF Decoding with Reliability Guarantees over Correlated Noncoherent Fading Channels
comment: Accepted in IEEE International Symposium on Information Theory (ISIT) 2026 Guangzhou, China
summary: This paper studies reliability-guaranteed decoding for variable-length stop-feedback (VLSF) codes over correlated noncoherent fading channels. The decoding rule is based on the evolution of the information density associated with a given channel input-output realization. Due to channel memory, exact evaluation of this information density is intractable. To enable constructive decoding, computable finite-blocklength lower and upper bounds on the information density that hold uniformly over time along each input-output sequence are derived. The lower bound enables a stopping-time analysis for VLSF decoding and has an operational meaning, while the upper bound provides a reference for the relaxation gap, which is explicitly characterized. As a concrete application, the Gauss-Markov fading channel with Gaussian signaling is considered to numerically investigate the stopping-time distribution and the impact of fading correlation on decoding performance.

AI.tldr: VLSF codes with reliability-guaranteed decoding for correlated noncoherent fading channels using information density bounds and Gauss-Markov fading analysis.
AI.motivation: Need reliable decoding for variable-length stop-feedback codes over correlated fading channels where exact information density evaluation is intractable due to channel memory.
AI.method: Derive computable finite-blocklength lower and upper bounds on information density that hold uniformly over time, enabling constructive decoding and stopping-time analysis.
AI.result: Developed operational lower bound for stopping-time analysis and upper bound characterizing relaxation gap, with numerical investigation of Gauss-Markov fading showing impact of correlation on decoding performance.
AI.conclusion: Proposed bounds enable practical VLSF decoding for correlated fading channels with explicit characterization of performance gaps, validated through Gauss-Markov fading analysis.

title: Topology-Driven Fusion of nnU-Net and MedNeXt for Accurate Brain Tumor Segmentation on Sub-Saharan Africa Dataset
summary: Accurate automatic brain tumor segmentation in Low and Middle-Income (LMIC) countries is challenging due to the lack of defined national imaging protocols, diverse imaging data, extensive use of low-field Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) scanners and limited health-care resources. As part of the Brain Tumor Segmentation (BraTS) Africa 2025 Challenge, we applied topology refinement to the state-of-the-art segmentation models like nnU-Net, MedNeXt, and a combination of both. Since the BraTS-Africa dataset has low MRI image quality, we incorporated the BraTS 2025 challenge data of pre-treatment adult glioma (Task 1) to pre-train the segmentation model and use it to fine-tune on the BraTS-Africa dataset. We added an extra topology refinement module to address the issue of deformation in prediction that arose due to topological error. With the introduction of this module, we achieved a better Normalized Surface Distance (NSD) of 0.810, 0.829, and 0.895 on Surrounding Non-Enhancing FLAIR Hyperintensity (SNFH) , Non-Enhancing Tumor Core (NETC) and Enhancing tumor (ET).

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a topology refinement approach for brain tumor segmentation in LMIC settings, combining nnU-Net and MedNeXt models with pre-training on BraTS 2025 data and fine-tuning on BraTS-Africa dataset to handle low-quality MRI images.
AI.motivation: Brain tumor segmentation in LMIC countries faces challenges due to diverse imaging protocols, low-field MRI scanners, limited healthcare resources, and poor image quality, requiring specialized approaches for accurate segmentation.
AI.method: Used state-of-the-art segmentation models (nnU-Net, MedNeXt, and their combination) with pre-training on BraTS 2025 challenge data and fine-tuning on BraTS-Africa dataset. Added a topology refinement module to address topological errors and deformation in predictions.
AI.result: Achieved improved Normalized Surface Distance (NSD) scores: 0.810 for Surrounding Non-Enhancing FLAIR Hyperintensity (SNFH), 0.829 for Non-Enhancing Tumor Core (NETC), and 0.895 for Enhancing Tumor (ET) with the topology refinement module.
AI.conclusion: Topology refinement combined with pre-training and fine-tuning strategies effectively addresses brain tumor segmentation challenges in LMIC settings with low-quality MRI data, improving segmentation accuracy for different tumor sub-regions.

title: NeuroMesh: A Unified Neural Inference Framework for Decentralized Multi-Robot Collaboration
comment: 8 page, 8 figures, Accepted at the IEEE Robotics Automation Letter (RA-L)
summary: Deploying learned multi-robot models on heterogeneous robots remains challenging due to hardware heterogeneity, communication constraints, and the lack of a unified execution stack. This paper presents NeuroMesh, a multi-domain, cross-platform, and modular decentralized neural inference framework that standardizes observation encoding, message passing, aggregation, and task decoding in a unified pipeline. NeuroMesh combines a dual-aggregation paradigm for reduction- and broadcast-based information fusion with a parallelized architecture that decouples cycle time from end-to-end latency. Our high-performance C++ implementation leverages Zenoh for inter-robot communication and supports hybrid GPU/CPU inference. We validate NeuroMesh on a heterogeneous team of aerial and ground robots across collaborative perception, decentralized control, and task assignment, demonstrating robust operation across diverse task structures and payload sizes. We plan to release NeuroMesh as an open-source framework to the community.

AI.tldr: NeuroMesh is a decentralized neural inference framework for heterogeneous multi-robot systems that standardizes neural network execution across different hardware platforms while maintaining low latency and supporting diverse communication patterns.
AI.motivation: Current multi-robot systems face challenges with hardware heterogeneity, communication constraints, and lack of unified execution stacks, making it difficult to deploy learned models across different robot platforms.
AI.method: NeuroMesh uses a dual-aggregation paradigm (reduction- and broadcast-based information fusion) with parallelized architecture that decouples cycle time from latency. It's implemented in high-performance C++ with Zenoh for communication and supports hybrid GPU/CPU inference.
AI.result: Validated on heterogeneous aerial and ground robots across collaborative perception, decentralized control, and task assignment, demonstrating robust operation across diverse task structures and payload sizes.
AI.conclusion: NeuroMesh provides a practical solution for deploying neural models on heterogeneous multi-robot systems and will be released as open-source to benefit the robotics community.

title: LACE: Lattice Attention for Cross-thread Exploration
summary: Current large language models reason in isolation. Although it is common to sample multiple reasoning paths in parallel, these trajectories do not interact, and often fail in the same redundant ways. We introduce LACE, a framework that transforms reasoning from a collection of independent trials into a coordinated, parallel process. By repurposing the model architecture to enable cross-thread attention, LACE allows concurrent reasoning paths to share intermediate insights and correct one another during inference. A central challenge is the absence of natural training data that exhibits such collaborative behavior. We address this gap with a synthetic data pipeline that explicitly teaches models to communicate and error-correct across threads. Experiments show that this unified exploration substantially outperforms standard parallel search, improving reasoning accuracy by over 7 points. Our results suggest that large language models can be more effective when parallel reasoning paths are allowed to interact.

AI.tldr: LACE enables parallel reasoning paths in LLMs to interact via cross-thread attention, improving accuracy by 7+ points through collaborative error correction.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs reason in isolation with parallel paths that don't interact, causing redundant failures. The authors want to transform reasoning from independent trials into a coordinated, parallel process where paths can share insights and correct each other.
AI.method: LACE framework repurposes model architecture to enable cross-thread attention, allowing concurrent reasoning paths to interact. Uses synthetic data pipeline to teach models to communicate and error-correct across threads since natural training data lacks such collaborative behavior.
AI.result: Experiments show unified exploration substantially outperforms standard parallel search, improving reasoning accuracy by over 7 points.
AI.conclusion: Large language models can be more effective when parallel reasoning paths are allowed to interact, suggesting collaborative reasoning as a promising direction.

title: Frequency-Aware Flow Matching for High-Quality Image Generation
summary: Flow matching models have emerged as a powerful framework for realistic image generation by learning to reverse a corruption process that progressively adds Gaussian noise. However, because noise is injected in the latent domain, its impact on different frequency components is non-uniform. As a result, during inference, flow matching models tend to generate low-frequency components (global structure) in the early stages, while high-frequency components (fine details) emerge only later in the reverse process. Building on this insight, we propose Frequency-Aware Flow Matching (FreqFlow), a novel approach that explicitly incorporates frequency-aware conditioning into the flow matching framework via time-dependent adaptive weighting. We introduce a two-branch architecture: (1) a frequency branch that separately processes low- and high-frequency components to capture global structure and refine textures and edges, and (2) a spatial branch that synthesizes images in the latent domain, guided by the frequency branch's output. By explicitly integrating frequency information into the generation process, FreqFlow ensures that both large-scale coherence and fine-grained details are effectively modeled low-frequency conditioning reinforces global structure, while high-frequency conditioning enhances texture fidelity and detail sharpness.
