AI.method: Proposes SIMMER using a single MLLM encoder (VLM2Vec) that processes both images and texts. Designs prompt templates for structured recipes (title, ingredients, instructions). Introduces component-aware data augmentation training on both complete and partial recipes for robustness.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art on Recipe1M dataset: improves 1k image-to-recipe R@1 from 81.8% to 87.5% and 10k image-to-recipe R@1 from 56.5% to 65.5% compared to previous best method.
AI.conclusion: SIMMER demonstrates that unified MLLM-based encoders with proper prompting and data augmentation can outperform complex dual-encoder architectures for cross-modal food retrieval, offering a simpler yet more effective paradigm.

title: Transmitter Noise Propagation in Millimeter-Wave and Sub-Terahertz: From Limits to Design Guidelines
comment: 13 pages, 10 figures, 7 tables. Submitted to IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society (OJCOMS)
summary: This paper presents a comprehensive link budget analysis for millimeter wave (mm-Wave) and sub-Terahertz (sub-THz) communication systems with primary focus on transmitter (TX) noise propagation, an often overlooked impairment that can dominate in scenarios where path loss is insufficient to suppress TX noise below receiver thermal and atmospheric molecular noise levels. Unlike traditional thermal noise limited analyses, this work demonstrates that TX noise is amplified by component noise figures that degrade significantly with frequency, rising from single digits to more than $15\,\mathrm{dB}$ in the sub-THz range. In the scenarios analyzed, this propagated TX noise reduces the achievable Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) by approximately $15$ to $25\,\mathrm{dB}$ at short distances, creating fundamental SNR ceilings at ranges below about $10\,\mathrm{cm}$. We develop a systematic framework quantifying TX noise dominance conditions as functions of distance, frequency, and component parameters, revealing fundamental performance constraints for short-range next generation wireless systems. Our findings indicate that the TX noise figure should be as low as possible for short-range communication, and both TX noise and atmospheric molecular noise should be considered for medium- and long-range links.

AI.tldr: TX noise propagation in mm-Wave/sub-THz systems creates SNR ceilings at short ranges (<10cm), reducing SNR by 15-25dB due to amplified component noise figures that degrade with frequency.
AI.motivation: Traditional link budget analyses overlook transmitter noise propagation, which becomes dominant in mm-Wave/sub-THz systems when path loss is insufficient to suppress TX noise below receiver thermal and atmospheric noise levels.
AI.method: Developed a systematic framework to quantify TX noise dominance conditions as functions of distance, frequency, and component parameters, with comprehensive link budget analysis focusing on TX noise propagation.
AI.result: TX noise figures degrade significantly with frequency (rising to >15dB in sub-THz range), reducing achievable SNR by 15-25dB at short distances and creating fundamental SNR ceilings below ~10cm ranges.
AI.conclusion: TX noise figure should be minimized for short-range communication, and both TX noise and atmospheric molecular noise must be considered for medium- and long-range links in next-generation wireless systems.

title: Dispatch-Aware Ragged Attention for Pruned Vision Transformers
summary: Token pruning methods for Vision Transformers (ViTs) promise quadratic reductions in attention FLOPs by dropping uninformative patches. Yet when pruned sequences are executed with state-of-the-art variable-length attention APIs -- including FlashAttention-2's varlen and PyTorch's NestedTensor SDPA-the wall-clock attention latency doesn't scale accordingly. We trace this to a dispatch-overhead bottleneck: at the short, post-pruning sequence lengths typical of ViTs (<=197 tokens), actual matrix arithmetic completes in single-digit microseconds while the host-side dispatch path consumes 60-90 us. We present a lightweight, bidirectional Triton attention kernel whose dispatch floor is 40 us roughly 1.5x lower than FlashAttention-2 varlen-allowing pruning savings to become more visible in wall-clock time. Integrated into a complete pack-attend-unpack pipeline, our system achieves up to 2.24x end-to-end throughput over padded PyTorch SDPA consistently across four pruning algorithms (Threshold-L2, DynamicViT, EViT, ATS), scales across DeiT-T/S/B, and maintains bit-exact classification predictions with <0.007 max absolute logit difference.

AI.tldr: A lightweight Triton attention kernel reduces dispatch overhead for pruned Vision Transformers, achieving up to 2.24x throughput over padded PyTorch SDPA while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Token pruning methods for Vision Transformers promise computational savings, but current variable-length attention APIs suffer from high dispatch overhead that prevents these savings from translating to actual wall-clock speedups, especially at the short sequence lengths typical of pruned ViTs.
AI.method: Developed a lightweight, bidirectional Triton attention kernel with reduced dispatch overhead (40μs vs 60-90μs for FlashAttention-2 varlen). Integrated into a complete pack-attend-unpack pipeline to efficiently handle pruned sequences.
AI.result: Achieves up to 2.24x end-to-end throughput over padded PyTorch SDPA across four pruning algorithms (Threshold-L2, DynamicViT, EViT, ATS), scales across DeiT-T/S/B models, and maintains bit-exact classification predictions with <0.007 max absolute logit difference.
AI.conclusion: The proposed lightweight attention kernel successfully addresses the dispatch-overhead bottleneck in pruned Vision Transformers, making token pruning savings more visible in actual wall-clock time while preserving model accuracy.

title: Long-Term Memory for VLA-based Agents in Open-World Task Execution
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated significant potential for embodied decision-making; however, their application in complex chemical laboratory automation remains restricted by limited long-horizon reasoning and the absence of persistent experience accumulation. Existing frameworks typically treat planning and execution as decoupled processes, often failing to consolidate successful strategies, which results in inefficient trial-and-error in multi-stage protocols. In this paper, we propose ChemBot, a dual-layer, closed-loop framework that integrates an autonomous AI agent with a progress-aware VLA model (Skill-VLA) for hierarchical task decomposition and execution. ChemBot utilizes a dual-layer memory architecture to consolidate successful trajectories into retrievable assets, while a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server facilitates efficient sub-agent and tool orchestration. To address the inherent limitations of VLA models, we further implement a future-state-based asynchronous inference mechanism to mitigate trajectory discontinuities. Extensive experiments on collaborative robots demonstrate that ChemBot achieves superior operational safety, precision, and task success rates compared to existing VLA baselines in complex, long-horizon chemical experimentation.

AI.tldr: ChemBot: A dual-layer closed-loop framework integrating AI agent with progress-aware VLA model for hierarchical chemical lab automation, featuring memory architecture for experience accumulation and asynchronous inference for trajectory continuity.
AI.motivation: Current Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have limited long-horizon reasoning and lack persistent experience accumulation for complex chemical lab automation. Existing frameworks treat planning and execution separately without consolidating successful strategies, leading to inefficient trial-and-error in multi-stage protocols.
AI.method: Proposes ChemBot with dual-layer closed-loop framework: autonomous AI agent integrated with progress-aware VLA model (Skill-VLA) for hierarchical task decomposition. Uses dual-layer memory architecture to consolidate successful trajectories into retrievable assets. Implements Model Context Protocol (MCP) server for efficient sub-agent and tool orchestration. Adds future-state-based asynchronous inference mechanism to mitigate VLA trajectory discontinuities.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on collaborative robots show ChemBot achieves superior operational safety, precision, and task success rates compared to existing VLA baselines in complex, long-horizon chemical experimentation.
AI.conclusion: ChemBot effectively addresses limitations of current VLA models for chemical lab automation by combining hierarchical task decomposition, experience accumulation through memory architecture, and trajectory continuity mechanisms, demonstrating practical viability for complex experimental protocols.

title: Structured Abductive-Deductive-Inductive Reasoning for LLMs via Algebraic Invariants
comment: 10 pages + 3 pages references. Accepted as a poster at the ICLR 2026 Workshop for LLM Reasoning
summary: Large language models exhibit systematic limitations in structured logical reasoning: they conflate hypothesis generation with verification, cannot distinguish conjecture from validated knowledge, and allow weak reasoning steps to propagate unchecked through inference chains. We present a symbolic reasoning scaffold that operationalizes Peirce's tripartite inference -- abduction, deduction, and induction -- as an explicit protocol for LLM-assisted reasoning. The framework enforces logical consistency through five algebraic invariants (the Gamma Quintet), the strongest of which -- the Weakest Link bound -- ensures that no conclusion in a reasoning chain can exceed the reliability of its least-supported premise. This principle, independently grounded as weakest link resolution in possibilistic logic and empirically validated for chain-of-thought reasoning, prevents logical inconsistencies from accumulating across multi-step inference. We verify all invariants through a property-based testing suite of 100 properties and 16 fuzz tests over 10^5+ generated cases, providing a verified reference implementation of the invariants suitable as a foundation for future reasoning benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A symbolic reasoning scaffold operationalizing Peirce's tripartite inference (abduction, deduction, induction) with algebraic invariants to enforce logical consistency in LLM reasoning, preventing error propagation through weakest link bounds.
AI.motivation: Large language models have systematic limitations in structured logical reasoning: they conflate hypothesis generation with verification, cannot distinguish conjecture from validated knowledge, and allow weak reasoning steps to propagate unchecked through inference chains.
AI.method: Presents a symbolic reasoning scaffold that operationalizes Peirce's tripartite inference (abduction, deduction, induction) as an explicit protocol for LLM-assisted reasoning. The framework enforces logical consistency through five algebraic invariants (the Gamma Quintet), with the strongest being the Weakest Link bound that ensures no conclusion can exceed the reliability of its least-supported premise.
AI.result: Verified all invariants through a property-based testing suite of 100 properties and 16 fuzz tests over 10^5+ generated cases, providing a verified reference implementation of the invariants suitable as a foundation for future reasoning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a principled approach to address LLM reasoning limitations by enforcing logical consistency through algebraic invariants, preventing error accumulation in multi-step inference chains.

title: Causal Bootstrapped Alignment for Unsupervised Video-Based Visible-Infrared Person Re-Identification
summary: VVI-ReID is a critical technique for all-day surveillance, where temporal information provides additional cues beyond static images. However, existing approaches rely heavily on fully supervised learning with expensive cross-modality annotations, limiting scalability. To address this issue, we investigate Unsupervised Learning for VVI-ReID (USL-VVI-ReID), which learns identity-discriminative representations directly from unlabeled video tracklets. Directly extending image-based USL-VI-ReID methods to this setting with generic pretrained encoders leads to suboptimal performance. Such encoders suffer from weak identity discrimination and strong modality bias, resulting in severe intra-modality identity confusion and pronounced clustering granularity imbalance between visible and infrared modalities. These issues jointly degrade pseudo-label reliability and hinder effective cross-modality alignment. To address these challenges, we propose a Causal Bootstrapped Alignment (CBA) framework that explicitly exploits inherent video priors. First, we introduce Causal Intervention Warm-up (CIW), which performs sequence-level causal interventions by leveraging temporal identity consistency and cross-modality identity consistency to suppress modality- and motion-induced spurious correlations while preserving identity-relevant semantics, yielding cleaner representations for unsupervised clustering. Second, we propose Prototype-Guided Uncertainty Refinement (PGUR), which employs a coarse-to-fine alignment strategy to resolve cross-modality granularity mismatch, reorganizing under-clustered infrared representations under the guidance of reliable visible prototypes with uncertainty-aware supervision. Extensive experiments on the HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus benchmarks demonstrate that CBA significantly outperforms existing USL-VI-ReID methods when extended to the USL-VVI-ReID setting.

AI.tldr: CBA framework for unsupervised visible-infrared video person re-identification addresses modality bias and clustering granularity imbalance using causal intervention warm-up and prototype-guided uncertainty refinement.
AI.motivation: Existing VVI-ReID methods rely on expensive cross-modality annotations, limiting scalability. Unsupervised learning approaches suffer from weak identity discrimination, modality bias, intra-modality identity confusion, and cross-modality clustering granularity imbalance when using generic pretrained encoders.
AI.method: Proposes Causal Bootstrapped Alignment (CBA) framework: 1) Causal Intervention Warm-up (CIW) performs sequence-level causal interventions using temporal and cross-modality identity consistency to suppress spurious correlations; 2) Prototype-Guided Uncertainty Refinement (PGUR) uses coarse-to-fine alignment with uncertainty-aware supervision to resolve cross-modality granularity mismatch.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on HITSZ-VCM and BUPTCampus benchmarks show CBA significantly outperforms existing USL-VI-ReID methods when extended to the USL-VVI-ReID setting.
AI.conclusion: CBA effectively addresses challenges in unsupervised visible-infrared video person re-identification by exploiting video priors through causal intervention and prototype-guided refinement, achieving superior performance without expensive annotations.

title: The Illusion of Equivalence: Systematic FP16 Divergence in KV-Cached Autoregressive Inference
summary: KV caching is a ubiquitous optimization in autoregressive transformer inference, long presumed to be numerically equivalent to cache-free computation. This assumption fails under standard FP16 precision: cache-ON and cache-OFF execution paths employ different floating-point accumulation orderings which, due to FP16 non-associativity, produce a deterministic divergence in decoded token sequences. Across three open-weight models (LLaMA-2-7B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Gemma-2-2B) evaluated on GSM8K, we observe a 100\% token divergence rate across all sampling strategies, including greedy decoding, which rules out sampling randomness as a cause, and also with cache-ON yielding higher accuracy in 8 of 9 conditions, where the accuracy difference serves as an indicator that the divergence direction is systematic rather than random. Controlled FP32 falsification reduces divergence by eight orders of magnitude, eliminates token flips, and drops the flip rate to exactly 0.0\%, confirming FP16 non-associativity as the sole causal driver. Layer-wise drift profiling reveals architecturally predictable propagation patterns: models using Grouped-Query Attention exhibit sharp divergence at the first layer, while Gemma's larger head dimension and sliding window attention produce uniform accumulation across all layers. Finally, activation patching of the entire residual stream fails to recover the cache-free trajectory, localizing the causal variable to the stateful KV cache. These findings establish that FP16 KV cache inference is fundamentally non-equivalent to recomputation and provide a mechanistic framework for understanding numerical instability in modern LLM inference systems.

AI.tldr: FP16 KV caching in transformers causes deterministic token divergence due to floating-point non-associativity, making cache-ON inference fundamentally different from cache-free computation.
AI.motivation: To investigate the assumption that KV caching is numerically equivalent to cache-free computation in transformer inference, which fails under FP16 precision due to non-associativity of floating-point operations.
AI.method: Evaluated three open-weight models (LLaMA-2-7B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Gemma-2-2B) on GSM8K with cache-ON vs cache-OFF execution paths. Used FP32 falsification to isolate FP16 effects, layer-wise drift profiling to analyze propagation patterns, and activation patching to localize causal variables.
AI.result: 100% token divergence rate across all sampling strategies, with cache-ON yielding higher accuracy in 8 of 9 conditions. FP32 eliminated divergence, confirming FP16 non-associativity as the sole cause. Different architectures showed distinct divergence patterns: Grouped-Query Attention models diverged sharply at first layer, while Gemma showed uniform accumulation.
AI.conclusion: FP16 KV cache inference is fundamentally non-equivalent to recomputation due to floating-point non-associativity, providing a mechanistic framework for understanding numerical instability in modern LLM inference systems.

title: Fuzzy Logic Theory-based Adaptive Reward Shaping for Robust Reinforcement Learning (FARS)
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) often struggles in real-world tasks with high-dimensional state spaces and long horizons, where sparse or fixed rewards severely slow down exploration and cause agents to get trapped in local optima. This paper presents a fuzzy logic based reward shaping method that integrates human intuition into RL reward design. By encoding expert knowledge into adaptive and interpreable terms, fuzzy rules promote stable learning and reduce sensitivity to hyperparameters. The proposed method leverages these properties to adapt reward contributions based on the agent state, enabling smoother transitions between fast motion and precise control in challenging navigation tasks. Extensive simulation results on autonomous drone racing benchmarks show stable learning behavior and consistent task performance across scenarios of increasing difficulty. The proposed method achieves faster convergence and reduced performance variability across training seeds in more challenging environments, with success rates improving by up to approximately 5 percent compared to non fuzzy reward formulations.

AI.tldr: Fuzzy logic reward shaping method improves RL performance in high-dimensional, long-horizon tasks by integrating human intuition into reward design.
AI.motivation: RL struggles with real-world tasks having high-dimensional state spaces and long horizons, where sparse/fixed rewards slow exploration and cause local optima. Need better reward design that incorporates expert knowledge.
AI.method: Fuzzy logic based reward shaping that encodes expert knowledge into adaptive, interpretable terms. Uses fuzzy rules to adapt reward contributions based on agent state, enabling smooth transitions between fast motion and precise control.
AI.result: Extensive simulation on autonomous drone racing benchmarks shows stable learning, consistent performance across difficulty levels. Achieves faster convergence, reduced performance variability across training seeds, with success rates improving by ~5% compared to non-fuzzy reward formulations.
AI.conclusion: Fuzzy logic reward shaping effectively integrates human intuition into RL, improving exploration and performance in challenging navigation tasks with high-dimensional state spaces.

title: KWBench: Measuring Unprompted Problem Recognition in Knowledge Work
summary:
We introduce the first version of KWBench (Knowledge Work Bench), a benchmark for unprompted problem recognition in large language models: can an LLM identify a professional scenario before attempting to solve it. Existing frontier benchmarks have saturated, and most knowledge-work evaluations to date reduce to extraction or task completion against a specification. KWBench targets the step before that: recognizing the governing structure of the situation from raw inputs alone.
  The benchmark contains 223 tasks sourced from practitioners across acquisitions, contract negotiations, clinical pharmacy, organizational politics, fraud analysis, and incentive design. Each task encodes a formal game-theoretic pattern (principal-agent conflict, signaling, mechanism design failure, strategic omission, coalitional dynamics, strategic interdependence) and carries structured ground truth recording the expert reading of the situation and the anticipated failure modes. Models receive raw data and a task prompt with no indication of problem type. Scoring is a three-tier rubric gated by a mandatory conjunctive check. Mandatory criteria encode the predicted wrong paths.
  We evaluate 16 models. The best model passes on 27.9% of tasks. The top two models agree on only 31.7% of their passes. Among the top 8, 44 tasks are solved by exactly one model; routing across the top 8 covers 50.7% of the benchmark, nearly double the best single model. Conditional on passing, quality scores converge (approx 83% across models); unconditional scores do not. Same models articulate the relevant game-theoretic concept correctly when asked, then fail to apply it unprompted. We release KWBench to shift how frontier models are evaluated on knowledge work, scoring them on whether they recognize the right problem from the situation alone, not only on how well they execute once the problem has been framed for them.

AI.tldr: KWBench is a new benchmark for evaluating LLMs' ability to recognize professional problem scenarios without prompting, focusing on game-theoretic patterns in knowledge work before solution attempts.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks have saturated and focus on task execution after problems are framed. There's a need to evaluate LLMs' ability to recognize the governing structure of professional situations from raw inputs alone, which is crucial for real-world knowledge work.
AI.method: Benchmark contains 223 tasks sourced from practitioners across various domains, each encoding formal game-theoretic patterns. Models receive raw data and task prompts with no indication of problem type. Scoring uses a three-tier rubric with mandatory conjunctive checks that encode predicted wrong paths.
AI.result: Best model passes only 27.9% of tasks. Top two models agree on only 31.7% of passes. Among top 8 models, 44 tasks solved by exactly one model. Routing across top 8 covers 50.7% of benchmark. Conditional on passing, quality scores converge (~83%), but unconditional scores do not. Models can articulate game-theoretic concepts when asked but fail to apply them unprompted.
AI.conclusion: LLMs struggle with unprompted problem recognition in knowledge work scenarios. The benchmark reveals significant gaps in current models' ability to identify professional situations from raw data alone. KWBench aims to shift evaluation focus from execution to problem recognition, highlighting the need for models that can frame problems autonomously.

title: SPLIT: Self-supervised Partitioning for Learned Inversion in Nonlinear Tomography
summary: Machine learning has achieved impressive performance in tomographic reconstruction, but supervised training requires paired measurements and ground-truth images that are often unavailable. This has motivated self-supervised approaches, which have primarily addressed denoising and, more recently, linear inverse problems. We address nonlinear inverse problems and introduce SPLIT (Self-supervised Partitioning for Learned Inversion in Nonlinear Tomography), a self-supervised machine-learning framework for reconstructing images from nonlinear, incomplete, and noisy projection data without any samples of ground-truth images. SPLIT enforces cross-partition consistency and measurement-domain fidelity while exploiting complementary information across multiple partitions. Our main theoretical result shows that, under mild conditions, the proposed self-supervised objective is equivalent to its supervised counterpart in expectation. We regularize training with an automatic stopping rule that halts optimization when a no-reference image-quality surrogate saturates. As a concrete application, we derive SPLIT variants for multispectral computed tomography. Experiments on sparse-view acquisitions demonstrate high reconstruction quality and robustness to noise, surpassing classical iterative reconstruction and recent self-supervised baselines.

AI.tldr: SPLIT is a self-supervised framework for nonlinear tomographic reconstruction that doesn't require ground-truth images, using cross-partition consistency and measurement fidelity.
AI.motivation: Supervised ML for tomography requires paired measurements and ground-truth images that are often unavailable, motivating self-supervised approaches that have primarily addressed denoising and linear problems, leaving nonlinear inverse problems unaddressed.
AI.method: SPLIT enforces cross-partition consistency and measurement-domain fidelity while exploiting complementary information across multiple partitions. It includes an automatic stopping rule that halts optimization when a no-reference image-quality surrogate saturates.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows the self-supervised objective is equivalent to supervised counterpart in expectation. Experiments on multispectral CT sparse-view acquisitions demonstrate high reconstruction quality and noise robustness, surpassing classical iterative reconstruction and recent self-supervised baselines.
AI.conclusion: SPLIT provides an effective self-supervised framework for nonlinear tomographic reconstruction without ground-truth images, with theoretical guarantees and practical stopping criteria, showing superior performance on challenging sparse-view CT problems.

title: Convergence Time Distributions for Max-Consensus over Unreliable Networks
summary: This paper proposes the LiFE-CD algorithm for convergence time analysis of the max-consensus algorithm in multi-agent systems under Bernoulli-distributed link failures. Unlike existing approaches, which either assume ideal communication or provide asymptotic upper bounds on the expected convergence time, LiFE-CD deterministically computes the full probability distribution of the convergence time from network topology and individual link failure probabilities, without simulation. The full probability distribution enables deadline-aware protocol design with specified reliability guarantees. Based on geometrically distributed link delays, the proposed algorithm iteratively reduces the given network topology considering both unicast and broadcast transmissions. LiFE-CD yields exact results for acyclic networks and, for cyclic networks, tight upper bounds on the convergence time via shortest-path spanning tree construction. Numerical results confirm analytical exactness for acyclic networks, validate tightness for cyclic networks, and demonstrate improvement over existing approaches. Our complexity analysis shows reduced computational cost compared to Monte Carlo simulations, while eliminating stochastic variability and enhancing reproducibility. All results extend directly to min-consensus by structural equivalence.

AI.tldr: LiFE-CD algorithm computes exact probability distribution of max-consensus convergence time under Bernoulli link failures, enabling deadline-aware protocol design with reliability guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for max-consensus convergence analysis either assume ideal communication (unrealistic) or provide only asymptotic upper bounds on expected convergence time, lacking full probability distribution needed for deadline-aware protocol design with reliability guarantees.
AI.method: LiFE-CD algorithm deterministically computes full probability distribution of convergence time from network topology and individual link failure probabilities without simulation. Based on geometrically distributed link delays, it iteratively reduces network topology considering both unicast and broadcast transmissions. For cyclic networks, uses shortest-path spanning tree construction to provide tight upper bounds.
AI.result: Algorithm yields exact results for acyclic networks and tight upper bounds for cyclic networks. Numerical results confirm analytical exactness for acyclic networks, validate tightness for cyclic networks, and demonstrate improvement over existing approaches. Complexity analysis shows reduced computational cost compared to Monte Carlo simulations while eliminating stochastic variability.
AI.conclusion: LiFE-CD provides deterministic computation of full convergence time probability distribution, enabling deadline-aware protocol design with reliability guarantees. Results extend directly to min-consensus by structural equivalence, offering practical tool for reliable multi-agent system design under link failures.

title: PRL-Bench: A Comprehensive Benchmark Evaluating LLMs' Capabilities in Frontier Physics Research
summary: The paradigm of agentic science requires AI systems to conduct robust reasoning and engage in long-horizon, autonomous exploration. However, current scientific benchmarks remain confined to domain knowledge comprehension and complex reasoning, failing to evaluate the exploratory nature and procedural complexity of real-world research. In this work, we present research-oriented evaluations in theoretical and computational physics, a natural testbed with comprehensive domain knowledge, complex reasoning, and verifiable end-to-end workflows without reliance on experiments. Here we introduce PRL-Bench (Physics Research by LLMs), a benchmark designed to systematically map the capability boundaries of LLMs in executing end-to-end physics research. Constructed from 100 curated papers from the latest issues of Physical Review Letters since August 2025 and validated by domain experts, PRL-Bench covers five major theory- and computation-intensive subfields of modern physics: astrophysics, condensed matter physics, high-energy physics, quantum information, and statistical physics. Each task in the benchmark is designed to replicate the core properties of authentic scientific research, including exploration-oriented formulation, long-horizon workflows, and objective verifiability, thereby reconstructing the essential reasoning processes and research workflows of real physics research. Evaluation across frontier models shows that performance remains limited, with the best overall score below 50, revealing a pronounced gap between current LLM capabilities and the demands of real scientific research. PRL-Bench serves a reliable testbed for accessing next generation AI scientists advancing AI systems toward autonomous scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: PRL-Bench is a new benchmark for evaluating LLMs' ability to conduct end-to-end physics research, covering 5 major subfields with 100 curated papers from Physical Review Letters since August 2025.
AI.motivation: Current scientific benchmarks focus on domain knowledge and reasoning but fail to evaluate the exploratory nature and procedural complexity of real-world research. There's a need to assess AI systems' ability to conduct autonomous scientific discovery with long-horizon workflows.
AI.method: Created PRL-Bench with 100 curated papers from latest Physical Review Letters issues (since August 2025), validated by domain experts. Covers 5 theory/computation-intensive physics subfields: astrophysics, condensed matter physics, high-energy physics, quantum information, and statistical physics. Tasks replicate authentic research properties including exploration-oriented formulation, long-horizon workflows, and objective verifiability.
AI.result: Evaluation across frontier models shows limited performance with best overall score below 50, revealing significant gap between current LLM capabilities and real scientific research demands.
AI.conclusion: PRL-Bench serves as a reliable testbed for advancing AI systems toward autonomous scientific discovery, highlighting the need for improved agentic science capabilities in LLMs.

title: From Seeing to Simulating: Generative High-Fidelity Simulation with Digital Cousins for Generalizable Robot Learning and Evaluation
summary: Learning robust robot policies in real-world environments requires diverse data augmentation, yet scaling real-world data collection is costly due to the need for acquiring physical assets and reconfiguring environments. Therefore, augmenting real-world scenes into simulation has become a practical augmentation for efficient learning and evaluation. We present a generative framework that establishes a generative real-to-sim mapping from real-world panoramas to high-fidelity simulation scenes, and further synthesize diverse cousin scenes via semantic and geometric editing. Combined with high-quality physics engines and realistic assets, the generated scenes support interactive manipulation tasks. Additionally, we incorporate multi-room stitching to construct consistent large-scale environments for long-horizon navigation across complex layouts. Experiments demonstrate a strong sim-to-real correlation validating our platform's fidelity, and show that extensively scaling up data generation leads to significantly better generalization to unseen scene and object variations, demonstrating the effectiveness of Digital Cousins for generalizable robot learning and evaluation.

AI.tldr: A generative framework creates high-fidelity simulation scenes from real-world panoramas, enabling diverse data augmentation for robot learning through semantic/geometric editing and multi-room stitching.
AI.motivation: Real-world robot policy learning requires diverse data augmentation, but physical data collection is costly and limited. There's a need for scalable simulation-based augmentation that maintains fidelity to real environments.
AI.method: Develops a generative framework that maps real-world panoramas to simulation scenes, performs semantic and geometric editing to create diverse "cousin scenes," incorporates physics engines and realistic assets, and uses multi-room stitching for large-scale environments.
AI.result: Experiments show strong sim-to-real correlation validating platform fidelity, and demonstrate that scaling up data generation leads to significantly better generalization to unseen scene and object variations.
AI.conclusion: The Digital Cousins framework provides an effective approach for generalizable robot learning and evaluation by enabling scalable, high-fidelity simulation data generation from real-world environments.

title: Stein Variational Black-Box Combinatorial Optimization
summary: Combinatorial black-box optimization in high-dimensional settings demands a careful trade-off between exploiting promising regions of the search space and preserving sufficient exploration to identify multiple optima. Although Estimation-of-Distribution Algorithms (EDAs) provide a powerful model-based framework, they often concentrate on a single region of interest, which may result in premature convergence when facing complex or multimodal objective landscapes. In this work, we incorporate the Stein operator to introduce a repulsive mechanism among particles in the parameter space, thereby encouraging the population to disperse and jointly explore several modes of the fitness landscape. Empirical evaluations across diverse benchmark problems show that the proposed method achieves performance competitive with, and in several cases superior to, leading state-of-the-art approaches, particularly on large-scale instances. These findings highlight the potential of Stein variational gradient descent as a promising direction for addressing large, computationally expensive, discrete black-box optimization problems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using Stein operator to add repulsive forces in EDAs, preventing premature convergence and improving exploration of multimodal landscapes in high-dimensional combinatorial optimization.
AI.motivation: EDAs often converge prematurely to single regions in complex multimodal landscapes, failing to explore multiple optima in high-dimensional combinatorial black-box optimization problems.
AI.method: Incorporates Stein operator to introduce repulsive mechanism among particles in parameter space, encouraging population dispersion and joint exploration of multiple fitness landscape modes.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show competitive/superior performance to state-of-the-art methods, especially on large-scale instances, demonstrating effectiveness in multimodal optimization.
AI.conclusion: Stein variational gradient descent shows promise for addressing large, computationally expensive discrete black-box optimization problems by improving exploration in EDAs.

title: Towards Realistic Open-Vocabulary Remote Sensing Segmentation: Benchmark and Baseline
summary: Open-vocabulary remote sensing image segmentation (OVRSIS) remains underexplored due to fragmented datasets, limited training diversity, and the lack of evaluation benchmarks that reflect realistic geospatial application demands. Our previous \textit{OVRSISBenchV1} established an initial cross-dataset evaluation protocol, but its limited scope is insufficient for assessing realistic open-world generalization. To address this issue, we propose \textit{OVRSISBenchV2}, a large-scale and application-oriented benchmark for OVRSIS. We first construct \textbf{OVRSIS95K}, a balanced dataset of about 95K image--mask pairs covering 35 common semantic categories across diverse remote sensing scenes. Built upon OVRSIS95K and 10 downstream datasets, OVRSISBenchV2 contains 170K images and 128 categories, substantially expanding scene diversity, semantic coverage, and evaluation difficulty. Beyond standard open-vocabulary segmentation, it further includes downstream protocols for building extraction, road extraction, and flood detection, thereby better reflecting realistic geospatial application demands and complex deployment scenarios. We also propose \textbf{Pi-Seg}, a baseline for OVRSIS. Pi-Seg improves transferability through a \textbf{positive-incentive noise} mechanism, where learnable and semantically guided perturbations broaden the visual-text feature space during training. Extensive experiments on OVRSISBenchV1, OVRSISBenchV2, and downstream tasks show that Pi-Seg delivers strong and consistent results, particularly on the more challenging OVRSISBenchV2 benchmark. Our results highlight both the importance of realistic benchmark design and the effectiveness of perturbation-based transfer for OVRSIS. The code and datasets are available at \href{https://github.com/LiBingyu01/RSKT-Seg/tree/Pi-Seg}{LiBingyu01/RSKT-Seg/tree/Pi-Seg}.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OVRSISBenchV2, a large-scale benchmark for open-vocabulary remote sensing image segmentation, and proposes Pi-Seg, a baseline method with positive-incentive noise mechanism for better transferability.
AI.motivation: Current open-vocabulary remote sensing image segmentation suffers from fragmented datasets, limited training diversity, and lack of evaluation benchmarks that reflect realistic geospatial application demands. Existing benchmarks like OVRSISBenchV1 have limited scope for assessing open-world generalization.
AI.method: 1) Construct OVRSIS95K dataset with ~95K image-mask pairs covering 35 semantic categories across diverse remote sensing scenes. 2) Build OVRSISBenchV2 benchmark with 170K images and 128 categories from OVRSIS95K and 10 downstream datasets. 3) Propose Pi-Seg baseline with positive-incentive noise mechanism - learnable and semantically guided perturbations that broaden visual-text feature space during training to improve transferability.
AI.result: Pi-Seg delivers strong and consistent results on OVRSISBenchV1, OVRSISBenchV2, and downstream tasks (building extraction, road extraction, flood detection), particularly excelling on the more challenging OVRSISBenchV2 benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the importance of realistic benchmark design for open-vocabulary remote sensing segmentation and shows the effectiveness of perturbation-based transfer methods like Pi-Seg. The benchmark better reflects geospatial application demands and complex deployment scenarios.

title: Beyond Single-Model Optimization: Preserving Plasticity in Continual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Continual reinforcement learning must balance retention with adaptation, yet many methods still rely on \emph{single-model preservation}, committing to one evolving policy as the main reusable solution across tasks. Even when a previously successful policy is retained, it may no longer provide a reliable starting point for rapid adaptation after interference, reflecting a form of \emph{loss of plasticity} that single-policy preservation cannot address. Inspired by quality-diversity methods, we introduce \textsc{TeLAPA} (Transfer-Enabled Latent-Aligned Policy Archives), a continual RL framework that organizes behaviorally diverse policy neighborhoods into per-task archives and maintains a shared latent space so that archived policies remain comparable and reusable under non-stationary drift. This perspective shifts continual RL from retaining isolated solutions to maintaining \emph{skill-aligned neighborhoods} with competent and behaviorally related policies that support future relearning. In our MiniGrid CL setting, \textsc{TeLAPA} learns more tasks successfully, recovers competence faster on revisited tasks after interference, and retains higher performance across a sequence of tasks. Our analyses show that source-optimal policies are often not transfer-optimal, even within a local competent neighborhood, and that effective reuse depends on retaining and selecting among multiple nearby alternatives rather than collapsing them to one representative. Together, these results reframe continual RL around reusable and competent policy neighborhoods, providing a route beyond single-model preservation toward more plastic lifelong agents.

AI.tldr: TeLAPA is a continual RL framework that maintains diverse policy archives per task with shared latent space, enabling faster adaptation and better performance by preserving skill-aligned neighborhoods rather than single policies.
AI.motivation: Single-model preservation in continual RL suffers from loss of plasticity - retained policies may not provide reliable starting points for adaptation after interference. Current methods lack mechanisms to preserve multiple competent policy alternatives.
AI.method: TeLAPA organizes behaviorally diverse policy neighborhoods into per-task archives with a shared latent space, keeping archived policies comparable and reusable under non-stationary drift. Inspired by quality-diversity methods, it maintains skill-aligned neighborhoods rather than isolated solutions.
AI.result: In MiniGrid CL setting, TeLAPA learns more tasks successfully, recovers competence faster on revisited tasks after interference, and retains higher performance across task sequences. Shows source-optimal policies are often not transfer-optimal, requiring multiple nearby alternatives.
AI.conclusion: Continual RL should shift from single-model preservation to maintaining reusable and competent policy neighborhoods. Effective reuse depends on retaining and selecting among multiple nearby alternatives rather than collapsing to one representative policy.

title: Limits of Lamarckian Evolution Under Pressure of Morphological Novelty
summary: Lamarckian inheritance has been shown to be a powerful accelerator in systems where the joint evolution of robot morphologies and controllers is enhanced with individual learning. Its defining advantage lies in the offspring inheriting controllers learned by their parents. The efficacy of this option, however, relies on morphological similarity between parent and offspring. In this study, we examine how Lamarckian inheritance performs when the search process is driven toward high morphological variance, potentially straining the requirement for parent-offspring similarity. Using a system of modular robots that can evolve and learn to solve a locomotion task, we compare Darwinian and Lamarckian evolution to determine how they respond to shifting from pure task-based selection to a multi-objective pressure that also rewards morphological novelty. Our results confirm that Lamarckian evolution outperforms Darwinian evolution when optimizing task-performance alone. However, introducing selection pressure for morphological diversity causes a substantial performance drop, which is much greater in the Lamarckian system. Further analyses show that promoting diversity reduces parent-offspring similarity, which in turn reduces the benefits of inheriting controllers learned by parents. These results reveal the limits of Lamarckian evolution by exposing a fundamental trade-off between inheritance-based exploitation and diversity-driven exploration.

AI.tldr: Lamarckian evolution outperforms Darwinian evolution for task performance but suffers more when morphological diversity is promoted, revealing a trade-off between inheritance-based exploitation and diversity-driven exploration.
AI.motivation: To examine how Lamarckian inheritance performs when evolutionary search is driven toward high morphological variance, which potentially strains the requirement for parent-offspring similarity that Lamarckian inheritance relies on.
AI.method: Used a system of modular robots that evolve and learn to solve a locomotion task, comparing Darwinian and Lamarckian evolution under two conditions: pure task-based selection vs. multi-objective pressure that also rewards morphological novelty.
AI.result: Lamarckian evolution outperforms Darwinian evolution when optimizing task performance alone, but introducing selection pressure for morphological diversity causes substantial performance drop, much greater in Lamarckian systems. Promoting diversity reduces parent-offspring similarity, reducing benefits of inheriting learned controllers.
AI.conclusion: Reveals limits of Lamarckian evolution by exposing a fundamental trade-off between inheritance-based exploitation (benefiting from learned controller inheritance) and diversity-driven exploration (requiring morphological novelty).

title: Discover and Prove: An Open-source Agentic Framework for Hard Mode Automated Theorem Proving in Lean 4
summary: Most ATP benchmarks embed the final answer within the formal statement -- a convention we call "Easy Mode" -- a design that simplifies the task relative to what human competitors face and may lead to optimistic estimates of model capability. We call the stricter, more realistic setting "Hard Mode": the system must independently discover the answer before constructing a formal proof. To enable Hard Mode research, we make two contributions. First, we release MiniF2F-Hard and FIMO-Hard, expert-reannotated Hard Mode variants of two widely-used ATP benchmarks. Second, we introduce Discover And Prove (DAP), an agentic framework that uses LLM natural-language reasoning with explicit self-reflection to discover answers, then rewrites Hard Mode statements into Easy Mode ones for existing ATP provers. DAP sets the state of the art: on CombiBench it raises solved problems from 7 (previous SOTA, Pass@16) to 10; on PutnamBench it is the first system to formally prove 36 theorems in Hard Mode -- while simultaneously revealing that state-of-the-art LLMs exceed 80% answer accuracy on the same problems where formal provers manage under 10%, exposing a substantial gap that Hard Mode benchmarks are uniquely suited to measure.

AI.tldr: Paper introduces "Hard Mode" ATP benchmarks where answers aren't embedded in statements, releases MiniF2F-Hard and FIMO-Hard datasets, and proposes DAP framework that discovers answers via LLM reasoning then proves them, achieving SOTA results.
AI.motivation: Current ATP benchmarks embed answers in formal statements ("Easy Mode"), which simplifies tasks and may overestimate model capabilities compared to what human competitors face. Need for more realistic "Hard Mode" benchmarks where systems must discover answers independently.
AI.method: 1) Release expert-reannotated Hard Mode variants of MiniF2F and FIMO benchmarks. 2) Introduce Discover And Prove (DAP) framework: uses LLM natural-language reasoning with self-reflection to discover answers, then rewrites Hard Mode statements into Easy Mode for existing ATP provers.
AI.result: DAP achieves state-of-the-art: on CombiBench raises solved problems from 7 to 10; on PutnamBench first to formally prove 36 theorems in Hard Mode. Reveals LLMs exceed 80% answer accuracy where formal provers manage under 10%, exposing substantial capability gap.
AI.conclusion: Hard Mode benchmarks better measure true ATP capabilities by requiring answer discovery. DAP framework effectively bridges LLM reasoning with formal proving, setting new SOTA while highlighting significant gap between LLM answer accuracy and formal prover success rates.

title: From Zero to Detail: A Progressive Spectral Decoupling Paradigm for UHD Image Restoration with New Benchmark
summary: Ultra-high-definition (UHD) image restoration poses unique challenges due to the high spatial resolution, diverse content, and fine-grained structures present in UHD images. To address these issues, we introduce a progressive spectral decomposition for the restoration process, decomposing it into three stages: zero-frequency \textbf{enhancement}, low-frequency \textbf{restoration}, and high-frequency \textbf{refinement}. Based on this formulation, we propose a novel framework, \textbf{ERR}, which integrates three cooperative sub-networks: the zero-frequency enhancer (ZFE), the low-frequency restorer (LFR), and the high-frequency refiner (HFR). The ZFE incorporates global priors to learn holistic mappings, the LFR reconstructs the main content by focusing on coarse-scale information, and the HFR adopts our proposed frequency-windowed Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (FW-KAN) to recover fine textures and intricate details for high-fidelity restoration. To further advance research in UHD image restoration, we also construct a large-scale, high-quality benchmark dataset, \textbf{LSUHDIR}, comprising 82{,}126 UHD images with diverse scenes and rich content. Our proposed methods demonstrate superior performance across a range of UHD image restoration tasks, and extensive ablation studies confirm the contribution and necessity of each module. Project page: https://github.com/NJU-PCALab/ERR.

AI.tldr: ERR framework uses progressive spectral decomposition into three stages (enhancement, restoration, refinement) with specialized sub-networks for UHD image restoration, plus a new large-scale UHD dataset.
AI.motivation: UHD image restoration is challenging due to high spatial resolution, diverse content, and fine-grained structures. Existing methods struggle with these unique UHD characteristics.
AI.method: Progressive spectral decomposition into three stages: zero-frequency enhancement, low-frequency restoration, and high-frequency refinement. ERR framework integrates three cooperative sub-networks: ZFE (global priors), LFR (coarse-scale reconstruction), and HFR with novel FW-KAN for fine texture recovery.
AI.result: Superior performance across various UHD image restoration tasks. Created LSUHDIR benchmark dataset with 82,126 UHD images. Ablation studies confirm each module's contribution and necessity.
AI.conclusion: The ERR framework effectively addresses UHD image restoration challenges through progressive spectral decomposition and specialized sub-networks, with the new LSUHDIR dataset advancing research in this domain.

title: ECG-Lens: Benchmarking ML & DL Models on PTB-XL Dataset
summary: Automated classification of electrocardiogram (ECG) signals is a useful tool for diagnosing and monitoring cardiovascular diseases. This study compares three traditional machine learning algorithms (Decision Tree Classifier, Random Forest Classifier, and Logistic Regression) and three deep learning models (Simple Convolutional Neural Network (CNN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), and Complex CNN (ECGLens)) for the classification of ECG signals from the PTB-XL dataset, which contains 12-lead recordings from normal patients and patients with various cardiac conditions. The DL models were trained on raw ECG signals, allowing them to automatically extract discriminative features. Data augmentation using the Stationary Wavelet Transform (SWT) was applied to enhance model performance, increase the diversity of training samples, and preserve the essential characteristics of the ECG signals. The models were evaluated using multiple metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. The ECG-Lens model achieved the highest performance, with 80% classification accuracy and a 90% ROC-AUC. These findings demonstrate that deep learning architectures, particularly complex CNNs substantially outperform traditional ML methods on raw 12-lead ECG data, and provide a practical benchmark for selecting automated ECG classification models and identifying directions for condition-specific model development.

AI.tldr: Comparison of traditional ML (Decision Tree, Random Forest, Logistic Regression) vs deep learning models (Simple CNN, LSTM, Complex CNN/ECGLens) for ECG classification on PTB-XL dataset shows deep learning, especially complex CNNs, substantially outperform traditional methods with 80% accuracy and 90% ROC-AUC.
AI.motivation: Automated ECG classification is crucial for diagnosing and monitoring cardiovascular diseases, but there's a need to compare traditional machine learning approaches with modern deep learning methods to determine the most effective approach for clinical applications.
AI.method: Used PTB-XL dataset with 12-lead ECG recordings. Compared three traditional ML algorithms (Decision Tree, Random Forest, Logistic Regression) with three DL models (Simple CNN, LSTM, Complex CNN/ECGLens). DL models were trained on raw ECG signals for automatic feature extraction. Applied data augmentation using Stationary Wavelet Transform (SWT) to enhance performance and preserve ECG characteristics.
AI.result: ECG-Lens (complex CNN) achieved the highest performance with 80% classification accuracy and 90% ROC-AUC. Deep learning architectures substantially outperformed traditional ML methods on raw 12-lead ECG data.
AI.conclusion: Complex CNN architectures like ECG-Lens provide superior performance for automated ECG classification compared to traditional ML methods, offering a practical benchmark for model selection and direction for condition-specific model development in clinical applications.

title: StoSignSGD: Unbiased Structural Stochasticity Fixes SignSGD for Training Large Language Models
summary: Sign-based optimization algorithms, such as SignSGD, have garnered significant attention for their remarkable performance in distributed learning and training large foundation models. Despite their empirical superiority, SignSGD is known to diverge on non-smooth objectives, which are ubiquitous in modern machine learning due to ReLUs, max-pools, and mixture-of-experts. To overcome this fundamental limitation, we propose \textbf{StoSignSGD}, an algorithm that injects structural stochasticity into the sign operator while maintaining an unbiased update step. In the regime of (online) convex optimization, our theoretical analysis shows that StoSignSGD rigorously resolves the non-convergence issues of SignSGD, achieving a sharp convergence rate matching the lower bound. For the more challenging non-convex non-smooth optimization, we introduce generalized stationary measures that encompass prior definitions, proving that StoSignSGD improves upon the best-known complexity bounds by dimensional factors. Empirically, StoSignSGD exhibits robust stability and superior efficiency across diverse large language model (LLM) training regimes. Notably, in low-precision FP8 pretraining -- a setting where AdamW fails catastrophically -- StoSignSGD remains highly stable and yields a remarkable 1.44$\times$ to 2.14$\times$ speedup relative to established baselines. Furthermore, when fine-tuning 7B LLMs on mathematical reasoning tasks, StoSignSGD delivers substantial performance gains over both AdamW and SignSGD. Finally, to dissect the mechanisms driving its success, we develop a sign conversion framework capable of transforming any general optimizer into its unbiased, sign-based counterpart. Utilizing this framework, we deconstruct the core components of StoSignSGD and present a comprehensive ablation study to empirically validate our algorithmic design choices.

AI.tldr: StoSignSGD is a new sign-based optimization algorithm that adds structural stochasticity to fix SignSGD's divergence on non-smooth objectives, achieving theoretical convergence guarantees and practical speedups in LLM training.
AI.motivation: SignSGD performs well empirically but diverges on non-smooth objectives common in modern ML (ReLUs, max-pools, MoE). This fundamental limitation needs to be addressed for robust optimization.
AI.method: StoSignSGD injects structural stochasticity into the sign operator while maintaining unbiased updates. Also introduces a sign conversion framework to transform any optimizer into its unbiased sign-based counterpart.
AI.result: Theoretically achieves sharp convergence rates matching lower bounds for convex optimization, and improves complexity bounds for non-convex non-smooth problems. Empirically shows 1.44-2.14× speedup in FP8 pretraining, stability where AdamW fails, and performance gains in LLM fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: StoSignSGD resolves SignSGD's divergence issues, provides theoretical guarantees, and demonstrates practical superiority in modern ML training scenarios, especially for large language models.

title: Environment-Adaptive Solid-State LiDAR-Inertial Odometry
summary: Solid-state LiDAR-inertial SLAM has attracted significant attention due to its advantages in speed and robustness. However, achieving accurate mapping in extreme environments remains challenging due to severe geometric degeneracy and unreliable observations, which often lead to ill-conditioned optimization and map inconsistencies. To address these challenges, we propose an environment-adaptive solid-state LiDAR-inertial odometry that integrates local normal-vector constraints with degeneracy-aware map maintenance to enhance localization accuracy. Specifically, we introduce local normal-vector constraints to improve the stability of state estimation, effectively suppressing localization drift in degenerate scenarios. Furthermore, we design a degeneration-guided map update strategy to improve map precision. Benefiting from the refined map representation, localization accuracy is further enhanced in subsequent estimation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior mapping accuracy and robustness in extreme and perceptually degraded environments, with an average RMSE reduction of up to 12.8% compared to the baseline method.

AI.tldr: An environment-adaptive solid-state LiDAR-inertial SLAM method that uses local normal-vector constraints and degeneracy-aware map maintenance to improve accuracy in extreme environments with geometric degeneracy.
AI.motivation: Solid-state LiDAR-inertial SLAM faces challenges in extreme environments due to severe geometric degeneracy and unreliable observations, leading to ill-conditioned optimization and map inconsistencies. Current methods struggle with accurate mapping in such conditions.
AI.method: Proposes two key innovations: 1) Local normal-vector constraints to improve state estimation stability and suppress localization drift in degenerate scenarios. 2) A degeneration-guided map update strategy to enhance map precision, which in turn improves subsequent localization accuracy.
AI.result: The method achieves superior mapping accuracy and robustness in extreme and perceptually degraded environments, with an average RMSE reduction of up to 12.8% compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed environment-adaptive approach effectively addresses geometric degeneracy challenges in solid-state LiDAR-inertial SLAM, enabling more accurate and robust mapping in extreme environments through integrated local constraints and intelligent map maintenance.

title: Experience Compression Spectrum: Unifying Memory, Skills, and Rules in LLM Agents
summary: As LLM agents scale to long-horizon, multi-session deployments, efficiently managing accumulated experience becomes a critical bottleneck. Agent memory systems and agent skill discovery both address this challenge -- extracting reusable knowledge from interaction traces -- yet a citation analysis of 1,136 references across 22 primary papers reveals a cross-community citation rate below 1%. We propose the \emph{Experience Compression Spectrum}, a unifying framework that positions memory, skills, and rules as points along a single axis of increasing compression (5--20$\times$ for episodic memory, 50--500$\times$ for procedural skills, 1,000$\times$+ for declarative rules), directly reducing context consumption, retrieval latency, and compute overhead. Mapping 20+ systems onto this spectrum reveals that every system operates at a fixed, predetermined compression level -- none supports adaptive cross-level compression, a gap we term the \emph{missing diagonal}. We further show that specialization alone is insufficient -- both communities independently solve shared sub-problems without exchanging solutions -- that evaluation methods are tightly coupled to compression levels, that transferability increases with compression at the cost of specificity, and that knowledge lifecycle management remains largely neglected. We articulate open problems and design principles for scalable, full-spectrum agent learning systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an "Experience Compression Spectrum" framework that unifies agent memory systems and skill discovery, showing they represent different compression levels of interaction experiences, and identifies a "missing diagonal" gap where no system supports adaptive cross-level compression.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents scale to long-horizon, multi-session deployments, efficiently managing accumulated experience becomes a critical bottleneck. There's a surprising disconnect between agent memory systems and agent skill discovery communities despite both addressing the same core challenge of extracting reusable knowledge from interaction traces.
AI.method: Conducted citation analysis of 1,136 references across 22 primary papers to measure cross-community engagement. Proposed the Experience Compression Spectrum framework that positions memory, skills, and rules along a single axis of increasing compression. Mapped 20+ existing systems onto this spectrum to analyze their characteristics and limitations.
AI.result: Found cross-community citation rate below 1%. Identified that every existing system operates at a fixed, predetermined compression level with no support for adaptive cross-level compression (the "missing diagonal"). Showed specialization alone is insufficient, evaluation methods are tightly coupled to compression levels, transferability increases with compression at the cost of specificity, and knowledge lifecycle management remains neglected.
AI.conclusion: The paper articulates open problems and design principles for scalable, full-spectrum agent learning systems that can adaptively compress experiences across different levels, enabling more efficient knowledge management and reuse in long-horizon agent deployments.

title: CPU Optimization of a Monocular 3D Biomechanics Pipeline for Low-Resource Deployment
summary: Markerless 3D movement analysis from monocular video enables accessible biomechanical assessment in clinical and sports settings. However, most research-grade pipelines rely on GPU acceleration, limiting deployment on consumer-grade hardware and in low-resource environments. In this work, we optimize a monocular 3D biomechanics pipeline derived from the MonocularBiomechanics framework for efficient CPU-only execution. Through profiling-driven system optimization, including model initialization restructuring, elimination of disk I/O serialization, and improved CPU parallelization. Experiments on a consumer workstation (AMD Ryzen 7 9700X CPU) show a 2.47x increase in processing throughput and a 59.6\% reduction in total runtime, with initialization latency reduced by 4.6x. Despite these changes, biomechanical outputs remain highly consistent with the baseline implementation (mean joint-angle deviation 0.35$^\circ$, $r=0.998$). These results demonstrate that research-grade vision-based biomechanics pipelines can be deployed on commodity CPU hardware for scalable movement assessment.

AI.tldr: Optimized monocular 3D biomechanics pipeline for CPU-only execution achieves 2.47x throughput increase and 59.6% runtime reduction while maintaining output accuracy comparable to GPU-accelerated baseline.
AI.motivation: Current research-grade biomechanics pipelines rely on GPU acceleration, limiting deployment on consumer-grade hardware and in low-resource environments where GPUs may not be available.
AI.method: Optimized the MonocularBiomechanics framework through profiling-driven system optimization including model initialization restructuring, elimination of disk I/O serialization, and improved CPU parallelization.
AI.result: On AMD Ryzen 7 9700X CPU: 2.47x increase in processing throughput, 59.6% reduction in total runtime, 4.6x reduction in initialization latency. Biomechanical outputs remain highly consistent (mean joint-angle deviation 0.35°, r=0.998).
AI.conclusion: Research-grade vision-based biomechanics pipelines can be effectively deployed on commodity CPU hardware, enabling scalable movement assessment in resource-constrained settings.

title: Transfer Learning from Foundational Optimization Embeddings to Unsupervised SAT Representations
summary: Foundational optimization embeddings have recently emerged as powerful pre-trained representations for mixed-integer programming (MIP) problems. These embeddings were shown to enable cross-domain transfer and reduce reliance on solver-generated labels. In this work, we investigate whether such representations generalize beyond optimization to decision problems, focusing on Boolean satisfiability (SAT). We adapt the foundational optimization architecture to SAT by mapping CNF formulas into the same bipartite constraint-variable graph representation used for MIPs. This allows direct reuse of the pre-trained embedding model without architectural changes or supervised fine-tuning. Our results show that these embeddings capture structural regularities in SAT instances and support unsupervised tasks such as instance clustering and distribution identification. We demonstrate, for the first time, that foundational optimization embeddings can transfer to constraint satisfaction domains. Our findings is a step toward a unified representational framework for both optimization and decision problems.

AI.tldr: Foundational optimization embeddings originally developed for MIP problems can be directly applied to SAT problems without architectural changes, enabling cross-domain transfer from optimization to decision problems.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether foundational optimization embeddings, which were developed for mixed-integer programming (MIP) problems, can generalize beyond optimization domains to decision problems like Boolean satisfiability (SAT). The goal is to explore a unified representational framework for both optimization and decision problems.
AI.method: Adapt the foundational optimization architecture to SAT by mapping CNF formulas into the same bipartite constraint-variable graph representation used for MIPs. This allows direct reuse of pre-trained embedding models without architectural modifications or supervised fine-tuning.
AI.result: The embeddings successfully capture structural regularities in SAT instances and support unsupervised tasks such as instance clustering and distribution identification. This demonstrates, for the first time, that foundational optimization embeddings can transfer to constraint satisfaction domains.
AI.conclusion: Foundational optimization embeddings can generalize to decision problems like SAT, representing a step toward a unified representational framework for both optimization and decision problems, enabling cross-domain transfer without architectural changes or fine-tuning.

title: DTEA: A Dual-Topology Elastic Actuator Enabling Real-Time Switching Between Series and Parallel Compliance
summary: Series and parallel elastic actuators offer complementary but mutually exclusive advantages, yet no existing actuator enables real-time transition between these topologies during operation. This paper presents a novel actuator design called the Dual-Topology Elastic Actuator (DTEA), which enables dynamic switching between SEA and PEA topologies during operation. A proof-of-concept prototype of the DTEA is developed to demonstrate the feasibility of the topology-switching mechanism. Experiments are conducted to evaluate the robustness and timing of the switching mechanism under operational conditions. The actuator successfully performed 324 topology-switching cycles under load without damage, demonstrating the robustness of the mechanism. The measured switching time between SEA and PEA modes is under 33.33 ms. Additional experiments are conducted to characterize the static stiffness and disturbance rejection performance in both SEA and PEA modes. Static stiffness tests show that the PEA mode is 1.53x stiffer than the SEA mode, with KSEA = 5.57 +/- 0.02 Nm/rad and KPEA = 8.54 +/- 0.02 Nm/rad. Disturbance rejection experiments show that the mean peak deflection in SEA mode is 2.26x larger than in PEA mode (5.2 deg vs. 2.3 deg), while the mean settling time is 3.45x longer (1380 ms vs. 400 ms). The observed behaviors are consistent with the known characteristics of conventional SEA and PEA actuators, validating the functionality of both modes in the DTEA actuator.

AI.tldr: A novel Dual-Topology Elastic Actuator (DTEA) enables real-time switching between series and parallel elastic actuator configurations during operation, with successful prototype demonstration and performance characterization.
AI.motivation: Series and parallel elastic actuators have complementary advantages but are mutually exclusive in existing designs, with no current actuator allowing real-time transition between these topologies during operation.
AI.method: Developed a proof-of-concept DTEA prototype with a topology-switching mechanism, conducted experiments to evaluate switching robustness/timing, and characterized static stiffness and disturbance rejection in both SEA and PEA modes.
AI.result: The actuator performed 324 switching cycles under load without damage, with switching time under 33.33 ms. PEA mode was 1.53x stiffer than SEA mode (8.54 vs 5.57 Nm/rad), and SEA mode showed 2.26x larger peak deflection and 3.45x longer settling time than PEA mode.
AI.conclusion: The DTEA successfully enables dynamic switching between SEA and PEA topologies during operation, with demonstrated robustness and performance characteristics consistent with conventional SEA and PEA actuators, validating both modes' functionality.

title: Towards Rigorous Explainability by Feature Attribution
summary: For around a decade, non-symbolic methods have been the option of choice when explaining complex machine learning (ML) models. Unfortunately, such methods lack rigor and can mislead human decision-makers. In high-stakes uses of ML, the lack of rigor is especially problematic. One prime example of provable lack of rigor is the adoption of Shapley values in explainable artificial intelligence (XAI), with the tool SHAP being a ubiquitous example. This paper overviews the ongoing efforts towards using rigorous symbolic methods of XAI as an alternative to non-rigorous non-symbolic approaches, concretely for assigning relative feature importance.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques non-symbolic XAI methods for lacking rigor, especially SHAP's use of Shapley values, and advocates for symbolic methods for feature importance attribution.
AI.motivation: Non-symbolic XAI methods have dominated for a decade but lack mathematical rigor and can mislead decision-makers, which is problematic in high-stakes ML applications. SHAP's use of Shapley values exemplifies this lack of rigor.
AI.method: The paper proposes using rigorous symbolic methods as an alternative to non-symbolic approaches for feature importance attribution, though specific symbolic techniques aren't detailed in the abstract.
AI.result: The paper provides an overview of ongoing efforts to develop and apply symbolic XAI methods that offer provable rigor compared to current non-symbolic approaches.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic XAI methods should replace non-symbolic approaches for feature importance attribution to ensure mathematical rigor and reliability in high-stakes ML applications.

title: PixDLM: A Dual-Path Multimodal Language Model for UAV Reasoning Segmentation
summary: Reasoning segmentation has recently expanded from ground-level scenes to remote-sensing imagery, yet UAV data poses distinct challenges, including oblique viewpoints, ultra-high resolutions, and extreme scale variations. To address these issues, we formally define the UAV Reasoning Segmentation task and organize its semantic requirements into three dimensions: Spatial, Attribute, and Scene-level reasoning. Based on this formulation, we construct DRSeg, a large-scale benchmark for UAV reasoning segmentation, containing 10k high-resolution aerial images paired with Chain-of-Thought QA supervision across all three reasoning types. As a benchmark companion, we introduce PixDLM, a simple yet effective pixel-level multimodal language model that serves as a unified baseline for this task. Experiments on DRSeg establish strong baseline results and highlight the unique challenges of UAV reasoning segmentation, providing a solid foundation for future research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DRSeg, a large-scale benchmark for UAV reasoning segmentation, and PixDLM, a pixel-level multimodal language model as a unified baseline, addressing unique challenges in UAV imagery analysis.
AI.motivation: UAV data presents distinct challenges compared to ground-level scenes and traditional remote-sensing imagery, including oblique viewpoints, ultra-high resolutions, and extreme scale variations. Current reasoning segmentation approaches are insufficient for these UAV-specific challenges.
AI.method: The authors formally define UAV Reasoning Segmentation and organize semantic requirements into three dimensions: Spatial, Attribute, and Scene-level reasoning. They construct DRSeg benchmark with 10k high-resolution aerial images and Chain-of-Thought QA supervision. They also introduce PixDLM, a pixel-level multimodal language model as a unified baseline.
AI.result: Experiments on DRSeg establish strong baseline results and highlight the unique challenges of UAV reasoning segmentation. The benchmark provides a solid foundation for future research in this domain.
AI.conclusion: The paper addresses the gap in UAV reasoning segmentation by providing a formal task definition, comprehensive benchmark (DRSeg), and effective baseline model (PixDLM), enabling future research on this challenging problem.

title: Evaluating LLM Simulators as Differentially Private Data Generators
summary: LLM-based simulators offer a promising path for generating complex synthetic data where traditional differentially private (DP) methods struggle with high-dimensional user profiles. But can LLMs faithfully reproduce statistical distributions from DP-protected inputs? We evaluate this using PersonaLedger, an agentic financial simulator, seeded with DP synthetic personas derived from real user statistics. We find that PersonaLedger achieves promising fraud detection utility (AUC 0.70 at epsilon=1) but exhibits significant distribution drift due to systematic LLM biases--learned priors overriding input statistics for temporal and demographic features. These failure modes must be addressed before LLM-based methods can handle the richer user representations where they might otherwise excel.

AI.tldr: LLM-based simulators show promise for synthetic data generation but struggle with statistical fidelity when seeded with DP-protected inputs, exhibiting distribution drift due to LLM biases.
AI.motivation: Traditional DP methods struggle with high-dimensional user profiles, so LLM-based simulators offer a promising alternative for generating complex synthetic data, but their ability to faithfully reproduce statistical distributions from DP-protected inputs needs evaluation.
AI.method: Used PersonaLedger, an agentic financial simulator, seeded with DP synthetic personas derived from real user statistics, to evaluate how well LLMs can reproduce statistical distributions from DP-protected inputs.
AI.result: PersonaLedger achieved promising fraud detection utility (AUC 0.70 at epsilon=1) but exhibited significant distribution drift due to systematic LLM biases, where learned priors override input statistics for temporal and demographic features.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based methods show potential for handling rich user representations but must address systematic biases and distribution drift issues before they can reliably work with DP-protected inputs.

title: Robust Fleet Sizing for Multi-UAV Inspection Missions under Synchronized Replacement Demand
summary:
Multi-UAV inspection missions require spare drones to replace active drones during recharging cycles. Existing fleet-sizing approaches often assume steady-state operating conditions that do not apply to finite-horizon missions, or they treat replacement requests as statistically independent events. The latter provides per-request blocking guarantees that fail to translate to mission-level reliability when demands cluster. This paper identifies a structural failure mode where efficient routing assigns similar workloads to each UAV, leading to synchronized battery depletion and replacement bursts that exhaust the spare pool even when average capacity is sufficient.
  We derive a closed-form sufficient fleet-sizing rule, k = m(ceil(R) + 1), where m is the number of active UAVs and R is the recovery-to-active time ratio.
