The methodology generalises to any location-allocation problem where existing desirable instances inform the selection of new candidates.

AI.tldr: A hybrid denoising autoencoder framework learns latent representations from urban features to identify optimal bike-sharing station expansion locations without explicit demand modeling, using similarity to existing successful stations.
AI.motivation: Traditional bike-sharing station expansion methods rely on explicit demand modeling that may miss complex urban patterns distinguishing successful stations, especially in data-constrained environments where leveraging patterns from existing stations is crucial.
AI.method: Hybrid Denoising Autoencoder (HDAE) learns compressed latent representations from multi-source grid-level features (socio-demographic, built environment, transport network) with supervised classification head to regularize embedding space. Expansion candidates selected via greedy allocation with spatial constraints based on latent-space similarity to existing successful stations.
AI.result: HDAE embeddings produce more spatially coherent clusters and allocation patterns than raw features. Sensitivity analyses confirm robustness across similarity methods and distance metrics. Consensus-based procedure across multiple parametrizations identifies 32 high-confidence extension zones where all parametrizations agree.
AI.conclusion: Representation learning captures complex patterns missed by raw features, enabling evidence-based expansion planning without explicit demand modeling. The consensus procedure strengthens recommendations, and framework configurability allows incorporation of operational knowledge. Methodology generalizes to location-allocation problems where existing desirable instances inform new candidate selection.

title: MMGait: Towards Multi-Modal Gait Recognition
summary: Gait recognition has emerged as a powerful biometric technique for identifying individuals at a distance without requiring user cooperation. Most existing methods focus primarily on RGB-derived modalities, which fall short in real-world scenarios requiring multi-modal collaboration and cross-modal retrieval. To overcome these challenges, we present MMGait, a comprehensive multi-modal gait benchmark integrating data from five heterogeneous sensors, including an RGB camera, a depth camera, an infrared camera, a LiDAR scanner, and a 4D Radar system. MMGait contains twelve modalities and 334,060 sequences from 725 subjects, enabling systematic exploration across geometric, photometric, and motion domains. Based on MMGait, we conduct extensive evaluations on single-modal, cross-modal, and multi-modal paradigms to analyze modality robustness and complementarity. Furthermore, we introduce a new task, Omni Multi-Modal Gait Recognition, which aims to unify the above three gait recognition paradigms within a single model. We also propose a simple yet powerful baseline, OmniGait, which learns a shared embedding space across diverse modalities and achieves promising recognition performance. The MMGait benchmark, codebase, and pretrained checkpoints are publicly available at https://github.com/BNU-IVC/MMGait.

AI.tldr: MMGait is a comprehensive multi-modal gait benchmark with 5 sensors, 12 modalities, and 334K sequences from 725 subjects, enabling unified gait recognition across single-modal, cross-modal, and multi-modal paradigms.
AI.motivation: Existing gait recognition methods focus primarily on RGB-derived modalities, which are insufficient for real-world scenarios requiring multi-modal collaboration and cross-modal retrieval.
AI.method: Created MMGait benchmark with data from RGB camera, depth camera, infrared camera, LiDAR scanner, and 4D Radar system. Proposed OmniGait baseline that learns a shared embedding space across diverse modalities.
AI.result: MMGait contains 12 modalities and 334,060 sequences from 725 subjects. OmniGait achieves promising recognition performance by unifying single-modal, cross-modal, and multi-modal paradigms within a single model.
AI.conclusion: MMGait enables systematic exploration of gait recognition across geometric, photometric, and motion domains, addressing limitations of RGB-only approaches and facilitating multi-modal gait research.

title: EVIL: Evolving Interpretable Algorithms for Zero-Shot Inference on Event Sequences and Time Series with LLMs
summary: We introduce EVIL (\textbf{EV}olving \textbf{I}nterpretable algorithms with \textbf{L}LMs), an approach that uses LLM-guided evolutionary search to discover simple, interpretable algorithms for dynamical systems inference. Rather than training neural networks on large datasets, EVIL evolves pure Python/NumPy programs that perform zero-shot, in-context inference across datasets. We apply EVIL to three distinct tasks: next-event prediction in temporal point processes, rate matrix estimation for Markov jump processes, and time series imputation. In each case, a single evolved algorithm generalizes across all evaluation datasets without per-dataset training (analogous to an amortized inference model). To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to show that LLM-guided program evolution can discover a single compact inference function for these dynamical-systems problems. Across the three domains, the discovered algorithms are often competitive with, and even outperform, state-of-the-art deep learning models while being orders of magnitudes faster, and remaining fully interpretable.

AI.tldr: EVIL uses LLM-guided evolutionary search to discover simple, interpretable Python/NumPy programs for zero-shot inference in dynamical systems, outperforming deep learning models while being faster and interpretable.
AI.motivation: Current approaches for dynamical systems inference often rely on training neural networks on large datasets, which can be computationally expensive, lack interpretability, and require per-dataset training. There's a need for simple, interpretable algorithms that can perform zero-shot inference across multiple datasets without extensive training.
AI.method: EVIL uses LLM-guided evolutionary search to evolve pure Python/NumPy programs. The approach discovers compact inference functions through evolutionary algorithms guided by large language models, creating interpretable code that performs zero-shot, in-context inference across datasets without per-dataset training.
AI.result: EVIL successfully discovers single compact inference algorithms for three domains: next-event prediction in temporal point processes, rate matrix estimation for Markov jump processes, and time series imputation. The evolved algorithms generalize across all evaluation datasets without per-dataset training, often matching or outperforming state-of-the-art deep learning models while being orders of magnitude faster and fully interpretable.
AI.conclusion: LLM-guided evolutionary search can discover simple, interpretable algorithms for dynamical systems inference that are competitive with deep learning approaches while offering significant advantages in speed and interpretability, demonstrating the potential of program evolution for creating efficient inference methods.

title: IA-CLAHE: Image-Adaptive Clip Limit Estimation for CLAHE
comment: Accepted to NTIRE 2026 Workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: This paper proposes image-adaptive contrast limited adaptive histogram equalization (IA-CLAHE). Conventional CLAHE is widely used to boost the performance of various computer vision tasks and to improve visual quality for human perception in practical industrial applications. CLAHE applies contrast limited histogram equalization to each local region to enhance local contrast. However, CLAHE often leads to over-enhancement, because the contrast-limiting parameter clip limit is fixed regardless of the histogram distribution of each local region. Our IA-CLAHE addresses this limitation by adaptively estimating tile-wise clip limits from the input image. To achieve this, we train a lightweight clip limits estimator with a differentiable extension of CLAHE, enabling end-to-end optimization. Unlike prior learning-based CLAHE methods, IA-CLAHE does not require pre-searched ground-truth clip limits or task-specific datasets, because it learns to map input image histograms toward a domain-invariant uniform distribution, enabling zero-shot generalization across diverse conditions. Experimental results show that IA-CLAHE consistently improves recognition performance, while simultaneously enhancing visual quality for human perception, without requiring any task-specific training data.

AI.tldr: IA-CLAHE is an adaptive version of CLAHE that learns tile-wise clip limits from input images using a lightweight estimator, enabling zero-shot generalization without task-specific training data.
AI.motivation: Conventional CLAHE often causes over-enhancement because it uses a fixed clip limit parameter regardless of local histogram distributions, limiting its effectiveness across diverse conditions.
AI.method: Train a lightweight clip limits estimator with a differentiable extension of CLAHE for end-to-end optimization, learning to map input image histograms toward a domain-invariant uniform distribution without requiring ground-truth clip limits or task-specific datasets.
AI.result: IA-CLAHE consistently improves recognition performance while enhancing visual quality for human perception, demonstrating zero-shot generalization across diverse conditions without task-specific training.
AI.conclusion: IA-CLAHE addresses CLAHE's over-enhancement limitation through adaptive clip limit estimation, offering a practical solution that works across various conditions without requiring task-specific training data.

title: Convolutionally Low-Rank Models with Modified Quantile Regression for Interval Time Series Forecasting
summary: The quantification of uncertainty in prediction models is crucial for reliable decision-making, yet remains a significant challenge. Interval time series forecasting offers a principled solution to this problem by providing prediction intervals (PIs), which indicates the probability that the true value falls within the predicted range. We consider a recently established point forecasts (PFs) method termed Learning-Based Convolution Nuclear Norm Minimization (LbCNNM), which directly generates multi-step ahead forecasts by leveraging the convolutional low-rankness property derived from training data. While theoretically complete and empirically effective, LbCNNM lacks inherent uncertainty estimation capabilities, a limitation shared by many advanced forecasting methods. To resolve the issue, we modify the well-known Quantile Regression (QR) and integrate it into LbCNNM, resulting in a novel interval forecasting method termed LbCNNM with Modified Quantile Regression (LbCNNM-MQR). In addition, we devise interval calibration techniques to further improve the accuracy of PIs. Extensive experiments on over 100,000 real-world time series demonstrate the superior performance of LbCNNM-MQR.

AI.tldr: LbCNNM-MQR extends LbCNNM point forecasting with modified quantile regression for uncertainty-aware interval time series forecasting.
AI.motivation: Existing advanced forecasting methods like LbCNNM lack inherent uncertainty estimation capabilities, which is crucial for reliable decision-making. Interval forecasting provides prediction intervals to quantify uncertainty.
AI.method: Modified Quantile Regression (MQR) integrated into LbCNNM framework, plus interval calibration techniques to improve prediction interval accuracy.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on over 100,000 real-world time series demonstrate superior performance of LbCNNM-MQR for interval forecasting.
AI.conclusion: LbCNNM-MQR successfully addresses the uncertainty estimation limitation of LbCNNM by integrating modified quantile regression, providing reliable prediction intervals for time series forecasting.

title: Breakout-picker: Reducing false positives in deep learning-based borehole breakout characterization from acoustic image logs
summary: Borehole breakouts are stress-induced spalling on the borehole wall, which are identifiable in acoustic image logs as paired zones with near-symmetry azimuths, low acoustic amplitudes, and increased borehole radius. Accurate breakout characterization is crucial for in-situ stress analysis. In recent years, deep learning has been introduced to automate the time-consuming and labor-intensive breakout picking process. However, existing approaches often suffer from misclassification of non-breakout features, leading to high false positive rates. To address this limitation, this study develops a deep learning framework, termed Breakout-picker, with a specific focus on reducing false positives in automatic breakout characterization. Breakout-picker reduces false positives through two strategies. First, the training of Breakout-picker incorporates negative samples of non-breakout features, including natural fractures, keyseats, and logging artifacts. They share similar characteristics with breakouts, such as low acoustic amplitude or locally enlarged borehole radius. These negative training samples enables Breakout-picker to better discriminate true breakouts and similar non-breakout features. Second, candidate breakouts identified by Breakout-picker are further validated by azimuthal symmetry criteria, whereby detections that do not exhibit the near-symmetry characteristics of breakout azimuth are excluded. The performance of Breakout-picker is evaluated using three acoustic image log datasets from different regions. The results demonstrate that Breakout-picker outperforms other automatic methods with higher accuracy and substantially lower false positive rates. By reducing false positives, Breakout-picker enhances the reliability of automatic breakout characterization from acoustic image logs, which in turn benefits in-situ stress analysis based on borehole breakouts.

AI.tldr: Breakout-picker: A deep learning framework that reduces false positives in automatic borehole breakout characterization from acoustic image logs by incorporating negative training samples and azimuthal symmetry validation.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning approaches for automating borehole breakout picking suffer from high false positive rates due to misclassification of non-breakout features like natural fractures, keyseats, and logging artifacts that share similar characteristics with breakouts.
AI.method: Two-pronged approach: 1) Training incorporates negative samples of non-breakout features to improve discrimination, 2) Candidate breakouts are validated by azimuthal symmetry criteria to exclude detections without near-symmetry characteristics.
AI.result: Breakout-picker outperforms other automatic methods with higher accuracy and substantially lower false positive rates when evaluated on three acoustic image log datasets from different regions.
AI.conclusion: By reducing false positives, Breakout-picker enhances the reliability of automatic breakout characterization from acoustic image logs, benefiting in-situ stress analysis based on borehole breakouts.

title: Self-Distillation as a Performance Recovery Mechanism for LLMs: Counteracting Compression and Catastrophic Forgetting
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success, underpinning diverse AI applications. However, they often suffer from performance degradation due to factors such as catastrophic forgetting during Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), quantization, and pruning. In this work, we introduce a performance recovery framework based on Self-Distillation Fine-Tuning (SDFT) that effectively restores model capabilities. Complementing this practical contribution, we provide a rigorous theoretical explanation for the underlying recovery mechanism. We posit that an LLM's generative capability fundamentally relies on the high-dimensional manifold constructed by its hidden layers. To investigate this, we employ Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to quantify the alignment between student and teacher activation trajectories, leveraging its invariance to orthogonal transformations and scaling. Our experiments demonstrate a strong correlation between performance recovery and manifold alignment, substantiating the claim that self-distillation effectively aligns the student's high-dimensional manifold with the optimal structure represented by the teacher. This study bridges the gap between practical recovery frameworks and geometric representation theory, offering new insights into the internal mechanisms of self-distillation.

AI.tldr: Self-Distillation Fine-Tuning (SDFT) framework recovers LLM performance lost from fine-tuning, quantization, or pruning, with theoretical explanation based on high-dimensional manifold alignment.
AI.motivation: LLMs suffer performance degradation from catastrophic forgetting during SFT, quantization, and pruning. There's a need for practical recovery methods and theoretical understanding of why they work.
AI.method: Proposes Self-Distillation Fine-Tuning (SDFT) framework for performance recovery. Uses Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to measure alignment between student and teacher activation trajectories, leveraging its invariance properties to analyze high-dimensional manifold structures.
AI.result: Experiments show strong correlation between performance recovery and manifold alignment, demonstrating that self-distillation effectively aligns the student's high-dimensional manifold with the optimal teacher structure.
AI.conclusion: The study bridges practical recovery frameworks with geometric representation theory, providing insights into self-distillation mechanisms and showing that LLM generative capability relies on high-dimensional manifold structures in hidden layers.

title: Ranking XAI Methods for Head and Neck Cancer Outcome Prediction
comment: 4-page conference paper, accepted at IEEE ISBI 2026 (International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging)
summary: For head and neck cancer (HNC) patients, prognostic outcome prediction can support personalized treatment strategy selection. Improving prediction performance of HNC outcomes has been extensively explored by using advanced artificial intelligence (AI) techniques on PET/CT data. However, the interpretability of AI remains a critical obstacle for its clinical adoption. Unlike previous HNC studies that empirically selected explainable AI (XAI) techniques, we are the first to comprehensively evaluate and rank 13 XAI methods across 24 metrics, covering faithfulness, robustness, complexity and plausibility. Experimental results on the multi-center HECKTOR challenge dataset show large variations across evaluation aspects among different XAI methods, with Integrated Gradients (IG) and DeepLIFT (DL) consistently obtained high rankings for faithfulness, complexity and plausibility. This work highlights the importance of comprehensive XAI method evaluation and can be extended to other medical imaging tasks.

AI.tldr: Comprehensive evaluation of 13 XAI methods for head and neck cancer outcome prediction using PET/CT data, ranking them across 24 metrics covering faithfulness, robustness, complexity, and plausibility.
AI.motivation: While AI techniques have improved HNC outcome prediction, their clinical adoption is hindered by interpretability issues. Previous studies empirically selected XAI methods without comprehensive evaluation, creating a need for systematic assessment.
AI.method: Comprehensive evaluation of 13 XAI methods across 24 metrics covering four aspects: faithfulness, robustness, complexity, and plausibility. Used multi-center HECKTOR challenge dataset for experimental validation.
AI.result: Large variations in performance across evaluation aspects among different XAI methods. Integrated Gradients (IG) and DeepLIFT (DL) consistently achieved high rankings for faithfulness, complexity, and plausibility.
AI.conclusion: This work emphasizes the importance of comprehensive XAI method evaluation for clinical adoption and provides a framework that can be extended to other medical imaging tasks.

title: Elucidating the SNR-t Bias of Diffusion Probabilistic Models
summary: Diffusion Probabilistic Models have demonstrated remarkable performance across a wide range of generative tasks. However, we have observed that these models often suffer from a Signal-to-Noise Ratio-timestep (SNR-t) bias. This bias refers to the misalignment between the SNR of the denoising sample and its corresponding timestep during the inference phase. Specifically, during training, the SNR of a sample is strictly coupled with its timestep. However, this correspondence is disrupted during inference, leading to error accumulation and impairing the generation quality. We provide comprehensive empirical evidence and theoretical analysis to substantiate this phenomenon and propose a simple yet effective differential correction method to mitigate the SNR-t bias. Recognizing that diffusion models typically reconstruct low-frequency components before focusing on high-frequency details during the reverse denoising process, we decompose samples into various frequency components and apply differential correction to each component individually. Extensive experiments show that our approach significantly improves the generation quality of various diffusion models (IDDPM, ADM, DDIM, A-DPM, EA-DPM, EDM, PFGM++, and FLUX) on datasets of various resolutions with negligible computational overhead. The code is at https://github.com/AMAP-ML/DCW.

AI.tldr: Diffusion models suffer from SNR-timestep bias during inference, where the signal-to-noise ratio of denoising samples doesn't match their timesteps, causing error accumulation. The paper proposes a differential correction method that applies frequency-specific corrections to mitigate this bias.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models exhibit SNR-t bias during inference - a misalignment between the SNR of denoising samples and their corresponding timesteps. This bias disrupts the training-time correspondence between SNR and timestep, leading to error accumulation and impaired generation quality.
AI.method: Proposes a differential correction method that leverages the observation that diffusion models reconstruct low-frequency components before high-frequency details. The method decomposes samples into various frequency components and applies differential correction to each component individually to mitigate SNR-t bias.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show significant improvement in generation quality across various diffusion models (IDDPM, ADM, DDIM, A-DPM, EA-DPM, EDM, PFGM++, FLUX) on datasets of various resolutions with negligible computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: The SNR-t bias is a fundamental issue in diffusion models that impairs generation quality. The proposed differential correction method effectively mitigates this bias, improving performance across multiple diffusion architectures with minimal computational cost.

title: Placing Puzzle Pieces Where They Matter: A Question Augmentation Framework for Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement learning has become a powerful approach for enhancing large language model reasoning, but faces a fundamental dilemma: training on easy problems can cause overfitting and pass@k degradation, while training on hard problems often results in sparse rewards. Recent question augmentation methods address this by prepending partial solutions as hints. However, uniform hint provision may introduce redundant information while missing critical reasoning bottlenecks, and excessive hints can reduce reasoning diversity, causing pass@k degradation. We propose \textbf{PieceHint}, a hint injection framework that strategically identifies and provides critical reasoning steps during training. By scoring the importance of different reasoning steps, selectively allocating hints based on problem difficulty, and progressively withdrawing scaffolding, PieceHint enables models to transition from guided learning to independent reasoning. Experiments on six mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that our 1.5B model achieves comparable average performance to 32B baselines while preserving pass@k diversity across all $k$ values.

AI.tldr: PieceHint is a reinforcement learning framework that strategically injects critical reasoning hints during training to improve LLM reasoning without causing overfitting or pass@k degradation.
AI.motivation: Current RL approaches for LLM reasoning face a dilemma: easy problems cause overfitting and pass@k degradation, while hard problems have sparse rewards. Existing hint methods provide uniform hints that introduce redundancy while missing critical bottlenecks, and excessive hints reduce reasoning diversity.
AI.method: PieceHint scores importance of different reasoning steps, selectively allocates hints based on problem difficulty, and progressively withdraws scaffolding to transition models from guided learning to independent reasoning.
AI.result: On six mathematical reasoning benchmarks, their 1.5B model achieves comparable average performance to 32B baselines while preserving pass@k diversity across all k values.
AI.conclusion: Strategic hint injection through PieceHint enables smaller models to achieve performance comparable to much larger baselines while maintaining reasoning diversity, addressing fundamental RL training dilemmas for LLM reasoning.

title: Mind's Eye: A Benchmark of Visual Abstraction, Transformation and Composition for Multimodal LLMs
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved impressive progress on vision language benchmarks, yet their capacity for visual cognitive and visuospatial reasoning remains less understood. We introduce "Mind's Eye", a multiple-choice benchmark of eight visuo-cognitive tasks inspired by classic human intelligence tests and organized under a novel "A-R-T" taxonomy: Abstraction, Relation, and Transformation. The tasks probe core processes of fluid intelligence such as pattern induction, analogical relation mapping, and mental transformation. We evaluate a diverse suite of closed-source and open-source MLLMs and compare their performance with human participants. Humans achieve 80% accuracy, while top performing MLLMs remain below 50%. Error analysis reveals failures in: (i) visual attention allocation, (ii) internal perceptual manipulation, and (iii) weak abstraction of underlying visual concepts. Our findings suggest that current MLLMs exhibit limited visuospatial reasoning capabilities, when compared with human participants, highlighting the need for more cognitively grounded evaluation frameworks.

AI.tldr: MLLMs perform poorly (under 50%) on visuospatial reasoning tasks where humans achieve 80% accuracy, revealing limitations in visual attention, mental manipulation, and concept abstraction.
AI.motivation: Despite impressive progress on vision-language benchmarks, the visual cognitive and visuospatial reasoning capabilities of multimodal large language models remain poorly understood and need systematic evaluation.
AI.method: Created "Mind's Eye" benchmark with 8 visuo-cognitive tasks organized under A-R-T taxonomy (Abstraction, Relation, Transformation), evaluated diverse MLLMs and compared with human performance.
AI.result: Humans achieved 80% accuracy while top MLLMs remained below 50%. Error analysis revealed failures in visual attention allocation, internal perceptual manipulation, and weak abstraction of visual concepts.
AI.conclusion: Current MLLMs exhibit limited visuospatial reasoning capabilities compared to humans, highlighting the need for more cognitively grounded evaluation frameworks to advance true visual understanding.

title: Modern Structure-Aware Simplicial Spatiotemporal Neural Network
summary: Spatiotemporal modeling has evolved beyond simple time series analysis to become fundamental in structural time series analysis. While current research extensively employs graph neural networks (GNNs) for spatial feature extraction with notable success, these networks are limited to capturing only pairwise relationships, despite real-world networks containing richer topological relationships. Additionally, GNN-based models face computational challenges that scale with graph complexity, limiting their applicability to large networks. To address these limitations, we present Modern Structure-Aware Simplicial SpatioTemporal neural network (ModernSASST), the first approach to leverage simplicial complex structures for spatiotemporal modeling. Our method employs spatiotemporal random walks on high-dimensional simplicial complexes and integrates parallelizable Temporal Convolutional Networks to capture high-order topological structures while maintaining computational efficiency. Our source code is publicly available on GitHub\footnote{Code is available at: https://github.com/ComplexNetTSP/ST_RUM.

AI.tldr: ModernSASST is a novel spatiotemporal model using simplicial complexes for high-order topological relationships, addressing limitations of GNNs that only capture pairwise relationships and have computational scalability issues.
AI.motivation: Current GNN-based spatiotemporal models are limited to pairwise relationships despite real-world networks containing richer topological structures, and they face computational challenges that scale with graph complexity, limiting applicability to large networks.
AI.method: Uses spatiotemporal random walks on high-dimensional simplicial complexes and integrates parallelizable Temporal Convolutional Networks to capture high-order topological structures while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.result: First approach to leverage simplicial complex structures for spatiotemporal modeling, enabling capture of richer topological relationships beyond pairwise connections.
AI.conclusion: ModernSASST addresses key limitations of GNN-based models by using simplicial complexes to capture high-order topological structures while maintaining computational efficiency for large-scale applications.

title: Chain-of-Thought Degrades Visual Spatial Reasoning Capabilities of Multimodal LLMs
summary: Multimodal Reasoning Models (MRMs) leveraging Chain-of-Thought (CoT) based thinking have revolutionized mathematical and logical problem-solving. However, we show that this paradigm struggles with generalized spatial intelligence. We perform a comprehensive evaluation of seventeen models across thirteen spatial benchmarks and identify a critical gap: CoT prompting consistently degrades performance in visual spatial reasoning. Furthermore, through a novel No-Image++ ablation, we demonstrate that MRMs and CoT prompted MLMs suffer from severe shortcut learning, and hallucinate visual details from textual priors even when the image is absent. These findings challenge the efficacy of text-only CoT for spatial tasks and underscore the need for vision-centric reasoning paradigms.

AI.tldr: CoT-based multimodal reasoning models fail at spatial tasks, performing worse with CoT prompting and hallucinating visual details from text priors.
AI.motivation: Despite success in math/logic problems, Chain-of-Thought reasoning in multimodal models struggles with spatial intelligence, requiring investigation of this critical gap.
AI.method: Comprehensive evaluation of 17 models across 13 spatial benchmarks, plus novel No-Image++ ablation to test shortcut learning and hallucination.
AI.result: CoT prompting consistently degrades spatial reasoning performance; models suffer severe shortcut learning and hallucinate visual details from text when images are absent.
AI.conclusion: Text-only CoT is ineffective for spatial tasks, highlighting need for vision-centric reasoning paradigms beyond current multimodal approaches.

title: Reversible Residual Normalization Alleviates Spatio-Temporal Distribution Shift
summary: Distribution shift severely degrades the performance of deep forecasting models. While this issue is well-studied for individual time series, it remains a significant challenge in the spatio-temporal domain. Effective solutions like instance normalization and its variants can mitigate temporal shifts by standardizing statistics. However, distribution shift on a graph is far more complex, involving not only the drift of individual node series but also heterogeneity across the spatial network where different nodes exhibit distinct statistical properties. To tackle this problem, we propose Reversible Residual Normalization (RRN), a novel framework that performs spatially-aware invertible transformations to address distribution shift in both spatial and temporal dimensions. Our approach integrates graph convolutional operations within invertible residual blocks, enabling adaptive normalization that respects the underlying graph structure while maintaining reversibility. By combining Center Normalization with spectral-constrained graph neural networks, our method captures and normalizes complex Spatio-Temporal relationships in a data-driven manner. The bidirectional nature of our framework allows models to learn in a normalized latent space and recover original distributional properties through inverse transformation, offering a robust and model-agnostic solution for forecasting on dynamic spatio-temporal systems.

AI.tldr: RRN is a novel framework using reversible residual normalization with graph convolutions to handle spatio-temporal distribution shifts in forecasting models.
AI.motivation: Distribution shift severely degrades deep forecasting models, especially in spatio-temporal domains where it's more complex than individual time series. Existing solutions like instance normalization only address temporal shifts but fail to handle spatial heterogeneity across graph networks where different nodes have distinct statistical properties.
AI.method: Proposes Reversible Residual Normalization (RRN) - a framework with spatially-aware invertible transformations using graph convolutional operations within invertible residual blocks. Combines Center Normalization with spectral-constrained graph neural networks for adaptive normalization that respects graph structure while maintaining reversibility.
AI.result: The bidirectional framework allows models to learn in a normalized latent space and recover original distributional properties through inverse transformation, offering robust and model-agnostic forecasting on dynamic spatio-temporal systems.
AI.conclusion: RRN provides an effective solution to complex spatio-temporal distribution shifts by integrating graph-aware normalization with reversible transformations, addressing both spatial heterogeneity and temporal drift simultaneously.

title: TableSeq: Unified Generation of Structure, Content, and Layout
summary: We present TableSeq, an image-only, end-to-end framework for joint table structure recognition, content recognition, and cell localization. The model formulates these tasks as a single sequence-generation problem: one decoder produces an interleaved stream of \texttt{HTML} tags, cell text, and discretized coordinate tokens, thereby aligning logical structure, textual content, and cell geometry within a unified autoregressive sequence. This design avoids external OCR, auxiliary decoders, and complex multi-stage post-processing. TableSeq combines a lightweight high-resolution FCN-H16 encoder with a minimal structure-prior head and a single-layer transformer encoder, yielding a compact architecture that remains effective on challenging layouts. Across standard benchmarks, TableSeq achieves competitive or state-of-the-art results while preserving architectural simplicity. It reaches 95.23 TEDS / 96.83 S-TEDS on PubTabNet, 97.45 TEDS / 98.69 S-TEDS on FinTabNet, and 99.79 / 99.54 / 99.66 precision / recall / F1 on SciTSR under the CAR protocol, while remaining competitive on PubTables-1M under GriTS. Beyond TSR/TCR, the same sequence interface generalizes to index-based table querying without task-specific heads, achieving the best IRDR score and competitive ICDR/ICR performance. We also study multi-token prediction for faster blockwise decoding and show that it reduces inference latency with only limited accuracy degradation. Overall, TableSeq provides a practical and reproducible single-stream baseline for unified table recognition, and the source code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/hamdilaziz/TableSeq.

AI.tldr: TableSeq is an end-to-end image-only framework that unifies table structure recognition, content recognition, and cell localization into a single sequence generation problem using HTML tags, text, and coordinates.
AI.motivation: To simplify table recognition by avoiding complex multi-stage pipelines, external OCR systems, auxiliary decoders, and extensive post-processing that current methods require.
AI.method: Formulates table recognition as a single sequence generation problem using a decoder that produces interleaved HTML tags, cell text, and discretized coordinate tokens. Uses a lightweight FCN-H16 encoder with structure-prior head and single-layer transformer encoder.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA or competitive results: 95.23 TEDS/96.83 S-TEDS on PubTabNet, 97.45 TEDS/98.69 S-TEDS on FinTabNet, 99.79/99.54/99.66 precision/recall/F1 on SciTSR. Also generalizes to table querying with best IRDR score.
AI.conclusion: TableSeq provides a practical, reproducible single-stream baseline for unified table recognition with architectural simplicity, competitive performance, and generalization capabilities beyond traditional TSR/TCR tasks.

title: DPrivBench: Benchmarking LLMs' Reasoning for Differential Privacy
summary: Differential privacy (DP) has a wide range of applications for protecting data privacy, but designing and verifying DP algorithms requires expert-level reasoning, creating a high barrier for non-expert practitioners. Prior works either rely on specialized verification languages that demand substantial domain expertise or remain semi-automated and require human-in-the-loop guidance. In this work, we investigate whether large language models (LLMs) can automate DP reasoning. We introduce DPrivBench, a benchmark in which each instance asks whether a function or algorithm satisfies a stated DP guarantee under specified assumptions. The benchmark is carefully designed to cover a broad range of DP topics, span diverse difficulty levels, and resist shortcut reasoning through trivial pattern matching. Experiments show that while the strongest models handle textbook mechanisms well, all models struggle with advanced algorithms, revealing substantial gaps in current DP reasoning capabilities. Through further analytic study and failure-mode analysis, we identify several promising directions for improving automated DP reasoning. Our benchmark provides a solid foundation for developing and evaluating such methods, and complements existing benchmarks for mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: LLMs struggle with automated differential privacy reasoning despite handling basic textbook mechanisms, revealing significant gaps in advanced DP algorithm verification.
AI.motivation: Differential privacy algorithm design and verification requires expert-level reasoning, creating barriers for non-experts. Existing approaches either need specialized verification languages or remain semi-automated with human guidance.
AI.method: Introduces DPrivBench, a benchmark with instances asking whether functions/algorithms satisfy stated DP guarantees under specified assumptions. Covers broad DP topics, spans difficulty levels, and resists shortcut reasoning through trivial pattern matching.
AI.result: Strongest models handle textbook mechanisms well, but all models struggle with advanced algorithms, revealing substantial gaps in current DP reasoning capabilities. Further analysis identifies promising directions for improvement.
AI.conclusion: DPrivBench provides foundation for developing/evaluating automated DP reasoning methods and complements existing mathematical reasoning benchmarks. LLMs show promise but need significant improvement for advanced DP verification.

title: The Amazing Stability of Flow Matching
comment: EurIPS 2025 Workshop on Principles of Generative Modeling (PriGM)
summary:
The success of deep generative models in generating high-quality and diverse samples is often attributed to particular architectures and large training datasets. In this paper, we investigate the impact of these factors on the quality and diversity of samples generated by \emph{flow-matching} models. Surprisingly, in our experiments on CelebA-HQ dataset, flow matching remains stable even when pruning 50\% of the dataset. That is, the quality and diversity of generated samples are preserved. Moreover, pruning impacts the latent representation only slightly, that is, samples generated by models trained on the full and pruned dataset map to visually similar outputs for a given seed. We observe similar stability when changing the architecture or training configuration, such that the latent representation is maintained under these changes as well.
  Our results quantify just how strong this stability can be in practice, and help explain the reliability of flow-matching models under various perturbations.

AI.tldr: Flow matching models show remarkable stability - they maintain sample quality and diversity even when 50% of training data is pruned, and preserve latent representations across architectural and training changes.
AI.motivation: To investigate how much deep generative models' success depends on specific architectures and large datasets, particularly examining the robustness of flow-matching models to data reduction and architectural changes.
AI.method: Experimental analysis of flow-matching models on CelebA-HQ dataset, systematically pruning training data (up to 50%), modifying architectures, and varying training configurations while evaluating sample quality, diversity, and latent representation consistency.
AI.result: Flow matching remains stable with 50% data pruning - quality and diversity preserved. Latent representations are minimally affected, with similar outputs for same seeds across full/pruned datasets. Stability also observed under architectural and training configuration changes.
AI.conclusion: Flow-matching models exhibit strong stability against data reduction and architectural perturbations, explaining their reliability in practice and suggesting they're less dependent on massive datasets than previously thought.

title: QuantSightBench: Evaluating LLM Quantitative Forecasting with Prediction Intervals
summary: Forecasting has become a natural benchmark for reasoning under uncertainty. Yet existing evaluations of large language models remain limited to judgmental tasks in simple formats, such as binary or multiple-choice questions. In practice, however, forecasting spans a far broader scope. Across domains such as economics, public health, and social demographics, decisions hinge on numerical estimates over continuous quantities, a capability that current benchmarks do not capture. Evaluating such estimates requires a format that makes uncertainty explicit and testable. We propose prediction intervals as a natural and rigorous interface for this purpose. They demand scale awareness, internal consistency across confidence levels, and calibration over a continuum of outcomes, making them a more suitable evaluation format than point estimates for numerical forecasting. To assess this capability, we introduce a new benchmark QuantSightBench, and evaluate frontier models under multiple settings, assessing both empirical coverage and interval sharpness. Our results show that none of the 11 evaluated frontier and open-weight models achieves the 90\% coverage target, with the top performers Gemini 3.1 Pro (79.1\%), Grok 4 (76.4\%), and GPT-5.4 (75.3\%) all falling at least 10 percentage points short. Calibration degrades sharply at extreme magnitudes, revealing systematic overconfidence across all evaluated models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces prediction intervals as a better evaluation format for numerical forecasting in LLMs, creates QuantSightBench benchmark, and finds all tested models fail to achieve target coverage with systematic overconfidence.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluations for forecasting are limited to simple judgmental tasks (binary/multiple-choice), but real-world forecasting requires numerical estimates over continuous quantities across domains like economics, public health, and demographics. Existing benchmarks don't capture this capability or properly evaluate uncertainty.
AI.method: Proposes prediction intervals as evaluation format for numerical forecasting, requiring scale awareness, internal consistency across confidence levels, and calibration over outcome continuum. Introduces QuantSightBench benchmark to assess this capability, evaluating models on empirical coverage and interval sharpness across multiple settings.
AI.result: None of 11 evaluated frontier and open-weight models achieved the 90% coverage target. Top performers: Gemini 3.1 Pro (79.1%), Grok 4 (76.4%), GPT-5.4 (75.3%) - all falling at least 10 percentage points short. Calibration degrades sharply at extreme magnitudes, revealing systematic overconfidence across all models.
AI.conclusion: Prediction intervals provide a rigorous evaluation format for numerical forecasting in LLMs, revealing significant gaps in current models' ability to properly quantify uncertainty. Models show systematic overconfidence, especially at extreme magnitudes, highlighting the need for better uncertainty quantification in forecasting applications.

title: Early Detection of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) Using YOLOv12 Deep Learning Model
summary: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) is one of the most life-threatening type of blood cancers, and its accurate classification is considered and remains a challenging task due to the visual similarity between various cell types. This study addresses the classification of the multiclasses of AML cells Utilizing YOLOv12 deep learning model. We applied two segmentation approaches based on cell and nucleus features, using Hue channel and Otsu thresholding techniques to preprocess the images prior to classification. Our experiments demonstrate that YOLOv12 with Otsu thresholding on cell-based segmentation achieved the highest level of validation and test accuracy, both reaching 99.3%.

AI.tldr: YOLOv12 with Otsu thresholding on cell-based segmentation achieves 99.3% accuracy for multiclass AML cell classification.
AI.motivation: AML is a life-threatening blood cancer where accurate classification is challenging due to visual similarity between cell types. Current methods struggle with distinguishing between different AML cell classes, creating a need for more accurate automated classification systems.
AI.method: Used YOLOv12 deep learning model with two segmentation approaches: cell-based and nucleus-based features. Applied Hue channel and Otsu thresholding techniques for image preprocessing before classification. Compared different segmentation strategies for optimal performance.
AI.result: YOLOv12 with Otsu thresholding on cell-based segmentation achieved the highest performance with both validation and test accuracy reaching 99.3%. This approach outperformed other segmentation methods tested in the study.
AI.conclusion: The proposed YOLOv12 model with Otsu thresholding and cell-based segmentation provides highly accurate multiclass classification of AML cells, demonstrating significant potential for improving AML diagnosis through automated cell analysis.

title: (Weighted) Adaptive Radius Near Neighbor Search: Evaluation for WiFi Fingerprint-based Positioning
comment: 11 pages, 2 figures, 2 tables, submitted to IPIN 2026
summary: Fixed Radius Near Neighbor (FRNN) search is an alternative to the widely used k Nearest Neighbors (kNN) search. Unlike kNN, FRNN determines a label or an estimate for a test sample based on all training samples within a predefined distance. While this approach is beneficial in certain scenarios, assuming a fixed maximum distance for all training samples can decrease the accuracy of the FRNN. Therefore, in this paper we propose the Adaptive Radius Near Neighbor (ARNN) and the Weighted ARNN (WARNN), which employ adaptive distances and in latter case weights. All three methods are compared to kNN and twelve of its variants for a regression problem, namely WiFi fingerprinting indoor positioning, using 22 different datasets to provide a comprehensive analysis. While the performances of the tested FRNN and ARNN versions were amongst the worse, three of the four best methods in the test were WARNN versions, indicating that using weights together with adaptive distances achieves performance comparable or even better than kNN variants.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Adaptive Radius Near Neighbor (ARNN) and Weighted ARNN (WARNN) methods as improvements over Fixed Radius Near Neighbor (FRNN) for regression problems, showing that weighted adaptive radius methods can outperform kNN variants in WiFi fingerprinting indoor positioning.
AI.motivation: Fixed Radius Near Neighbor (FRNN) search uses a predefined distance for all training samples, which can decrease accuracy. The authors aim to improve FRNN by making the radius adaptive and adding weights to achieve better performance than kNN variants.
AI.method: Proposed Adaptive Radius Near Neighbor (ARNN) with adaptive distances and Weighted ARNN (WARNN) with both adaptive distances and weights. Compared these methods against kNN and 12 kNN variants for WiFi fingerprinting indoor positioning regression problem using 22 different datasets.
AI.result: While FRNN and ARNN versions performed poorly (among the worst methods), three of the four best methods in the test were WARNN versions. Weighted adaptive radius methods achieved performance comparable or better than kNN variants.
AI.conclusion: Using weights together with adaptive distances in near neighbor search (WARNN) can achieve superior performance to kNN variants for regression problems like indoor positioning, demonstrating the value of adaptive radius approaches with weighting mechanisms.

title: DINOv3 Beats Specialized Detectors: A Simple Foundation Model Baseline for Image Forensics
summary: With the rapid advancement of deep generative models, realistic fake images have become increasingly accessible, yet existing localization methods rely on complex designs and still struggle to generalize across manipulation types and imaging conditions. We present a simple but strong baseline based on DINOv3 with LoRA adaptation and a lightweight convolutional decoder. Under the CAT-Net protocol, our best model improves average pixel-level F1 by 17.0 points over the previous state of the art on four standard benchmarks using only 9.1\,M trainable parameters on top of a frozen ViT-L backbone, and even our smallest variant surpasses all prior specialized methods. LoRA consistently outperforms full fine-tuning across all backbone scales. Under the data-scarce MVSS-Net protocol, LoRA reaches an average F1 of 0.774 versus 0.530 for the strongest prior method, while full fine-tuning becomes highly unstable, suggesting that pre-trained representations encode forensic information that is better preserved than overwritten. The baseline also exhibits strong robustness to Gaussian noise, JPEG re-compression, and Gaussian blur. We hope this work can serve as a reliable baseline for the research community and a practical starting point for future image-forensic applications. Code is available at https://github.com/Irennnne/DINOv3-IML.

AI.tldr: Simple DINOv3 with LoRA adaptation and lightweight decoder achieves state-of-the-art image manipulation localization with strong generalization across manipulation types and imaging conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing image manipulation localization methods are complex, struggle to generalize across different manipulation types and imaging conditions, and lack reliable baselines for the research community.
AI.method: Uses DINOv3 vision transformer backbone with LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) for parameter-efficient fine-tuning, combined with a lightweight convolutional decoder for pixel-level localization. Two evaluation protocols: CAT-Net and data-scarce MVSS-Net.
AI.result: Achieves 17.0 point average F1 improvement over previous SOTA on four benchmarks with only 9.1M trainable parameters. LoRA consistently outperforms full fine-tuning, reaching 0.774 average F1 vs 0.530 for prior methods under MVSS-Net protocol. Shows strong robustness to noise, JPEG compression, and blur.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a simple yet strong baseline for image manipulation localization, demonstrating that pre-trained representations encode forensic information better preserved than overwritten through parameter-efficient adaptation like LoRA.

title: TwinTrack: Post-hoc Multi-Rater Calibration for Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) segmentation on contrast-enhanced CT is inherently ambiguous: inter-rater disagreement among experts reflects genuine uncertainty rather than annotation noise. Standard deep learning approaches assume a single ground truth, producing probabilistic outputs that can be poorly calibrated and difficult to interpret under such ambiguity. We present TwinTrack, a framework that addresses this gap through post-hoc calibration of ensemble segmentation probabilities to the empirical mean human response (MHR) -the fraction of expert annotators labeling a voxel as tumor. Calibrated probabilities are thus directly interpretable as the expected proportion of annotators assigning the tumor label, explicitly modeling inter-rater disagreement. The proposed post-hoc calibration procedure is simple and requires only a small multi-rater calibration set. It consistently improves calibration metrics over standard approaches when evaluated on the MICCAI 2025 CURVAS-PDACVI multi-rater benchmark.

AI.tldr: TwinTrack is a framework for pancreatic cancer segmentation that calibrates ensemble probabilities to match expert disagreement rates, making outputs interpretable as expected proportion of annotators labeling a voxel as tumor.
AI.motivation: Pancreatic cancer segmentation on CT scans has genuine uncertainty among experts, not just annotation noise. Standard deep learning assumes single ground truth, producing poorly calibrated probabilities that don't reflect real inter-rater disagreement.
AI.method: Post-hoc calibration of ensemble segmentation probabilities to match empirical mean human response (MHR) - the fraction of experts labeling a voxel as tumor. Uses simple calibration procedure requiring only small multi-rater dataset.
AI.result: Consistently improves calibration metrics over standard approaches when evaluated on MICCAI 2025 CURVAS-PDACVI multi-rater benchmark.
AI.conclusion: TwinTrack provides interpretable segmentation probabilities that explicitly model inter-rater disagreement, addressing the inherent ambiguity in pancreatic cancer segmentation through calibrated ensemble outputs.

title: Stylistic-STORM (ST-STORM) : Perceiving the Semantic Nature of Appearance
comment: 20 pages, 16 figures, ICPR 2026 (28th International Conference on Pattern Recognition)
summary: One of the dominant paradigms in self-supervised learning (SSL), illustrated by MoCo or DINO, aims to produce robust representations by capturing features that are insensitive to certain image transformations such as illumination, or geometric changes. This strategy is appropriate when the objective is to recognize objects independently of their appearance. However, it becomes counterproductive as soon as appearance itself constitutes the discriminative signal. In weather analysis, for example, rain streaks, snow granularity, atmospheric scattering, as well as reflections and halos, are not noise: they carry the essential information. In critical applications such as autonomous driving, ignoring these cues is risky, since grip and visibility depend directly on ground conditions and atmospheric conditions. We introduce ST-STORM, a hybrid SSL framework that treats appearance (style) as a semantic modality to be disentangled from content. Our architecture explicitly separates two latent streams, regulated by gating mechanisms. The Content branch aims at a stable semantic representation through a JEPA scheme coupled with a contrastive objective, promoting invariance to appearance variations. In parallel, the Style branch is constrained to capture appearance signatures (textures, contrasts, scattering) through feature prediction and reconstruction under an adversarial constraint. We evaluate ST-STORM on several tasks, including object classification (ImageNet-1K), fine-grained weather characterization, and melanoma detection (ISIC 2024 Challenge). The results show that the Style branch effectively isolates complex appearance phenomena (F1=97% on Multi-Weather and F1=94% on ISIC 2024 with 10% labeled data), without degrading the semantic performance (F1=80% on ImageNet-1K) of the Content branch, and improves the preservation of critical appearance

AI.tldr: ST-STORM is a hybrid SSL framework that disentangles style (appearance) from content, treating appearance as a semantic modality rather than noise, enabling better weather analysis and medical diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Traditional SSL methods like MoCo/DINO aim for invariance to appearance changes, which is problematic when appearance itself is discriminative (e.g., weather phenomena, medical conditions). In applications like autonomous driving, ignoring appearance cues like rain or snow is risky.
AI.method: Architecture with two latent streams: Content branch uses JEPA scheme with contrastive objective for semantic invariance; Style branch captures appearance signatures (textures, contrasts, scattering) through feature prediction and reconstruction with adversarial constraint.
AI.result: Style branch effectively isolates appearance phenomena (F1=97% on Multi-Weather, F1=94% on ISIC 2024 with 10% labeled data) without degrading semantic performance (F1=80% on ImageNet-1K), improving preservation of critical appearance information.
AI.conclusion: ST-STORM successfully disentangles style from content in SSL, enabling applications where appearance is discriminative while maintaining semantic representation quality.

title: Multi-Objective Bayesian Optimization via Adaptive \varepsilon-Constraints Decomposition
summary: Multi-objective Bayesian optimization (MOBO) provides a principled framework for optimizing expensive black-box functions with multiple objectives. However, existing MOBO methods often struggle with coverage, scalability with respect to the number of objectives, and integrating constraints and preferences. In this work, we propose \textit{STAGE-BO, Sequential Targeting Adaptive Gap-Filling $\varepsilon$-Constraint Bayesian Optimization}, that explicitly targets under-explored regions of the Pareto front. By analyzing the coverage of the approximate Pareto front, our method identifies the largest geometric gaps. These gaps are then used as constraints, which transforms the problem into a sequence of inequality-constrained subproblems, efficiently solved via constrained expected improvement acquisition. Our approach provides a uniform Pareto coverage without hypervolume computation and naturally applies to constrained and preference-based settings. Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks demonstrate superior coverage and competitive hypervolume performance against state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: STAGE-BO is a new multi-objective Bayesian optimization method that explicitly targets under-explored regions of the Pareto front by identifying and filling geometric gaps, providing uniform coverage without hypervolume computation.
AI.motivation: Existing MOBO methods struggle with coverage, scalability to many objectives, and integrating constraints/preferences. There's a need for methods that can better explore the Pareto front and handle practical constraints.
AI.method: Sequential Targeting Adaptive Gap-Filling ε-Constraint Bayesian Optimization (STAGE-BO) analyzes Pareto front coverage to identify largest geometric gaps, uses these as constraints to transform into inequality-constrained subproblems, and solves them via constrained expected improvement acquisition.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world benchmarks show superior coverage and competitive hypervolume performance against state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: STAGE-BO provides uniform Pareto coverage without hypervolume computation, naturally applies to constrained/preference-based settings, and addresses key limitations of existing MOBO methods.

title: DenTab: A Dataset for Table Recognition and Visual QA on Real-World Dental Estimates
summary:
Tables condense key transactional and administrative information into compact layouts, but practical extraction requires more than text recognition: systems must also recover structure (rows, columns, merged cells, headers) and interpret roles such as line items, subtotals, and totals under common capture artifacts. Many existing resources for table structure recognition and TableVQA are built from clean digital-born sources or rendered tables, and therefore only partially reflect noisy administrative conditions.
  We introduce DenTab, a dataset of 2{,}000 cropped table images from dental estimates with high-quality HTML annotations, enabling evaluation of table recognition (TR) and table visual question answering (TableVQA) on the same inputs. DenTab includes 2{,}208 questions across eleven categories spanning retrieval, aggregation, and logic/consistency checks. We benchmark 16 systems, including 14 vision--language models (VLMs) and two OCR baselines. Across models, strong structure recovery does not consistently translate into reliable performance on multi-step arithmetic and consistency questions, and these reasoning failures persist even when using ground-truth HTML table inputs.
  To improve arithmetic reliability without training, we propose the Table Router Pipeline, which routes arithmetic questions to deterministic execution. The pipeline combines (i) a VLM that produces a baseline answer, a structured table representation, and a constrained table program with (ii) a rule-based executor that performs exact computation over the parsed table. The source code and dataset will be made publicly available at https://github.com/hamdilaziz/DenTab.

AI.tldr: DenTab: A dataset of 2,000 dental estimate table images with HTML annotations for evaluating table recognition and visual question answering, plus a Table Router Pipeline for improving arithmetic reliability.
AI.motivation: Existing table recognition and VQA datasets are built from clean digital sources and don't reflect real-world noisy administrative conditions, creating a gap in evaluating practical table extraction systems.
AI.method: Created DenTab dataset with 2,000 cropped dental estimate table images and 2,208 questions across 11 categories. Benchmarked 16 systems (14 VLMs + 2 OCR baselines). Proposed Table Router Pipeline that combines VLM outputs with rule-based execution for arithmetic questions.
AI.result: Strong structure recovery doesn't guarantee reliable performance on multi-step arithmetic and consistency questions. Table Router Pipeline improves arithmetic reliability without training by routing questions to deterministic execution.
AI.conclusion: Real-world table extraction requires robust handling of noisy administrative documents. The DenTab dataset enables better evaluation, and the Table Router Pipeline offers a practical approach to improve arithmetic reliability in table VQA systems.

title: Evaluating quality in synthetic data generation for large tabular health datasets
summary: There is no consensus in the field of synthetic data on concise metrics for quality evaluations or benchmarks on large health datasets, such as historical epidemiological data. This study presents an evaluation of seven recent models from major machine learning families. The models were evaluated using four different datasets, each with a distinct scale. To ensure a fair comparison, we systematically tuned the hyperparameters of each model for each dataset. We propose a methodology for evaluating the fidelity of synthesized joint distributions, aligning metrics with visualization on a single plot. This method is applicable to any dataset and is complemented by a domain-specific analysis of the German Cancer Registries' epidemiological dataset. The analysis reveals the challenges models face in strictly adhering to the medical domain. We hope this approach will serve as a foundational framework for guiding the selection of synthesizers and remain accessible to all stakeholders involved in releasing synthetic datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a standardized evaluation framework for synthetic data quality, specifically for large health datasets like epidemiological data, by testing seven ML models across four datasets and introducing a methodology to assess fidelity of joint distributions with visualizations.
AI.motivation: There's no consensus on concise quality metrics or benchmarks for synthetic data in healthcare, particularly for large historical epidemiological datasets. The field lacks standardized evaluation methods for comparing different synthetic data generation models.
AI.method: Evaluated seven recent models from major ML families across four datasets of different scales. Systematically tuned hyperparameters for each model-dataset combination. Proposed a methodology for evaluating fidelity of synthesized joint distributions with aligned metrics and visualizations on a single plot. Applied this to German Cancer Registries' epidemiological dataset for domain-specific analysis.
AI.result: The analysis revealed challenges models face in strictly adhering to medical domain constraints. The proposed evaluation method provides a standardized way to assess synthetic data quality and compare different synthesizers.
AI.conclusion: The approach serves as a foundational framework for guiding synthesizer selection and remains accessible to all stakeholders involved in releasing synthetic datasets, addressing the current lack of standardized evaluation in synthetic health data.

title: Polyglot: Multilingual Style Preserving Speech-Driven Facial Animation
comment: The project website is available at https://fedenoce.github.io/polyglot/
summary: Speech-Driven Facial Animation (SDFA) has gained significant attention due to its applications in movies, video games, and virtual reality. However, most existing models are trained on single-language data, limiting their effectiveness in real-world multilingual scenarios. In this work, we address multilingual SDFA, which is essential for realistic generation since language influences phonetics, rhythm, intonation, and facial expressions. Speaking style is also shaped by individual differences, not only by language. Existing methods typically rely on either language-specific or speaker-specific conditioning, but not both, limiting their ability to model their interaction. We introduce Polyglot, a unified diffusion-based architecture for personalized multilingual SDFA. Our method uses transcript embeddings to encode language information and style embeddings extracted from reference facial sequences to capture individual speaking characteristics. Polyglot does not require predefined language or speaker labels, enabling generalization across languages and speakers through self-supervised learning. By jointly conditioning on language and style, it captures expressive traits such as rhythm, articulation, and habitual facial movements, producing temporally coherent and realistic animations. Experiments show improved performance in both monolingual and multilingual settings, providing a unified framework for modeling language and personal style in SDFA.

AI.tldr: Polyglot is a diffusion-based model for multilingual speech-driven facial animation that jointly conditions on language and personal speaking style without requiring predefined labels.
AI.motivation: Most existing SDFA models are trained on single-language data, limiting effectiveness in real-world multilingual scenarios. Language influences phonetics, rhythm, intonation, and facial expressions, while speaking style is also shaped by individual differences. Existing methods typically rely on either language-specific or speaker-specific conditioning, but not both, limiting their ability to model their interaction.
AI.method: Introduces Polyglot, a unified diffusion-based architecture that uses transcript embeddings to encode language information and style embeddings extracted from reference facial sequences to capture individual speaking characteristics. The method does not require predefined language or speaker labels, enabling generalization across languages and speakers through self-supervised learning.
AI.result: Experiments show improved performance in both monolingual and multilingual settings. The model captures expressive traits such as rhythm, articulation, and habitual facial movements, producing temporally coherent and realistic animations.
AI.conclusion: Polyglot provides a unified framework for modeling both language and personal style in speech-driven facial animation, addressing the limitations of existing single-language or single-speaker approaches.

title: Impact of Nonlinear Power Amplifier on Massive MIMO: Machine Learning Prediction Under Realistic Radio Channel
comment: Accepted for publication in IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
summary: M-MIMO is one of the crucial technologies for increasing spectral and energy efficiency of wireless networks. Most of the current works assume that M-MIMO arrays are equipped with a linear front end. However, ongoing efforts to make wireless networks more energy-efficient push the hardware to the limits, where its nonlinear behavior appears. This is especially a common problem for the multicarrier systems, e.g., OFDM used in 4G, 5G, and possibly also in 6G, which is characterized by a high Peak-to-Average Power Ratio. While the impact of a nonlinear Power Amplifier (PA) on an OFDM signal is well characterized, it is a relatively new topic for the M-MIMO OFDM systems. Most of the recent works either neglect nonlinear effects or utilize simplified models proper for Rayleigh or LoS radio channel models. In this paper, we first theoretically characterize the nonlinear distortion in the M-MIMO system under commonly used radio channel models. Then, utilizing 3D-Ray Tracing (3D-RT) software, we demonstrate that these models are not very accurate. Instead, we propose two models: a statistical one and an ML-based one using 3D-RT results. The proposed statistical model utilizes the Generalized Extreme Value (GEV) distribution to model Signal to Distortion Ratio (SDR) for victim users, receiving nonlinear distortion, e.g., as interference from neighboring cells. The proposed ML model aims to predict SDR for a scheduled user (receiving nonlinear distortion along with the desired signal), based on the spatial characteristics of the radio channel and the operation point of each PA feeding at the M-MIMO antenna array. The predicted SDR can then be used to perform PA-aware per-user power allocation. The results show about 12% median gain in user throughput achieved by the proposed ML-based power allocation scheme over the state-of-the-art, fixed operating point scheme.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes nonlinear distortion in Massive MIMO OFDM systems, proposes statistical and ML-based models for Signal-to-Distortion Ratio prediction, and demonstrates 12% median throughput gain with ML-based power allocation.
AI.motivation: Current M-MIMO research often assumes linear front ends, but energy efficiency pushes hardware to nonlinear operation, especially problematic for OFDM systems with high PAPR. Existing models neglect nonlinear effects or use simplified channel models that are inaccurate for real-world scenarios.
AI.method: 1) Theoretical characterization of nonlinear distortion in M-MIMO under common channel models; 2) Use of 3D-Ray Tracing software to validate models; 3) Proposal of two models: statistical model using Generalized Extreme Value distribution for victim users, and ML-based model for scheduled users using spatial channel characteristics and PA operation points; 4) ML-based power allocation scheme using predicted SDR.
AI.result: The paper shows that conventional channel models are inaccurate for modeling nonlinear distortion. The proposed ML-based power allocation scheme achieves about 12% median gain in user throughput compared to state-of-the-art fixed operating point schemes.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the nonlinear distortion problem in M-MIMO OFDM systems by proposing accurate statistical and ML-based models for SDR prediction, enabling PA-aware power allocation that significantly improves system throughput performance.

title: Towards In-Context Tone Style Transfer with A Large-Scale Triplet Dataset
summary: Tone style transfer for photo retouching aims to adapt the stylistic tone of the reference image to a given content image. However, the lack of high-quality large-scale triplet datasets with stylized ground truth forces existing methods to rely on self-supervised or proxy objectives, which limits model capability. To mitigate this gap, we design a data construction pipeline to build TST100K, a large-scale dataset of 100,000 content-reference-stylized triplets. At the core of this pipeline, we train a tone style scorer to ensure strict stylistic consistency for each triplet. In addition, existing methods typically extract content and reference features independently and then fuse them in a decoder, which may cause semantic loss and lead to inappropriate color transfer and degraded visual aesthetics. Instead, we propose ICTone, a diffusion-based framework that performs tone transfer in an in-context manner by jointly conditioning on both images, leveraging the semantic priors of generative models for semantic-aware transfer. Reward feedback learning using the tone style scorer is further incorporated to improve stylistic fidelity and visual quality. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of TST100K, and ICTone achieves state-of-the-art performance on both quantitative metrics and human evaluations.

AI.tldr: ICTone: A diffusion-based framework for photo retouching tone style transfer using a new large-scale dataset TST100K and in-context conditioning with reward feedback learning.
AI.motivation: Existing tone style transfer methods suffer from lack of high-quality triplet datasets and semantic loss due to independent feature extraction, leading to inappropriate color transfer and degraded aesthetics.
AI.method: 1) Build TST100K dataset with 100K content-reference-stylized triplets using a tone style scorer for consistency. 2) ICTone diffusion framework performs in-context conditioning on both images. 3) Reward feedback learning with tone style scorer improves stylistic fidelity.
AI.result: ICTone achieves state-of-the-art performance on both quantitative metrics and human evaluations, demonstrating effectiveness of TST100K dataset and the proposed framework.
AI.conclusion: The proposed large-scale dataset TST100K and ICTone framework with in-context diffusion conditioning and reward learning effectively address limitations of existing tone style transfer methods.

title: Corner Reflector Array Jamming Discrimination Using Multi-Dimensional Micro-Motion Features with Frequency Agile Radar
summary: This paper introduces a robust discrimination method for distinguishing real ship targets from corner-reflector-array jamming with frequency-agile radar. The key idea is to exploit the multidimensional micro-motion signatures that separate rigid ships from non-rigid decoys. From Range-Velocity maps we derive two new hand-crafted descriptors-mean weighted residual (MWR) and complementary contrast factor (CCF) and fuse them with deep features learned by a lightweight CNN. An XGBoost classifier then gives the final decision. Extensive simulations show that the hybrid feature set consistently outperforms state-of-the-art alternatives, confirming the superiority of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: Hybrid feature approach combining hand-crafted micro-motion descriptors (MWR and CCF) with CNN-learned features for robust ship vs. decoy discrimination using frequency-agile radar.
AI.motivation: Need to distinguish real ship targets from corner-reflector-array jamming (decoys) in frequency-agile radar systems, where traditional discrimination methods may fail.
AI.method: Extract micro-motion signatures from Range-Velocity maps, create two hand-crafted descriptors (MWR and CCF), combine with deep features from lightweight CNN, and use XGBoost classifier for final decision.
AI.result: Hybrid feature set consistently outperforms state-of-the-art alternatives in extensive simulations, demonstrating superior discrimination performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach effectively leverages both hand-crafted micro-motion features and deep learning capabilities for robust ship target discrimination against sophisticated decoy jamming.

title: From Articles to Canopies: Knowledge-Driven Pseudo-Labelling for Tree Species Classification using LLM Experts
summary: Hyperspectral tree species classification is challenging due to limited and imbalanced class labels, spectral mixing (overlapping light signatures from multiple species), and ecological heterogeneity (variability among ecological systems). Addressing these challenges requires methods that integrate biological and structural characteristics of vegetation, such as canopy architecture and interspecific interactions, rather than relying solely on spectral signatures. This paper presents a biologically informed, semi-supervised deep learning method that integrates multi-sensor Earth observation data, specifically hyperspectral imaging (HSI) and airborne laser scanning (ALS), with expert, ecological knowledge. The approach relies on biologically inspired pseudo-labelling over a precomputed canopy graph, yielding accurate classification at low training cost. In addition, ecological priors on species cohabitation are automatically derived from reliable sources using large language models (LLMs) and encoded as a cohabitation matrix with likelihoods of species occurring together. These priors are incorporated into the pseudo-labelling strategy, effectively introducing expert knowledge into the model. Experiments on a real-world forest dataset demonstrate 5.6% improvement over the best reference method. Expert evaluation of cohabitation priors reveals high accuracy with differences no larger than 15%.

AI.tldr: Biologically-informed semi-supervised deep learning method combines hyperspectral imaging, airborne laser scanning, and ecological knowledge via pseudo-labelling and LLM-derived cohabitation priors for improved tree species classification.
AI.motivation: Hyperspectral tree species classification faces challenges: limited/imbalanced labels, spectral mixing (overlapping signatures), and ecological heterogeneity. Need methods integrating biological/structural characteristics beyond just spectral data.
AI.method: Semi-supervised deep learning integrating multi-sensor data (HSI + ALS) with ecological knowledge. Uses biologically inspired pseudo-labelling over canopy graph.
