Automatically derives ecological priors on species cohabitation using LLMs, encoded as cohabitation matrix with likelihoods of species occurring together.
AI.result: 5.6% improvement over best reference method on real-world forest dataset. Expert evaluation shows cohabitation priors have high accuracy with differences no larger than 15%.
AI.conclusion: Integrating multi-sensor data with biologically informed pseudo-labelling and LLM-derived ecological priors effectively addresses challenges in tree species classification, achieving significant accuracy improvements with low training cost.

title: AEGIS: Anchor-Enforced Gradient Isolation for Knowledge-Preserving Vision-Language-Action Fine-Tuning
summary: Adapting pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) for robotic control requires injecting high-magnitude continuous gradients from a flow-matching action expert into a backbone trained exclusively with cross-entropy. This cross-modal gradient asymmetry - the spectral dimensionality mismatch between low-rank MSE regression gradients and the high-dimensional semantic manifold sculpted by CE pre-training, causes rapid, severe erosion of the VLM's visual-question-answering (VQA) capability. Industry-standard defences either sever the gradient pathway entirely via stop gradient, discarding the rich continuous supervision, or restrict parameter capacity through low-rank adapters (LoRA) that constrain the rank of updates but not their direction, and thus still overwrite the pre-trained manifold. We introduce AEGIS (Anchor-Enforced Gradient Isolation System): a buffer-free, layer-wise orthogonal gradient projection framework that enables direct continuous MSE learning while preserving the pre-trained VQA manifold - without any co-training data or replay buffer. AEGIS pre-computes a static Gaussian reference anchor from masked VQA forward passes across all transformer layers, then at each training step constructs a Wasserstein-2 transport penalty that generates an anchor restoration gradient. A sequential dual-backward decomposes the task and anchor gradients; for each transformer layer, AEGIS applies a single Gram-Schmidt orthogonal projection that bends the task gradient away from the destructive direction while preserving its constructive content. The projection sheds less than 1% of gradient energy on average, yet eliminates the cumulative activation drift that drives severe forgetting.

AI.tldr: AEGIS prevents forgetting in vision-language models during robotic control adaptation by projecting destructive gradients away from the pre-trained manifold while preserving useful learning signals.
AI.motivation: Current methods for adapting VLMs to robotic control either discard continuous supervision (stop gradient) or still overwrite the pre-trained manifold (LoRA), causing severe erosion of VQA capabilities due to cross-modal gradient asymmetry.
AI.method: AEGIS uses layer-wise orthogonal gradient projection: pre-computes static Gaussian reference anchors from masked VQA passes, constructs Wasserstein-2 transport penalty for anchor restoration, and applies Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization to bend destructive task gradients away from the pre-trained manifold while preserving constructive content.
AI.result: The method eliminates cumulative activation drift that drives severe forgetting while shedding less than 1% of gradient energy on average, enabling direct continuous MSE learning without co-training data or replay buffers.
AI.conclusion: AEGIS provides a buffer-free solution to preserve VQA capabilities during robotic control adaptation by isolating destructive gradients while maintaining useful learning signals through orthogonal projection.

title: Motion-Adapter: A Diffusion Model Adapter for Text-to-Motion Generation of Compound Actions
comment: 12 pages, 12 figures, Under review for publication in IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
summary: Recent advances in generative motion synthesis have enabled the production of realistic human motions from diverse input modalities. However, synthesizing compound actions from texts, which integrate multiple concurrent actions into coherent full-body sequences, remains a major challenge. We identify two key limitations in current text-to-motion diffusion models: (i) catastrophic neglect, where earlier actions are overwritten by later ones due to improper handling of temporal information, and (ii) attention collapse, which arises from excessive feature fusion in cross-attention mechanisms. As a result, existing approaches often depend on overly detailed textual descriptions (e.g., raising right hand), explicit body-part specifications (e.g., editing the upper body), or the use of large language models (LLMs) for body-part interpretation. These strategies lead to deficient semantic representations of physical structures and kinematic mechanisms, limiting the ability to incorporate natural behaviors such as greeting while walking. To address these issues, we propose the Motion-Adapter, a plug-and-play module that guides text-to-motion diffusion models in generating compound actions by computing decoupled cross-attention maps, which serve as structural masks during the denoising process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method consistently produces more faithful and coherent compound motions across diverse textual prompts, surpassing state-of-the-art approaches.

AI.tldr: Motion-Adapter: A plug-and-play module for text-to-motion diffusion models that generates coherent compound actions by computing decoupled cross-attention maps as structural masks during denoising.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-motion diffusion models struggle with synthesizing compound actions (multiple concurrent actions) due to catastrophic neglect (earlier actions overwritten by later ones) and attention collapse (excessive feature fusion in cross-attention). Existing approaches require overly detailed descriptions, explicit body-part specifications, or LLM assistance, leading to deficient semantic representations of physical structures and kinematic mechanisms.
AI.method: Proposes Motion-Adapter, a plug-and-play module that guides text-to-motion diffusion models by computing decoupled cross-attention maps that serve as structural masks during the denoising process, enabling better handling of compound actions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the method consistently produces more faithful and coherent compound motions across diverse textual prompts, surpassing state-of-the-art approaches.
AI.conclusion: Motion-Adapter effectively addresses the limitations of current text-to-motion diffusion models for compound action synthesis by providing structural guidance through decoupled cross-attention maps, enabling more natural and coherent motion generation from textual descriptions.

title: Prototype-Grounded Concept Models for Verifiable Concept Alignment
summary: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) aim to improve interpretability in Deep Learning by structuring predictions through human-understandable concepts, but they provide no way to verify whether learned concepts align with the human's intended meaning, hurting interpretability. We introduce Prototype-Grounded Concept Models (PGCMs), which ground concepts in learned visual prototypes: image parts that serve as explicit evidence for the concepts. This grounding enables direct inspection of concept semantics and supports targeted human intervention at the prototype level to correct misalignments. Empirically, PGCMs match the predictive performance of state-of-the-art CBMs while substantially improving transparency, interpretability, and intervenability.

AI.tldr: PGCMs improve CBMs by grounding concepts in visual prototypes for better interpretability and human intervention.
AI.motivation: Current Concept Bottleneck Models lack verification that learned concepts align with human understanding, which undermines their interpretability.
AI.method: Introduce Prototype-Grounded Concept Models that ground concepts in learned visual prototypes (image parts) that serve as explicit evidence for concepts.
AI.result: PGCMs match predictive performance of state-of-the-art CBMs while substantially improving transparency, interpretability, and intervenability.
AI.conclusion: Grounding concepts in visual prototypes enables direct inspection of concept semantics and supports targeted human intervention to correct misalignments.

title: SWNet: A Cross-Spectral Network for Camouflaged Weed Detection
summary: This paper presents SWNet, a bimodal end-to-end cross-spectral network specifically engineered for the detection of camouflaged weeds in dense agricultural environments. Plant camouflage, characterized by homochromatic blending where invasive species mimic the phenotypic traits of primary crops, poses a significant challenge for traditional computer vision systems. To overcome these limitations, SWNet utilizes a Pyramid Vision Transformer v2 backbone to capture long-range dependencies and a Bimodal Gated Fusion Module to dynamically integrate Visible and Near-Infrared information. By leveraging the physiological differences in chlorophyll reflectance captured in the NIR spectrum, the proposed architecture effectively discriminates targets that are otherwise indistinguishable in the visible range. Furthermore, an Edge-Aware Refinement module is employed to produce sharper object boundaries and reduce structural ambiguity. Experimental results on the Weeds-Banana dataset indicate that SWNet outperforms ten state-of-the-art methods. The study demonstrates that the integration of cross-spectral data and boundary-guided refinement is essential for high segmentation accuracy in complex crop canopies. The code is available on GitHub: https://cod-espol.github.io/SWNet/

AI.tldr: SWNet is a bimodal end-to-end cross-spectral network for detecting camouflaged weeds in dense agricultural environments using Visible and Near-Infrared data with transformer backbone and fusion modules.
AI.motivation: Plant camouflage where invasive species mimic primary crops poses significant challenges for traditional computer vision systems in agriculture, requiring advanced methods to distinguish weeds from crops.
AI.method: Uses Pyramid Vision Transformer v2 backbone for long-range dependencies, Bimodal Gated Fusion Module to integrate Visible and NIR data, and Edge-Aware Refinement module for sharper object boundaries.
AI.result: Outperforms ten state-of-the-art methods on the Weeds-Banana dataset, demonstrating superior segmentation accuracy for camouflaged weed detection.
AI.conclusion: Integration of cross-spectral data and boundary-guided refinement is essential for high segmentation accuracy in complex crop canopies, enabling effective weed detection despite camouflage.

title: Unveiling Stochasticity: Universal Multi-modal Probabilistic Modeling for Traffic Forecasting
summary: Traffic forecasting is a challenging spatio-temporal modeling task and a critical component of urban transportation management. Current studies mainly focus on deterministic predictions, with limited considerations on the uncertainty and stochasticity in traffic dynamics. Therefore, this paper proposes an elegant yet universal approach that transforms existing models into probabilistic predictors by replacing only the final output layer with a novel Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) layer. The modified model requires no changes to the training pipeline and can be trained using only the Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) loss, without any auxiliary or regularization terms. Experiments on multiple traffic datasets show that our approach generalizes from classic to modern model architectures while preserving deterministic performance. Furthermore, we propose a systematic evaluation procedure based on cumulative distributions and confidence intervals, and demonstrate that our approach is considerably more accurate and informative than unimodal or deterministic baselines. Finally, a more detailed study on a real-world dense urban traffic network is presented to examine the impact of data quality on uncertainty quantification and to show the robustness of our approach under imperfect data conditions. Code available at https://github.com/Weijiang-Xiong/OpenSkyTraffic

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a universal approach to transform existing traffic forecasting models into probabilistic predictors by replacing the final output layer with a Gaussian Mixture Model layer, requiring minimal changes while improving uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: Current traffic forecasting studies mainly focus on deterministic predictions with limited consideration of uncertainty and stochasticity in traffic dynamics, creating a need for better probabilistic forecasting methods.
AI.method: Transform existing models into probabilistic predictors by replacing only the final output layer with a novel Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) layer, trained using only Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) loss without auxiliary or regularization terms.
AI.result: The approach generalizes from classic to modern model architectures while preserving deterministic performance, shows superior accuracy and informativeness compared to unimodal/deterministic baselines, and demonstrates robustness under imperfect data conditions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GMM layer provides an elegant, universal solution for probabilistic traffic forecasting that requires minimal changes to existing models, improves uncertainty quantification, and maintains performance across various architectures and data conditions.

title: neuralCAD-Edit: An Expert Benchmark for Multimodal-Instructed 3D CAD Model Editing
summary: We introduce neuralCAD-Edit, the first benchmark for editing 3D CAD models collected from expert CAD engineers. Instead of text conditioning as in prior works, we collect realistic CAD editing requests by capturing videos of professional designers, interacting directly with CAD models in CAD software, while talking, pointing and drawing. We recruited ten consenting designers to contribute to this contained study. We benchmark leading foundation models against human CAD experts carrying out edits, and find a large performance gap in both automatic metrics and human evaluations. Even the best foundation model (GPT 5.2) scores 53% lower (absolute) than CAD experts in human acceptance trials, demonstrating the challenge of neuralCAD-Edit. We hope neuralCAD-Edit will provide a solid foundation against which 3D CAD editing approaches and foundation models can be developed. Code/data: https://autodeskailab.github.io/neuralCAD-Edit

AI.tldr: NeuralCAD-Edit is the first benchmark for 3D CAD model editing collected from expert CAD engineers, featuring realistic editing requests captured from professional designers' interactions rather than text conditioning.
AI.motivation: Prior works rely on text conditioning for 3D CAD editing, which doesn't capture realistic CAD editing workflows. There's a need for a benchmark that reflects how professional designers actually interact with CAD software through talking, pointing, and drawing.
AI.method: Collected realistic CAD editing requests by capturing videos of 10 professional CAD designers interacting directly with CAD models in CAD software while talking, pointing, and drawing. Created a benchmark dataset from these expert interactions.
AI.result: Benchmarking shows a large performance gap between leading foundation models (including GPT 5.2) and human CAD experts. The best foundation model scores 53% lower than CAD experts in human acceptance trials.
AI.conclusion: NeuralCAD-Edit demonstrates the significant challenge of 3D CAD editing and provides a solid foundation for developing better 3D CAD editing approaches and foundation models.

title: The Harder Path: Last Iterate Convergence for Uncoupled Learning in Zero-Sum Games with Bandit Feedback
comment: Accepted at the 42nd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2025)
summary: We study the problem of learning in zero-sum matrix games with repeated play and bandit feedback. Specifically, we focus on developing uncoupled algorithms that guarantee, without communication between players, the convergence of the last-iterate to a Nash equilibrium. Although the non-bandit case has been studied extensively, this setting has only been explored recently, with a bound of $\mathcal{O}(T^{-1/8})$ on the exploitability gap. We show that, for uncoupled algorithms, guaranteeing convergence of the policy profiles to a Nash equilibrium is detrimental to the performance, with the best attainable rate being $Ω(T^{-1/4})$ in contrast to the usual $Ω(T^{-1/2})$ rate for convergence of the average iterates. We then propose two algorithms that achieve this optimal rate up to constant and logarithmic factors. The first algorithm leverages a straightforward trade-off between exploration and exploitation, while the second employs a regularization technique based on a two-step mirror descent approach.

AI.tldr: The paper studies uncoupled algorithms for zero-sum matrix games with bandit feedback, showing that last-iterate convergence to Nash equilibrium has a fundamental Ω(T^{-1/4}) lower bound (worse than the Ω(T^{-1/2}) rate for average iterates), and proposes two optimal algorithms achieving this rate.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand the fundamental limitations of uncoupled algorithms in zero-sum matrix games with bandit feedback, particularly focusing on last-iterate convergence to Nash equilibrium. Previous work showed a bound of O(T^{-1/8}), but the authors want to determine the optimal achievable rate and whether the requirement for last-iterate convergence imposes additional constraints compared to average-iterate convergence.
AI.method: The authors first establish a fundamental lower bound of Ω(T^{-1/4}) for uncoupled algorithms with last-iterate convergence. They then propose two algorithms: 1) A straightforward exploration-exploitation trade-off algorithm, and 2) A regularization-based algorithm using a two-step mirror descent approach. Both algorithms achieve the optimal rate up to constant and logarithmic factors.
AI.result: The main results are: 1) A fundamental lower bound showing that for uncoupled algorithms, last-iterate convergence to Nash equilibrium must have rate Ω(T^{-1/4}), which is worse than the Ω(T^{-1/2}) rate achievable for average-iterate convergence. 2) Two algorithms that achieve this optimal rate up to constant and logarithmic factors, matching the lower bound.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that requiring last-iterate convergence to Nash equilibrium in uncoupled algorithms for zero-sum matrix games with bandit feedback imposes a fundamental performance limitation, with the best achievable rate being Ω(T^{-1/4}) rather than the Ω(T^{-1/2}) rate possible for average-iterate convergence. The proposed algorithms achieve this optimal rate, providing a complete characterization of the problem.

title: Winner of CVPR2026 NTIRE Challenge on Image Shadow Removal: Semantic and Geometric Guidance for Shadow Removal via Cascaded Refinement
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures, 5 tables, accepted at the CVPR 2026 Workshops (NTIRE 2026 Image Shadow Removal Challenge). Code and materials are available at https://github.com/AIT-Assistive-Autonomous-Systems/SGCR-SR
summary: We present a three-stage progressive shadow-removal pipeline for the CVPR2026 NTIRE WSRD+ challenge. Built on OmniSR, our method treats deshadowing as iterative direct refinement, where later stages correct residual artefacts left by earlier predictions. The model combines RGB appearance with frozen DINOv2 semantic guidance and geometric cues from monocular depth and surface normals, reused across all stages. To stabilise multi-stage optimisation, we introduce a contraction-constrained objective that encourages non-increasing reconstruction error across the cascade. A staged training pipeline transfers from earlier WSRD pretraining to WSRD+ supervision and final WSRD+ 2026 adaptation with cosine-annealed checkpoint ensembling. On the official WSRD+ 2026 hidden test set, our final ensemble achieved 26.680 PSNR, 0.8740 SSIM, 0.0578 LPIPS, and 26.135 FID, ranked first overall, and won the NTIRE 2026 Image Shadow Removal Challenge. The strong performance of the proposed model is further validated on the ISTD+ and UAV-SC+ datasets.

AI.tldr: A three-stage progressive shadow removal pipeline won the NTIRE 2026 challenge by iteratively refining predictions with semantic and geometric guidance, using contraction-constrained optimization and staged training.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of shadow removal in images by developing a robust multi-stage approach that can handle residual artifacts and leverage semantic understanding for better performance in the NTIRE WSRD+ competition.
AI.method: Three-stage progressive pipeline based on OmniSR that treats deshadowing as iterative direct refinement. Combines RGB appearance with frozen DINOv2 semantic guidance and geometric cues from monocular depth and surface normals. Uses contraction-constrained objective to stabilize multi-stage optimization and staged training with cosine-annealed checkpoint ensembling.
AI.result: Achieved 26.680 PSNR, 0.8740 SSIM, 0.0578 LPIPS, and 26.135 FID on WSRD+ 2026 hidden test set, ranked first overall and won the NTIRE 2026 Image Shadow Removal Challenge. Also validated on ISTD+ and UAV-SC+ datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed progressive shadow removal pipeline with semantic guidance and contraction-constrained optimization is highly effective, achieving state-of-the-art performance and winning the NTIRE 2026 challenge.

title: Sample Complexity Bounds for Stochastic Shortest Path with a Generative Model
comment: Accepted at the 32nd International Conference on Algorithmic Learning Theory (ALT 2021)
summary: We study the sample complexity of learning an $ε$-optimal policy in the Stochastic Shortest Path (SSP) problem. We first derive sample complexity bounds when the learner has access to a generative model. We show that there exists a worst-case SSP instance with $S$ states, $A$ actions, minimum cost $c_{\min}$, and maximum expected cost of the optimal policy over all states $B_{\star}$, where any algorithm requires at least $Ω(SAB_{\star}^3/(c_{\min}ε^2))$ samples to return an $ε$-optimal policy with high probability. Surprisingly, this implies that whenever $c_{\min} = 0$ an SSP problem may not be learnable, thus revealing that learning in SSPs is strictly harder than in the finite-horizon and discounted settings. We complement this lower bound with an algorithm that matches it, up to logarithmic factors, in the general case, and an algorithm that matches it up to logarithmic factors even when $c_{\min} = 0$, but only under the condition that the optimal policy has a bounded hitting time to the goal state.

AI.tldr: The paper establishes fundamental sample complexity bounds for learning optimal policies in Stochastic Shortest Path (SSP) problems, revealing SSP learning is harder than other RL settings and providing matching algorithms.
AI.motivation: To understand the fundamental difficulty of learning in Stochastic Shortest Path problems compared to finite-horizon and discounted RL settings, and to establish tight sample complexity bounds for learning ε-optimal policies.
AI.method: Derived lower bounds through worst-case SSP instance analysis, then developed matching algorithms: one general algorithm for all cases and another specialized algorithm for cases with c_min=0 but bounded hitting time to goal.
AI.result: Proved lower bound of Ω(SAB⋆³/(c_minε²)) samples needed for ε-optimal policy, showing SSP learning is strictly harder than other settings (especially when c_min=0). Provided algorithms matching this bound up to logarithmic factors.
AI.conclusion: SSP learning has fundamentally different complexity than finite-horizon/discounted RL, with c_min=0 making problems potentially unlearnable unless optimal policy has bounded hitting time to goal, establishing tight sample complexity bounds.

title: Saturation-Aware Space-Variant Blind Image Deblurring
summary: This paper presents a novel saturation aware space variant blind image deblurring framework designed to address challenges posed by saturated pixels in deblurring under high dynamic range and low light conditions. The proposed approach effectively segments the image based on blur intensity and proximity to saturation, leveraging a pre estimated Light Spread Function to mitigate stray light effects. By accurately estimating the true radiance of saturated regions using the dark channel prior, our method enhances the deblurring process without introducing artifacts like ringing. Experimental evaluations on both synthetic and real world datasets demonstrate that the framework improves deblurring outcomes across various scenarios showcasing superior performance compared to state of the art saturation-aware and general purpose methods. This adaptability highlights the framework potential integration with existing and emerging blind image deblurring techniques.

AI.tldr: Novel saturation-aware space-variant blind image deblurring framework that handles saturated pixels in high dynamic range/low light conditions using blur intensity segmentation and dark channel prior for accurate radiance estimation.
AI.motivation: Address challenges posed by saturated pixels in deblurring under high dynamic range and low light conditions, where traditional methods fail due to artifacts like ringing and inaccurate handling of saturated regions.
AI.method: Segments image based on blur intensity and proximity to saturation, leverages pre-estimated Light Spread Function to mitigate stray light effects, and uses dark channel prior to accurately estimate true radiance of saturated regions.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations on synthetic and real-world datasets show improved deblurring outcomes across various scenarios, with superior performance compared to state-of-the-art saturation-aware and general-purpose methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively handles saturated pixels in challenging conditions without introducing artifacts, demonstrating adaptability for integration with existing and emerging blind image deblurring techniques.

title: SCRIPT: Implementing an Intelligent Tutoring System for Programming in a German University Context
comment: In: Cristea, A.I., Walker, E., Lu, Y., Santos, O.C., Isotani, S. (eds) Artificial Intelligence in Education. Posters and Late Breaking Results, Workshops and Tutorials, Industry and Innovation Tracks, Practitioners, Doctoral Consortium, Blue Sky, and WideAIED. AIED 2025. Communications in Computer and Information Science, vol 2590 . Springer, Cham
summary: Practice and extensive exercises are essential in programming education. Intelligent tutoring systems (ITSs) are a viable option to provide individualized hints and advice to programming students even when human tutors are not available. However, prior ITS for programming rarely support the Python programming language, mostly focus on introductory programming, and rarely take recent developments in generative models into account. We aim to establish a novel ITS for Python programming that is highly adaptable, serves both as a teaching and research platform, provides interfaces to plug in hint mechanisms (e.g.\ via large language models), and works inside the particularly challenging regulatory environment of Germany, that is, conforming to the European data protection regulation, the European AI act, and ethical framework of the German Research Foundation. In this paper, we present the description of the current state of the ITS along with future development directions, as well as discuss the challenges and opportunities for improving the system.

AI.tldr: A novel intelligent tutoring system for Python programming that addresses gaps in existing ITS by supporting Python, going beyond introductory levels, incorporating generative models, and complying with strict European/German regulations.
AI.motivation: Existing ITS for programming rarely support Python, mostly focus on introductory programming, don't incorporate recent generative models, and lack compliance with European/German regulatory frameworks (GDPR, AI Act, DFG ethics).
AI.method: Developing a highly adaptable ITS platform that serves both teaching and research purposes, provides interfaces for plug-in hint mechanisms (including large language models), and ensures compliance with European data protection, AI regulations, and ethical frameworks.
AI.result: Presents the current state of the ITS development along with future directions, discussing challenges and opportunities for improving the system.
AI.conclusion: The paper describes work-in-progress on establishing a novel Python programming ITS that addresses multiple gaps in existing systems while navigating complex regulatory environments, with potential to significantly enhance programming education.

title: AIFIND: Artifact-Aware Interpreting Fine-Grained Alignment for Incremental Face Forgery Detection
summary: As forgery types continue to emerge consistently, Incremental Face Forgery Detection (IFFD) has become a crucial paradigm. However, existing methods typically rely on data replay or coarse binary supervision, which fails to explicitly constrain the feature space, leading to severe feature drift and catastrophic forgetting. To address this, we propose AIFIND, Artifact-Aware Interpreting Fine-Grained Alignment for Incremental Face Forgery Detection, which leverages semantic anchors to stabilize incremental learning. We design the Artifact-Driven Semantic Prior Generator to instantiate invariant semantic anchors, establishing a fixed coordinate system from low-level artifact cues. These anchors are injected into the image encoder via Artifact-Probe Attention, which explicitly constrains volatile visual features to align with stable semantic anchors. Adaptive Decision Harmonizer harmonizes the classifiers by preserving angular relationships of semantic anchors, maintaining geometric consistency across tasks. Extensive experiments on multiple incremental protocols validate the superiority of AIFIND.

AI.tldr: AIFIND uses artifact-aware semantic anchors to stabilize incremental face forgery detection, preventing feature drift and catastrophic forgetting through fine-grained alignment.
AI.motivation: Existing incremental face forgery detection methods rely on data replay or binary supervision, which fail to explicitly constrain feature space, leading to severe feature drift and catastrophic forgetting as new forgery types emerge.
AI.method: Proposes AIFIND with three key components: 1) Artifact-Driven Semantic Prior Generator creates invariant semantic anchors from low-level artifact cues; 2) Artifact-Probe Attention injects anchors into image encoder to align volatile visual features; 3) Adaptive Decision Harmonizer preserves angular relationships of semantic anchors to maintain geometric consistency across tasks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple incremental protocols validate the superiority of AIFIND over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: AIFIND effectively addresses feature drift and catastrophic forgetting in incremental face forgery detection by leveraging semantic anchors for stable feature alignment across emerging forgery types.

title: Univariate Channel Fusion for Multivariate Time Series Classification
summary: Multivariate time series classification (MTSC) plays a crucial role in various domains, including biomedical signal analysis and motion monitoring. However, existing approaches, particularly deep learning models, often require high computational resources, making them unsuitable for real-time applications or deployment on low-cost hardware, such as IoT devices and wearable systems. In this paper, we propose the Univariate Channel Fusion (UCF) method to deal with MTSC efficiently. UCF transforms multivariate time series into a univariate representation through simple channel fusion strategies such as the mean, median, or dynamic time warping barycenter. This transformation enables the use of any classifier originally designed for univariate time series, providing a flexible and computationally lightweight alternative to complex models. We evaluate UCF in five case studies covering diverse application domains, including chemical monitoring, brain-computer interfaces, and human activity analysis. The results demonstrate that UCF often outperforms baseline methods and state-of-the-art algorithms tailored for MTSC, while achieving substantial gains in computational efficiency, being particularly effective in problems with high inter-channel correlation.

AI.tldr: UCF transforms multivariate time series to univariate via channel fusion (mean/median/DTW barycenter), enabling use of univariate classifiers for efficient MTSC with good performance.
AI.motivation: Existing deep learning approaches for multivariate time series classification require high computational resources, making them unsuitable for real-time applications and deployment on low-cost hardware like IoT devices and wearable systems.
AI.method: Proposes Univariate Channel Fusion (UCF) method that transforms multivariate time series into univariate representation through simple channel fusion strategies: mean, median, or dynamic time warping barycenter. This enables use of any classifier designed for univariate time series.
AI.result: UCF often outperforms baseline methods and state-of-the-art algorithms tailored for MTSC while achieving substantial gains in computational efficiency. Particularly effective in problems with high inter-channel correlation. Evaluated in five case studies covering chemical monitoring, brain-computer interfaces, and human activity analysis.
AI.conclusion: UCF provides a flexible and computationally lightweight alternative to complex models for multivariate time series classification, making it suitable for real-time applications and deployment on resource-constrained hardware.

title: GAViD: A Large-Scale Multimodal Dataset for Context-Aware Group Affect Recognition from Videos
summary: Understanding affective dynamics in real-world social systems is fundamental to modeling and analyzing human-human interactions in complex environments. Group affect emerges from intertwined human-human interactions, contextual influences, and behavioral cues, making its quantitative modeling a challenging computational social systems problem. However, computational modeling of group affect in in-the-wild scenarios remains challenging due to limited large-scale annotated datasets and the inherent complexity of multimodal social interactions shaped by contextual and behavioral variability. The lack of comprehensive datasets annotated with multimodal and contextual information further limits advances in the field. To address this, we introduce the Group Affect from ViDeos (GAViD) dataset, comprising 5091 video clips with multimodal data (video, audio and context), annotated with ternary valence and discrete emotion labels and enriched with VideoGPT-generated contextual metadata and human-annotated action cues. We also present Context-Aware Group Affect Recognition Network (CAGNet) for multimodal context-aware group affect recognition. CAGNet achieves 63.20\% test accuracy on GAViD, comparable to state-of-the-art performance. The dataset and code are available at github.com/deepakkumar-iitr/GAViD.

AI.tldr: Introduces GAViD dataset (5091 multimodal video clips) and CAGNet model for context-aware group affect recognition, achieving 63.20% accuracy.
AI.motivation: Group affect modeling in real-world social systems is challenging due to limited large-scale annotated datasets and complexity of multimodal social interactions with contextual variability.
AI.method: Created GAViD dataset with multimodal data (video, audio, context) annotated with valence/emotion labels, plus VideoGPT-generated metadata and human-annotated action cues. Developed CAGNet (Context-Aware Group Affect Recognition Network) for multimodal context-aware recognition.
AI.result: CAGNet achieves 63.20% test accuracy on GAViD dataset, comparable to state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: GAViD dataset addresses data limitations in group affect research, and CAGNet demonstrates effective context-aware modeling for group affect recognition in real-world scenarios.

title: Tabular foundation models for in-context prediction of molecular properties
summary: Accurate molecular property prediction is central to drug discovery, catalysis, and process design, yet real-world applications are often limited by small datasets. Molecular foundation models provide a promising direction by learning transferable molecular representations; however, they typically involve task-specific fine-tuning, require machine learning expertise, and often fail to outperform classical baselines. Tabular foundation models (TFMs) offer a fundamentally different paradigm: they perform predictions through in-context learning, enabling inference without task-specific training. Here, we evaluate TFMs in the low- to medium-data regime across both standardized pharmaceutical benchmarks and chemical engineering datasets. We evaluate both frozen molecular foundation model representations, as well as classical descriptors and fingerprints. Across the benchmarks, the approach shows excellent predictive performance while reducing computational cost, compared to fine-tuning, with these advantages also transferring to practical engineering data settings. In particular, combining TFMs with CheMeleon embeddings yields up to 100\% win rates on 30 MoleculeACE tasks, while compact RDKit2d and Mordred descriptors provide strong descriptor-based alternatives. Molecular representation emerges as a key determinant in TFM performance, with molecular foundation model embeddings and 2D descriptor sets both providing substantial gains over classic molecular fingerprints on many tasks. These results suggest that in-context learning with TFMs provides a highly accurate and cost-efficient alternative for property prediction in practical applications.

AI.tldr: TFMs (tabular foundation models) with in-context learning outperform fine-tuned molecular foundation models for property prediction in low-data regimes, using molecular embeddings and descriptors.
AI.motivation: Real-world molecular property prediction is limited by small datasets, and current molecular foundation models require task-specific fine-tuning, ML expertise, and often underperform classical baselines.
AI.method: Evaluate tabular foundation models (TFMs) using in-context learning across pharmaceutical benchmarks and chemical engineering datasets, testing frozen molecular foundation model embeddings, classical descriptors, and fingerprints.
AI.result: TFMs show excellent predictive performance with reduced computational cost vs fine-tuning. CheMeleon embeddings achieve up to 100% win rates on MoleculeACE tasks. RDKit2d and Mordred descriptors provide strong alternatives. Molecular representation is key to performance.
AI.conclusion: In-context learning with TFMs provides a highly accurate and cost-efficient alternative for molecular property prediction in practical applications, especially in low-data regimes.

title: Dental Panoramic Radiograph Analysis Using YOLO26 From Tooth Detection to Disease Diagnosis
summary: Panoramic radiography is a fundamental diagnostic tool in dentistry, offering a comprehensive view of the entire dentition with minimal radiation exposure. However, manual interpretation is time-consuming and prone to errors, especially in high-volume clinical settings. This creates a pressing need for efficient automated solutions. This study presents the first application of YOLOv26 for automated tooth detection, FDI-based numbering, and dental disease segmentation in panoramic radiographs. The DENTEX dataset was preprocessed using Roboflow for format conversion and augmentation, yielding 1,082 images for tooth enumeration and 1,040 images for disease segmentation across four pathology classes. Five YOLOv26-seg variants were trained on Google Colab using transfer learning at a resolution of 800x800. Results demonstrate that the YOLOv26m-seg model achieved the best performance for tooth enumeration, with a precision of 0.976, recall of 0.970, and box mAP50 of 0.976. It outperformed the YOLOv8x baseline by 4.9% in precision and 3.3% in mAP50, while also enabling high-quality mask-level segmentation (mask mAP50 = 0.970). For disease segmentation, the YOLOv26l-seg model attained a box mAP50 of 0.591 and a mask mAP50 of 0.547. Impacted teeth showed the highest per-class average precision (0.943), indicating that visual distinctiveness influences detection performance more than annotation quantity. Overall, these findings demonstrate that YOLOv26-based models offer a robust and accurate framework for automated dental image analysis, with strong potential to enhance diagnostic efficiency and consistency in clinical practice.

AI.tldr: First application of YOLOv26 for automated tooth detection, FDI numbering, and dental disease segmentation in panoramic radiographs, achieving high accuracy and outperforming YOLOv8 baseline.
AI.motivation: Manual interpretation of panoramic radiographs is time-consuming and error-prone in high-volume clinical settings, creating need for efficient automated solutions for dental image analysis.
AI.method: Used DENTEX dataset preprocessed with Roboflow (1,082 images for tooth enumeration, 1,040 for disease segmentation). Trained five YOLOv26-seg variants using transfer learning at 800x800 resolution on Google Colab.
AI.result: YOLOv26m-seg achieved best tooth enumeration performance: precision 0.976, recall 0.970, box mAP50 0.976, outperforming YOLOv8x by 4.9% precision and 3.3% mAP50. YOLOv26l-seg achieved disease segmentation: box mAP50 0.591, mask mAP50 0.547. Impacted teeth had highest AP (0.943).
AI.conclusion: YOLOv26-based models offer robust and accurate framework for automated dental image analysis with strong potential to enhance diagnostic efficiency and consistency in clinical practice.

title: Training Time Prediction for Mixed Precision-based Distributed Training
summary: Accurate prediction of training time in distributed deep learning is crucial for resource allocation, cost estimation, and job scheduling. We observe that the floating-point precision setting is a key determinant of training time, leading to training time variations of ~2.4x over its minimum. However, existing studies on distributed training time prediction rely on static model computation graphs that do not capture precision variations, including mixed precision. According to our experiments, training time prediction without considering precision results in significant prediction errors - reaching up to 147.85% in mean absolute percentage error (MAPE). To address this issue, we propose a precision-aware distributed training time predictor that achieves robust accuracy across diverse precision settings, including mixed precision, with 9.8% MAPE.

AI.tldr: Proposes precision-aware predictor for distributed deep learning training time that accounts for floating-point precision variations, achieving 9.8% MAPE vs 147.85% error from precision-unaware methods.
AI.motivation: Current distributed training time predictors use static computation graphs that don't capture precision variations, leading to large prediction errors (up to 147.85% MAPE). Precision settings cause ~2.4x training time variations, making accurate prediction crucial for resource allocation and scheduling.
AI.method: Develops a precision-aware distributed training time predictor that incorporates floating-point precision variations, including mixed precision settings, into the prediction model.
AI.result: Achieves robust accuracy across diverse precision settings with 9.8% mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), significantly better than precision-unaware methods that reach up to 147.85% MAPE.
AI.conclusion: Precision awareness is essential for accurate distributed training time prediction; the proposed method effectively addresses precision variations to enable reliable resource allocation and scheduling.

title: A Two-Stage, Object-Centric Deep Learning Framework for Robust Exam Cheating Detection
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures. Accepted at the FISU Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (FJCAI 2026), Vietnam
summary: Academic integrity continues to face the persistent challenge of examination cheating. Traditional invigilation relies on human observation, which is inefficient, costly, and prone to errors at scale. Although some existing AI-powered monitoring systems have been deployed and trusted, many lack transparency or require multi-layered architectures to achieve the desired performance. To overcome these challenges, we propose an improvement over a simple two-stage framework for exam cheating detection that integrates object detection and behavioral analysis using well-known technologies. First, the state-of-the-art YOLOv8n model is used to localize students in exam-room images. Each detected region is cropped and preprocessed, then classified by a fine-tuned RexNet-150 model as either normal or cheating behavior. The system is trained on a dataset compiled from 10 independent sources with a total of 273,897 samples, achieving 0.95 accuracy, 0.94 recall, 0.96 precision, and 0.95 F1-score - a 13\% increase over a baseline accuracy of 0.82 in video-based cheating detection. In addition, with an average inference time of 13.9 ms per sample, the proposed approach demonstrates robustness and scalability for deployment in large-scale environments. Beyond the technical contribution, the AI-assisted monitoring system also addresses ethical concerns by ensuring that final outcomes are delivered privately to individual students after the examination, for example, via personal email. This prevents public exposure or shaming and offers students an opportunity to reflect on their behavior. For further improvement, it is possible to incorporate additional factors, such as audio data and consecutive frames, to achieve greater accuracy. This study provides a foundation for developing real-time, scalable, ethical, and open-source solutions.

AI.tldr: A two-stage AI framework for exam cheating detection using YOLOv8n for student localization and fine-tuned RexNet-150 for behavior classification, achieving 95% accuracy with ethical privacy protections.
AI.motivation: Traditional human invigilation is inefficient, costly, and error-prone at scale. Existing AI monitoring systems often lack transparency or require complex architectures, creating a need for simpler, more effective solutions.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) YOLOv8n model localizes students in exam-room images, (2) cropped regions are classified by fine-tuned RexNet-150 model as normal or cheating behavior. Trained on dataset from 10 sources with 273,897 samples.
AI.result: Achieved 0.95 accuracy, 0.94 recall, 0.96 precision, and 0.95 F1-score - a 13% improvement over baseline accuracy of 0.82. Average inference time of 13.9 ms per sample, demonstrating scalability for large-scale deployment.
AI.conclusion: The system provides robust, scalable cheating detection with ethical privacy protections (private delivery of results). Future improvements could incorporate audio data and consecutive frames for greater accuracy, offering a foundation for real-time, open-source solutions.

title: JumpLoRA: Sparse Adapters for Continual Learning in Large Language Models
summary: Adapter-based methods have become a cost-effective approach to continual learning (CL) for Large Language Models (LLMs), by sequentially learning a low-rank update matrix for each task. To mitigate catastrophic forgetting, state-of-the-art approaches impose constraints on new adapters with respect to the previous ones, by targeting either subspace or coordinate-wise interference. In this paper, we propose JumpLoRA, a novel framework to adaptively induce sparsity in the Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) blocks through the use of JumpReLU gating. The method achieves dynamic parameter isolation, which helps prevent task interference. We demonstrate that our method is highly modular and compatible with LoRA-based CL approaches. Specifically, it significantly boosts the performance of IncLoRA and outperforms the leading state-of-the-art CL method, ELLA.

AI.tldr: JumpLoRA introduces adaptive sparsity in LoRA blocks using JumpReLU gating for dynamic parameter isolation, preventing task interference in continual learning for LLMs.
AI.motivation: Current adapter-based continual learning methods for LLMs struggle with catastrophic forgetting. While state-of-the-art approaches constrain new adapters relative to previous ones (targeting subspace or coordinate-wise interference), there's a need for more effective parameter isolation to prevent task interference.
AI.method: JumpLoRA framework adaptively induces sparsity in LoRA blocks using JumpReLU gating. This enables dynamic parameter isolation that prevents task interference. The method is modular and compatible with existing LoRA-based continual learning approaches.
AI.result: JumpLoRA significantly boosts the performance of IncLoRA and outperforms the leading state-of-the-art continual learning method, ELLA.
AI.conclusion: JumpLoRA provides an effective solution for continual learning in LLMs through adaptive sparsity and dynamic parameter isolation, offering modular compatibility with existing LoRA-based approaches while achieving superior performance.

title: CollideNet: Hierarchical Multi-scale Video Representation Learning with Disentanglement for Time-To-Collision Forecasting
summary: Time-to-Collision (TTC) forecasting is a critical task in collision prevention, requiring precise temporal prediction and comprehending both local and global patterns encapsulated in a video, both spatially and temporally. To address the multi-scale nature of video, we introduce a novel spatiotemporal hierarchical transformer-based architecture called CollideNet, specifically catered for effective TTC forecasting. In the spatial stream, CollideNet aggregates information for each video frame simultaneously at multiple resolutions. In the temporal stream, along with multi-scale feature encoding, CollideNet also disentangles the non-stationarity, trend, and seasonality components. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in comparison to prior works on three commonly used public datasets, setting a new state-of-the-art by a considerable margin. We conduct cross-dataset evaluations to analyze the generalization capabilities of our method, and visualize the effects of disentanglement of the trend and seasonality components of the video data. We release our code at https://github.com/DeSinister/CollideNet/.

AI.tldr: CollideNet: A spatiotemporal hierarchical transformer for Time-to-Collision forecasting that uses multi-scale spatial aggregation and temporal disentanglement of trend/seasonality components.
AI.motivation: TTC forecasting requires precise temporal prediction and understanding of both local and global spatiotemporal patterns in videos, addressing the multi-scale nature of video data.
AI.method: Introduces CollideNet with two streams: spatial stream aggregates information at multiple resolutions per frame; temporal stream performs multi-scale feature encoding and disentangles non-stationarity, trend, and seasonality components.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on three public datasets by a considerable margin, shows good generalization in cross-dataset evaluations, and provides visualization of trend/seasonality disentanglement effects.
AI.conclusion: CollideNet effectively addresses multi-scale spatiotemporal patterns for TTC forecasting through hierarchical transformer architecture with disentangled temporal components, setting new SOTA benchmarks.

title: Synthetic data in cryptocurrencies using generative models
summary: Data plays a fundamental role in consolidating markets, services, and products in the digital financial ecosystem. However, the use of real data, especially in the financial context, can lead to privacy risks and access restrictions, affecting institutions, research, and modeling processes. Although not all financial datasets present such limitations, this work proposes the use of deep learning techniques for generating synthetic data applied to cryptocurrency price time series. The approach is based on Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (CGANs), combining an LSTM-type recurrent generator and an MLP discriminator to produce statistically consistent synthetic data. The experiments consider different crypto-assets and demonstrate that the model is capable of reproducing relevant temporal patterns, preserving market trends and dynamics. The generation of synthetic series through GANs is an efficient alternative for simulating financial data, showing potential for applications such as market behavior analysis and anomaly detection, with lower computational cost compared to more complex generative approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using Conditional GANs with LSTM generator and MLP discriminator to generate synthetic cryptocurrency price time series that preserve statistical properties and temporal patterns while addressing privacy and access limitations of real financial data.
AI.motivation: Real financial data poses privacy risks and access restrictions that hinder institutions, research, and modeling. Synthetic data generation addresses these limitations while maintaining data utility for financial applications.
AI.method: Uses Conditional Generative Adversarial Networks (CGANs) with an LSTM-based recurrent generator and MLP discriminator to generate synthetic cryptocurrency price time series that preserve statistical consistency and temporal patterns.
AI.result: The model successfully reproduces relevant temporal patterns, preserves market trends and dynamics across different crypto-assets, and offers lower computational cost compared to more complex generative approaches.
AI.conclusion: GAN-based synthetic data generation is an efficient alternative for simulating financial data with applications in market behavior analysis and anomaly detection, addressing privacy concerns while maintaining data utility.

title: Find, Fix, Reason: Context Repair for Video Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning has advanced video reasoning in large multi-modal models, yet dominant pipelines either rely on on-policy self-exploration, which plateaus at the model's knowledge boundary, or hybrid replay that mixes policies and demands careful regularization. Dynamic context methods zoom into focused evidence but often require curated pretraining and two-stage tuning, and their context remains bounded by a small model's capability. In contrast, larger models excel at instruction following and multi-modal understanding, can supply richer context to smaller models, and rapidly zoom in on target regions via simple tools. Building on this capability, we introduce an observation-level intervention: a frozen, tool-integrated teacher identifies the missing spatiotemporal dependency and provides a minimal evidence patch (e.g., timestamps, regions etc.) from the original video while the question remains unchanged. The student answers again with the added context, and training updates with a chosen-rollout scheme integrated into Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). We further propose a Robust Improvement Reward (RIR) that aligns optimization with two goals: outcome validity through correct answers and dependency alignment through rationales that reflect the cited evidence. Advantages are group-normalized across the batch, preserving on-policy exploration while directing it along causally meaningful directions with minimal changes to the training stack. Experiments on various related benchmarks show consistent accuracy gains and strong generalization. Web page and source code will be available at https://github.com/JethroJames/FFR.git.

AI.tldr: FFR introduces a teacher-student framework where a frozen teacher identifies missing spatiotemporal dependencies and provides evidence patches to a student model, using Group Relative Policy Optimization with Robust Improvement Reward for better video reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current video reasoning methods either plateau at model knowledge boundaries (on-policy self-exploration) or require careful regularization (hybrid replay). Dynamic context methods need curated pretraining and are limited by small model capabilities. Larger models can provide richer context to smaller models but aren't effectively leveraged.
AI.method: Observation-level intervention: frozen tool-integrated teacher identifies missing spatiotemporal dependencies and provides minimal evidence patches (timestamps, regions). Student answers with added context, trained with chosen-rollout scheme in Group Relative Policy Optimization. Robust Improvement Reward aligns optimization with correct answers and dependency alignment through rationales reflecting cited evidence.
AI.result: Experiments on various benchmarks show consistent accuracy gains and strong generalization. The method preserves on-policy exploration while directing it along causally meaningful directions with minimal changes to training stack.
AI.conclusion: FFR effectively leverages larger models' capabilities to provide targeted evidence to smaller models, improving video reasoning through observation-level interventions and robust reward design that aligns optimization with both outcome validity and dependency alignment.

title: Sketching the Readout of Large Language Models for Scalable Data Attribution and Valuation
summary: Data attribution and valuation are critical for understanding data-model synergy for Large Language Models (LLMs), yet existing gradient-based methods suffer from scalability challenges on LLMs. Inspired by human cognition, where decision making relies on a focused readout of relevant memories rather than replaying all pathways, we introduce RISE (Readout Influence Sketching Estimator). Instead of computing and indexing gradients across the entire LLM, RISE focuses on influence hotspots at the output layer, where influence signals concentrate, and the gradient admits a decomposed outer-product form. This enables a dual-channel representation combining a lexical residual channel (RH) and a semantic projected-error channel (GH). Applying CountSketch projections to these channels achieves strong compression while maintaining accurate attribution. Across the OLMo (1B-32B) and Pythia (14M-6.9B) families, RISE reduces index storage by up to 112$\times$ compared to RapidIn and scales to 32B parameters LLM, where gradient-based baselines such as RapidIn and ZO-Inf become memory-infeasible. We evaluate RISE on two paradigms: (1) retrospective attribution, retrieving influential training examples for specific predictions, and (2) prospective valuation, scoring candidate data utility zero-shot. We validate RISE on three tasks: Howdy backdoor data detection, Finance-Medical domain separation, and Brain Rot high-quality data selection. In a closed-loop Brain Rot study, continued pretraining on RISE-selected data yields consistent downstream improvements. Overall, RISE provides a practical and scalable primitive for influence analysis and training-data selection in modern large language models.

AI.tldr: RISE is a scalable data attribution method for LLMs that uses output-layer influence hotspots and CountSketch compression, achieving 112× storage reduction and scaling to 32B parameters where gradient-based methods fail.
AI.motivation: Existing gradient-based data attribution methods for LLMs face scalability challenges due to the massive parameter space. The paper is inspired by human cognition where decision-making focuses on relevant memories rather than all pathways.
AI.method: RISE focuses on influence hotspots at the output layer where influence signals concentrate, using a decomposed outer-product gradient form. It creates dual-channel representation (lexical residual channel RH and semantic projected-error channel GH) and applies CountSketch projections for compression while maintaining accurate attribution.
AI.result: RISE reduces index storage by up to 112× compared to RapidIn, scales to 32B parameter LLMs where baselines become memory-infeasible, and performs well on retrospective attribution and prospective valuation tasks including backdoor detection, domain separation, and high-quality data selection.
AI.conclusion: RISE provides a practical and scalable primitive for influence analysis and training-data selection in modern large language models, enabling continued pretraining improvements through better data selection.

title: Where Do Vision-Language Models Fail? World Scale Analysis for Image Geolocalization
comment: Accepted to the CVPR EarthVision 2026 Workshop
summary: Image geolocalization has traditionally been addressed through retrieval-based place recognition or geometry-based visual localization pipelines. Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong zero-shot reasoning capabilities across multimodal tasks, yet their performance in geographic inference remains underexplored. In this work, we present a systematic evaluation of multiple state-of-the-art VLMs for country-level image geolocalization using ground-view imagery only. Instead of relying on image matching, GPS metadata, or task-specific training, we evaluate prompt-based country prediction in a zero-shot setting. The selected models are tested on three geographically diverse datasets to assess their robustness and generalization ability. Our results reveal substantial variation across models, highlighting the potential of semantic reasoning for coarse geolocalization and the limitations of current VLMs in capturing fine-grained geographic cues. This study provides the first focused comparison of modern VLMs for country-level geolocalization and establishes a foundation for future research at the intersection of multimodal reasoning and geographic understanding.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically evaluates state-of-the-art Vision-Language Models (VLMs) for zero-shot country-level image geolocalization using only ground-view imagery, without relying on traditional methods like image matching or GPS metadata.
AI.motivation: Traditional image geolocalization uses retrieval-based place recognition or geometry-based visual localization. While VLMs show strong zero-shot reasoning in multimodal tasks, their performance in geographic inference remains underexplored. The paper aims to evaluate VLMs' capabilities for country-level geolocalization in a zero-shot setting.
AI.method: The study evaluates multiple state-of-the-art VLMs using prompt-based country prediction in a zero-shot setting. Models are tested on three geographically diverse datasets to assess robustness and generalization. The approach uses only ground-view imagery without image matching, GPS metadata, or task-specific training.
AI.result: Results show substantial variation across different VLMs. The study reveals the potential of semantic reasoning for coarse geolocalization but also highlights limitations of current VLMs in capturing fine-grained geographic cues.
AI.conclusion: This work provides the first focused comparison of modern VLMs for country-level geolocalization and establishes a foundation for future research at the intersection of multimodal reasoning and geographic understanding.

title: OT on the Map: Quantifying Domain Shifts in Geographic Space
summary: In computer vision and machine learning for geographic data, out-of-domain generalization is a pervasive challenge, arising from uneven global data coverage and distribution shifts across geographic regions. Though models are frequently trained in one region and deployed in another, there is no principled method for determining when this cross-region adaptation will be successful. A well-defined notion of distance between distributions can effectively quantify how different a new target domain is compared to the domains used for model training, which in turn could support model training and deployment decisions. In this paper, we propose a strategy for computing distances between geospatial domains that leverages geographic information with Optimal Transport methods (GeoSpOT). In our experiments, GeoSpOT distances emerge as effective predictors of cross-domain transfer difficulty. We further demonstrate that embeddings from pretrained location encoders provide information comparable to image/text embeddings, despite relying solely on longitude-latitude pairs as input. This allows users to get an approximation of out-of-domain performance for geospatial models, even when the exact downstream task is unknown, or no task-specific data is available. Building on these findings, we show that GeoSpOT distances can preemptively guide data selection and enable predictive tools to analyze regions where a model is likely to underperform.

AI.tldr: GeoSpOT: A method using Optimal Transport to compute distances between geospatial domains, predicting cross-domain transfer difficulty and guiding model deployment decisions.
AI.motivation: Out-of-domain generalization is a major challenge in geospatial ML due to uneven global data coverage and distribution shifts. There's no principled method to determine when cross-region model adaptation will succeed, creating a need for ways to quantify domain differences to support training and deployment decisions.
AI.method: Proposes GeoSpOT (Geographic Optimal Transport), a strategy that leverages geographic information with Optimal Transport methods to compute distances between geospatial domains. Uses embeddings from pretrained location encoders (trained on longitude-latitude pairs) that provide comparable information to image/text embeddings.
AI.result: GeoSpOT distances effectively predict cross-domain transfer difficulty. Location encoder embeddings work well despite using only longitude-latitude inputs. The method allows approximation of out-of-domain performance even when downstream tasks are unknown or no task-specific data is available.
AI.conclusion: GeoSpOT distances can preemptively guide data selection and enable predictive tools to analyze regions where models are likely to underperform, providing a principled approach for geospatial model deployment decisions across different geographic domains.

title: Do Vision-Language Models Truly Perform Vision Reasoning? A Rigorous Study of the Modality Gap
summary: Reasoning in vision-language models (VLMs) has recently attracted significant attention due to its broad applicability across diverse downstream tasks. However, it remains unclear whether the superior performance of VLMs stems from genuine vision-grounded reasoning or relies predominantly on the reasoning capabilities of their textual backbones. To systematically measure this, we introduce CrossMath, a novel multimodal reasoning benchmark designed for controlled cross-modal comparisons. Specifically, we construct each problem in text-only, image-only, and image+text formats guaranteeing identical task-relevant information, verified by human annotators. This rigorous alignment effectively isolates modality-specific reasoning differences while eliminating confounding factors such as information mismatch. Extensive evaluation of state-of-the-art VLMs reveals a consistent phenomenon: a substantial performance gap between textual and visual reasoning. Notably, VLMs excel with text-only inputs, whereas incorporating visual data (image+text) frequently degrades performance compared to the text-only baseline. These findings indicate that current VLMs conduct reasoning primarily in the textual space, with limited genuine reliance on visual evidence. To mitigate this limitation, we curate a CrossMath training set for VLM fine-tuning. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that fine-tuning on this training set significantly boosts reasoning performance across all individual and joint modalities, while yielding robust gains on two general visual reasoning tasks. Source code is available at https://github.com/xuyige/CrossMath.

AI.tldr: CrossMath benchmark reveals VLMs rely more on textual reasoning than visual evidence, showing performance degradation when adding images to text-only inputs.
AI.motivation: To systematically measure whether VLMs' superior performance stems from genuine vision-grounded reasoning or primarily from their textual backbones' reasoning capabilities.
AI.method: Introduce CrossMath, a multimodal reasoning benchmark with text-only, image-only, and image+text formats containing identical task-relevant information, verified by human annotators. Evaluate state-of-the-art VLMs and create training set for fine-tuning.
AI.result: VLMs show substantial performance gap between textual and visual reasoning: excel with text-only inputs but degrade with image+text inputs. Fine-tuning on CrossMath training set significantly boosts reasoning across all modalities and general visual reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Current VLMs conduct reasoning primarily in textual space with limited genuine reliance on visual evidence. CrossMath benchmark enables controlled cross-modal comparisons and fine-tuning can mitigate this limitation.

title: Neuro-Symbolic ODE Discovery with Latent Grammar Flow
summary:
Understanding natural and engineered systems often relies on symbolic formulations, such as differential equations, which provide interpretability and transferability beyond black-box models.
  We introduce Latent Grammar Flow (LGF), a neuro-symbolic generative framework for discovering ordinary differential equations from data. LGF embeds equations as grammar-based representations into a discrete latent space and forces semantically similar equations to be positioned closer together with a behavioural loss. Then, a discrete flow model guides the sampling process to recursively generate candidate equations that best fit the observed data. Domain knowledge and constraints, such as stability, can be either embedded into the rules or used as conditional predictors.

AI.tldr: LGF is a neuro-symbolic framework that discovers ODEs from data using grammar-based representations in discrete latent space with behavioral loss and discrete flow sampling.
AI.motivation: Symbolic formulations like differential equations provide interpretability and transferability that black-box models lack, but discovering them from data is challenging.
AI.method: Embeds equations as grammar-based representations into discrete latent space, uses behavioral loss to cluster semantically similar equations, and employs discrete flow model to recursively generate candidate equations fitting observed data.
AI.result: A framework capable of discovering ordinary differential equations from data while incorporating domain knowledge and constraints like stability.
AI.conclusion: LGF provides a neuro-symbolic approach that combines the interpretability of symbolic models with the flexibility of neural methods for equation discovery.

title: Information Router for Mitigating Modality Dominance in Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision Language models (VLMs) have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of benchmarks, yet they often suffer from modality dominance, where predictions rely disproportionately on a single modality. Prior approaches primarily address this issue by steering model's attention allocation, implicitly assuming that all modalities provide sufficient information. However, attention only determines where the model focuses, and cannot enrich information that is missing or ambiguous. In the real world, input modalities often differ in information density and their signal-to-noise ratios. In such cases, simply adjusting model's attention does not resolve the underlying lack of information. In this paper, we propose \textsc{MoIR}: \textit{Multi-modal Information Router}, an information-level fusion method that explicitly reduces information disparity prior to fusion. \textsc{MoIR} identifies less informative tokens and routes complementary information from a stronger modality, constructing information-dense token representations before they are processed by a large language model. By modifying information availability, \textsc{MoIR} enables reliable shifts in modality dominance, even when one modality is degraded. We evaluate \textsc{MoIR} on three widely used multi-modal benchmarks across multiple model backbones. Experimental results show that \textsc{MoIR} consistently demonstrates more balanced modality contribution, and improves robustness and downstream performance, particularly even under modality degradation. These findings demonstrate that explicitly modifying cross-modal information is an effective and complementary strategy for mitigating modality dominance in multi-modal reasoning models.

AI.tldr: MoIR is a multi-modal fusion method that reduces information disparity between modalities by routing complementary information from stronger to weaker modalities before fusion, addressing modality dominance more effectively than attention-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs suffer from modality dominance where predictions rely disproportionately on one modality. Existing attention-based approaches assume all modalities provide sufficient information, but in reality, modalities often differ in information density and signal-to-noise ratios, making attention adjustment insufficient when information is missing or ambiguous.
AI.method: MoIR (Multi-modal Information Router) is an information-level fusion method that identifies less informative tokens and routes complementary information from stronger modalities to construct information-dense token representations before processing by LLMs. It explicitly reduces information disparity prior to fusion rather than just adjusting attention.
AI.result: MoIR consistently demonstrates more balanced modality contribution across three widely used multi-modal benchmarks with multiple model backbones. It improves robustness and downstream performance, particularly under modality degradation conditions.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modifying cross-modal information availability is an effective and complementary strategy for mitigating modality dominance in multi-modal reasoning models, addressing the fundamental issue of information disparity rather than just attention allocation.

title: Enhancing AI and Dynamical Subseasonal Forecasts with Probabilistic Bias Correction
summary: Decision-makers rely on weather forecasts to plant crops, manage wildfires, allocate water and energy, and prepare for weather extremes. Today, such forecasts enjoy unprecedented accuracy out to two weeks thanks to steady advances in physics-based dynamical models and data-driven artificial intelligence (AI) models. However, model skill drops precipitously at subseasonal timescales (2 - 6 weeks ahead), due to compounding errors and persistent biases. To counter this degradation, we introduce probabilistic bias correction (PBC), a machine learning framework that substantially reduces systematic error by learning to correct historical probabilistic forecasts. When applied to the leading dynamical and AI models from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF), PBC doubles the subseasonal skill of the AI Forecasting System and improves the skill of the operationally-debiased dynamical model for 91% of pressure, 92% of temperature, and 98% of precipitation targets. We designed PBC for operational deployment, and, in ECMWF's 2025 real-time forecasting competition, its global forecasts placed first for all weather variables and lead times, outperforming the dynamical models from six operational forecasting centers, an international dynamical multi-model ensemble, ECMWF's AI Forecasting System, and the forecasting systems of 34 teams worldwide. These probabilistic skill gains translate into more accurate prediction of extreme events and have the potential to improve agricultural planning, energy management, and disaster preparedness in vulnerable communities.

AI.tldr: PBC (probabilistic bias correction) is a machine learning framework that doubles subseasonal forecast skill by correcting systematic errors in leading weather models, winning ECMWF's 2025 forecasting competition.
AI.motivation: Current weather forecast models experience significant skill degradation at subseasonal timescales (2-6 weeks ahead) due to compounding errors and persistent biases, limiting their usefulness for critical applications like agriculture, wildfire management, and disaster preparedness.
AI.method: Probabilistic bias correction (PBC) - a machine learning framework that learns to correct historical probabilistic forecasts by reducing systematic errors. It was applied to both dynamical and AI models from ECMWF.
AI.result: PBC doubled the subseasonal skill of ECMWF's AI Forecasting System and improved the operationally-debiased dynamical model for 91% of pressure, 92% of temperature, and 98% of precipitation targets. It won ECMWF's 2025 real-time forecasting competition, outperforming all other models and teams.
AI.conclusion: PBC represents a significant advancement in subseasonal forecasting, with practical applications for agricultural planning, energy management, and disaster preparedness in vulnerable communities through more accurate extreme event prediction.

title: Hero-Mamba: Mamba-based Dual Domain Learning for Underwater Image Enhancement
summary: Underwater images often suffer from severe degradation, such as color distortion, low contrast, and blurred details, due to light absorption and scattering in water. While learning-based methods like CNNs and Transformers have shown promise, they face critical limitations: CNNs struggle to model the long-range dependencies needed for non-uniform degradation, and Transformers incur quadratic computational complexity, making them inefficient for high-resolution images. To address these challenges, we propose Hero-Mamba, a novel Mamba-based network that achieves efficient dual-domain learning for underwater image enhancement. Our approach uniquely processes information from both the spatial domain (RGB image) and the spectral domain (FFT components) in parallel. This dual-domain input allows the network to decouple degradation factors, separating color/brightness information from texture/noise. The core of our network utilizes Mamba-based SS2D blocks to capture global receptive fields and long-range dependencies with linear complexity, overcoming the limitations of both CNNs and Transformers. Furthermore, we introduce a ColorFusion block, guided by a background light prior, to restore color information with high fidelity. Extensive experiments on the LSUI and UIEB benchmark datasets demonstrate that Hero-Mamba outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Notably, our model achieves a PSNR of 25.802 and an SSIM of 0.913 on LSUI, validating its superior performance and generalization capabilities.

AI.tldr: Hero-Mamba: A Mamba-based network for underwater image enhancement using efficient dual-domain learning (spatial RGB + spectral FFT) with linear complexity, outperforming CNN and Transformer methods.
AI.motivation: Underwater images suffer from severe degradation (color distortion, low contrast, blurred details) due to light absorption and scattering.
