Existing learning-based methods have limitations: CNNs struggle with long-range dependencies for non-uniform degradation, while Transformers have quadratic computational complexity that's inefficient for high-resolution images.
AI.method: Proposes Hero-Mamba, a Mamba-based network that performs efficient dual-domain learning by processing both spatial domain (RGB images) and spectral domain (FFT components) in parallel. Uses Mamba-based SS2D blocks to capture global receptive fields with linear complexity. Introduces ColorFusion block guided by background light prior to restore color information with high fidelity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on LSUI and UIEB benchmark datasets show Hero-Mamba outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Achieves PSNR of 25.802 and SSIM of 0.913 on LSUI, demonstrating superior performance and generalization capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Hero-Mamba effectively addresses limitations of CNNs and Transformers for underwater image enhancement through efficient dual-domain learning with linear complexity, achieving state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets.

title: Detecting and Suppressing Reward Hacking with Gradient Fingerprints
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) typically optimizes for outcome rewards without imposing constraints on intermediate reasoning. This leaves training susceptible to reward hacking, where models exploit loopholes (e.g., spurious patterns in training data) in the reward function to achieve high scores without solving the intended task. These reward-hacking behaviors are often implicit, as the intermediate chain-of-thought (CoT) may appear plausible on the surface, limiting the effectiveness of purely text-based monitoring. We propose Gradient Fingerprint (GRIFT), a method for detecting reward hacking using models' internal computations. Given a prompt and a model-generated CoT, GRIFT computes gradients of the CoT conditioned on the prompt and compresses them into a compact representation, which is then used to assess whether the CoT reflects reward hacking behavior. Across verifiable reasoning benchmarks spanning math, code, and logical reasoning, GRIFT substantially outperforms strong baselines, including CoT Monitor and TRACE, achieving over 25% relative improvement in detecting reward hacking behavior. Moreover, integrating GRIFT into the rejection fine-tuning pipeline for reasoning tasks reduces reward hacking and improves performance on the true task objective. Our results highlight a promising direction of leveraging gradient level representations for assessing the quality of CoT reasoning traces. Our code is available at: https://github.com/songtao-x/reward_hack.

AI.tldr: GRIFT detects reward hacking in RL by analyzing gradient fingerprints of chain-of-thought reasoning, outperforming baselines by 25%+ and improving true task performance when integrated into fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: RL with verifiable rewards is vulnerable to reward hacking where models exploit loopholes to achieve high scores without solving the intended task. Current text-based monitoring fails to detect these implicit behaviors in chain-of-thought reasoning.
AI.method: GRIFT computes gradients of chain-of-thought reasoning conditioned on prompts, compresses them into compact gradient fingerprint representations, and uses these to detect reward hacking behavior.
AI.result: GRIFT achieves over 25% relative improvement in detecting reward hacking compared to baselines (CoT Monitor and TRACE) across math, code, and logical reasoning benchmarks. Integration into rejection fine-tuning reduces reward hacking and improves true task performance.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-level representations are promising for assessing chain-of-thought reasoning quality and detecting reward hacking, offering a more effective approach than purely text-based monitoring methods.

title: Joint-Centric Dual Contrastive Alignment with Structure-Preserving and Information-Balanced Regularization
summary: We propose HILBERT (HIerarchical Long-sequence Balanced Embedding with Reciprocal contrastive Training), a cross-attentive multimodal framework for learning document-level audio-text representations from long, segmented sequences in low-resource data settings. HILBERT leverages frozen pre-trained speech and language encoders to extract segment-level features, which are aggregated via cross-modal attention and self-attentive pooling to form modality-specific document representations and a joint cross-attentive embedding. To align modalities while preserving modality-specific structure under severe audio-text dimensional imbalance, we introduce a reciprocal dual contrastive objective that simultaneously aligns audio-to-joint and text-to-joint representations, rather than directly contrasting audio and text alone. Two auxiliary regularizers further stabilize long-sequence fusion: a Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) loss that preserves structural consistency between each modality and the joint embedding, and a mutual information balancing loss that prevents dominance of a single modality by equalizing information flow from audio and text into the joint space. For downstream prediction, HILBERT employs a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) classifier over concatenated audio, text, and joint representations to accommodate heterogeneous label regimes. Extensive evaluation across multiple audio-text backbone combinations demonstrates that HILBERT learns semantically meaningful long-sequence representations and achieves superior performance on highly imbalanced multi-class settings.

AI.tldr: HILBERT is a cross-attentive multimodal framework for learning document-level audio-text representations from long, segmented sequences in low-resource settings, using reciprocal contrastive training and auxiliary regularizers to handle severe dimensional imbalance.
AI.motivation: To learn document-level audio-text representations from long, segmented sequences in low-resource data settings while addressing severe audio-text dimensional imbalance and preserving modality-specific structure.
AI.method: Uses frozen pre-trained speech/language encoders, cross-modal attention with self-attentive pooling, reciprocal dual contrastive objective (audio-to-joint and text-to-joint alignment), CKA loss for structural consistency, mutual information balancing loss, and MoE classifier for downstream prediction.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation shows HILBERT learns semantically meaningful long-sequence representations and achieves superior performance on highly imbalanced multi-class settings across multiple audio-text backbone combinations.
AI.conclusion: HILBERT effectively addresses long-sequence multimodal representation learning in low-resource settings with severe dimensional imbalance through reciprocal contrastive training and auxiliary regularization techniques.

title: VEFX-Bench: A Holistic Benchmark for Generic Video Editing and Visual Effects
summary: As AI-assisted video creation becomes increasingly practical, instruction-guided video editing has become essential for refining generated or captured footage to meet professional requirements. Yet the field still lacks both a large-scale human-annotated dataset with complete editing examples and a standardized evaluator for comparing editing systems. Existing resources are limited by small scale, missing edited outputs, or the absence of human quality labels, while current evaluation often relies on expensive manual inspection or generic vision-language model judges that are not specialized for editing quality. We introduce VEFX-Dataset, a human-annotated dataset containing 5,049 video editing examples across 9 major editing categories and 32 subcategories, each labeled along three decoupled dimensions: Instruction Following, Rendering Quality, and Edit Exclusivity. Building on VEFX-Dataset, we propose VEFX-Reward, a reward model designed specifically for video editing quality assessment. VEFX-Reward jointly processes the source video, the editing instruction, and the edited video, and predicts per-dimension quality scores via ordinal regression. We further release VEFX-Bench, a benchmark of 300 curated video-prompt pairs for standardized comparison of editing systems. Experiments show that VEFX-Reward aligns more strongly with human judgments than generic VLM judges and prior reward models on both standard IQA/VQA metrics and group-wise preference evaluation. Using VEFX-Reward as an evaluator, we benchmark representative commercial and open-source video editing systems, revealing a persistent gap between visual plausibility, instruction following, and edit locality in current models.

AI.tldr: VEFX introduces a large-scale human-annotated video editing dataset (5,049 examples), a specialized reward model for video editing quality assessment, and a benchmark for standardized evaluation of video editing systems.
AI.motivation: The field lacks both large-scale human-annotated datasets with complete editing examples and standardized evaluators for comparing video editing systems. Existing resources are limited by small scale, missing edited outputs, or absence of human quality labels, while current evaluation relies on expensive manual inspection or generic vision-language models not specialized for editing quality.
AI.method: 1) Created VEFX-Dataset: 5,049 human-annotated video editing examples across 9 major categories and 32 subcategories, labeled along three dimensions (Instruction Following, Rendering Quality, Edit Exclusivity). 2) Developed VEFX-Reward: A specialized reward model that jointly processes source video, editing instruction, and edited video to predict per-dimension quality scores via ordinal regression. 3) Released VEFX-Bench: A benchmark of 300 curated video-prompt pairs for standardized system comparison.
AI.result: VEFX-Reward aligns more strongly with human judgments than generic VLM judges and prior reward models on both standard IQA/VQA metrics and group-wise preference evaluation. Benchmarking of commercial and open-source video editing systems reveals persistent gaps between visual plausibility, instruction following, and edit locality in current models.
AI.conclusion: The VEFX framework provides comprehensive resources for advancing instruction-guided video editing, including a large-scale annotated dataset, specialized evaluation model, and standardized benchmark, addressing critical gaps in the field and enabling more systematic development and comparison of video editing systems.

title: Beyond Distribution Sharpening: The Importance of Task Rewards
summary: Frontier models have demonstrated exceptional capabilities following the integration of task-reward-based reinforcement learning (RL) into their training pipelines, enabling systems to evolve from pure reasoning models into sophisticated agents. However, debate persists regarding whether RL genuinely instills new skills within a base model or merely sharpens its existing distribution to elicit latent capabilities. To address this dichotomy, we present an explicit comparison between distribution sharpening and task-reward-based learning, utilizing RL as a tool to implement both paradigms. Our analysis reveals the inherent limitations of distribution sharpening, demonstrating from first principles how and why the optima can be unfavorable and the approach fundamentally unstable. Furthermore, our experiments using Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 on math datasets confirm that sharpening yields limited gains, whereas incorporating task-based reward signal can greatly help achieve robust performance improvements and stable learning.

AI.tldr: RL with task rewards improves frontier models beyond mere distribution sharpening, enabling genuine skill acquisition rather than just eliciting latent capabilities.
AI.motivation: To resolve the debate about whether RL instills new skills in base models or merely sharpens existing distributions to reveal latent capabilities.
AI.method: Explicit comparison between distribution sharpening and task-reward-based learning using RL as implementation tool, with experiments on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 models on math datasets.
AI.result: Distribution sharpening has inherent limitations with unfavorable optima and fundamental instability, yielding limited gains. Task-based reward signals enable robust performance improvements and stable learning.
AI.conclusion: Task-reward-based RL genuinely enhances model capabilities beyond mere distribution sharpening, providing stable learning and substantial performance improvements.

title: Enhancing Hazy Wildlife Imagery: AnimalHaze3k and IncepDehazeGan
summary: Atmospheric haze significantly degrades wildlife imagery, impeding computer vision applications critical for conservation, such as animal detection, tracking, and behavior analysis. To address this challenge, we introduce AnimalHaze3k a synthetic dataset comprising of 3,477 hazy images generated from 1,159 clear wildlife photographs through a physics-based pipeline. Our novel IncepDehazeGan architecture combines inception blocks with residual skip connections in a GAN framework, achieving state-of-the-art performance (SSIM: 0.8914, PSNR: 20.54, and LPIPS: 0.1104), delivering 6.27% higher SSIM and 10.2% better PSNR than competing approaches. When applied to downstream detection tasks, dehazed images improved YOLOv11 detection mAP by 112% and IoU by 67%. These advances can provide ecologists with reliable tools for population monitoring and surveillance in challenging environmental conditions, demonstrating significant potential for enhancing wildlife conservation efforts through robust visual analytics.

AI.tldr: Researchers created AnimalHaze3k, a synthetic dataset of 3,477 hazy wildlife images, and developed IncepDehazeGan, a GAN-based dehazing model that outperforms existing methods and significantly improves animal detection performance in hazy conditions.
AI.motivation: Atmospheric haze degrades wildlife imagery, hindering computer vision applications essential for conservation tasks like animal detection, tracking, and behavior analysis. There's a need for effective dehazing solutions tailored to wildlife photography in challenging environmental conditions.
AI.method: 1) Created AnimalHaze3k dataset: 3,477 synthetic hazy images generated from 1,159 clear wildlife photos using physics-based pipeline. 2) Developed IncepDehazeGan architecture: combines inception blocks with residual skip connections in a GAN framework for image dehazing.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance: SSIM 0.8914, PSNR 20.54, LPIPS 0.1104 (6.27% higher SSIM and 10.2% better PSNR than competing approaches). Downstream detection improvements: YOLOv11 detection mAP increased by 112% and IoU by 67% when using dehazed images.
AI.conclusion: The AnimalHaze3k dataset and IncepDehazeGan architecture provide ecologists with reliable tools for population monitoring and surveillance in challenging environmental conditions, demonstrating significant potential for enhancing wildlife conservation through robust visual analytics.

title: FL-MHSM: Spatially-adaptive Fusion and Ensemble Learning for Flood-Landslide Multi-Hazard Susceptibility Mapping at Regional Scale
summary: Existing multi-hazard susceptibility mapping (MHSM) studies often rely on spatially uniform models, treat hazards independently, and provide limited representation of cross-hazard dependence and uncertainty. To address these limitations, this study proposes a deep learning (DL) workflow for joint flood-landslide multi-hazard susceptibility mapping (FL-MHSM) that combines two-level spatial partitioning, probabilistic Early Fusion (EF), a tree-based Late Fusion (LF) baseline, and a soft-gating Mixture of Experts (MoE) model, with MoE serving as final predictive model. The proposed design preserves spatial heterogeneity through zonal partitions and enables data-parallel large-area prediction using overlapping lattice grids. In Kerala, EF remained competitive with LF, improving flood recall from 0.816 to 0.840 and reducing Brier score from 0.092 to 0.086, while MoE provided strongest performance for flood susceptibility, achieving an AUC-ROC of 0.905, recall of 0.930, and F1-score of 0.722. In Nepal, EF similarly improved flood recall from 0.820 to 0.858 and reduced Brier score from 0.057 to 0.049 relative to LF, while MoE outperformed both EF and LF for landslide susceptibility, achieving an AUC-ROC of 0.914, recall of 0.901, and F1-score of 0.559. GeoDetector analysis of MoE outputs further showed that dominant factors varied more across zones in Kerala, where susceptibility was shaped by different combinations of topographic, land-cover, and drainage-related controls, while Nepal showed a more consistent influence of topographic and glacier-related factors across zones. These findings show that EF and LF provide complementary predictive behavior, and that their spatially adaptive integration through MoE yields robust overall predictive performance for FL-MHSM while supporting interpretable characterization of multi-hazard susceptibility in spatially heterogeneous landscapes.

AI.tldr: Proposed deep learning workflow for joint flood-landslide susceptibility mapping using spatial partitioning, probabilistic Early Fusion, tree-based Late Fusion, and soft-gating Mixture of Experts model to address limitations of existing uniform models.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-hazard susceptibility mapping studies rely on spatially uniform models, treat hazards independently, and provide limited representation of cross-hazard dependence and uncertainty, failing to capture spatial heterogeneity.
AI.method: Deep learning workflow combining two-level spatial partitioning (zonal partitions and overlapping lattice grids), probabilistic Early Fusion, tree-based Late Fusion baseline, and soft-gating Mixture of Experts as final predictive model for joint flood-landslide mapping.
AI.result: In Kerala, MoE achieved best flood performance (AUC-ROC 0.905, recall 0.930, F1 0.722); in Nepal, MoE best for landslides (AUC-ROC 0.914, recall 0.901, F1 0.559). EF improved flood recall and reduced Brier scores in both regions. GeoDetector analysis revealed different dominant factors across zones.
AI.conclusion: Early Fusion and Late Fusion provide complementary predictive behavior, and their spatially adaptive integration through Mixture of Experts yields robust performance for flood-landslide susceptibility mapping while supporting interpretable characterization in spatially heterogeneous landscapes.

title: Repurposing 3D Generative Model for Autoregressive Layout Generation
summary: We introduce LaviGen, a framework that repurposes 3D generative models for 3D layout generation. Unlike previous methods that infer object layouts from textual descriptions, LaviGen operates directly in the native 3D space, formulating layout generation as an autoregressive process that explicitly models geometric relations and physical constraints among objects, producing coherent and physically plausible 3D scenes. To further enhance this process, we propose an adapted 3D diffusion model that integrates scene, object, and instruction information and employs a dual-guidance self-rollout distillation mechanism to improve efficiency and spatial accuracy. Extensive experiments on the LayoutVLM benchmark show LaviGen achieves superior 3D layout generation performance, with 19% higher physical plausibility than the state of the art and 65% faster computation. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/fenghora/LaviGen.

AI.tldr: LaviGen is a framework that repurposes 3D generative models for 3D layout generation, operating directly in 3D space with autoregressive modeling of geometric relations and physical constraints, achieving state-of-the-art performance with improved plausibility and speed.
AI.motivation: Previous methods infer object layouts from textual descriptions, but there's a need for direct 3D space operation that explicitly models geometric relations and physical constraints to produce coherent and physically plausible 3D scenes.
AI.method: LaviGen formulates layout generation as an autoregressive process in native 3D space, using an adapted 3D diffusion model that integrates scene, object, and instruction information with a dual-guidance self-rollout distillation mechanism for efficiency and spatial accuracy.
AI.result: On the LayoutVLM benchmark, LaviGen achieves superior 3D layout generation performance with 19% higher physical plausibility than state-of-the-art methods and 65% faster computation.
AI.conclusion: LaviGen demonstrates that repurposing 3D generative models for layout generation with explicit geometric and physical constraint modeling in native 3D space leads to more coherent, physically plausible scenes with improved efficiency.

title: Evaluating the Progression of Large Language Model Capabilities for Small-Molecule Drug Design
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have the potential to accelerate small molecule drug design due to their ability to reason about information from diverse sources and formats. However, their practical utility remains unclear due to the lack of benchmarks that reflect real-world scenarios. In this work, we introduce a suite of chemically-grounded tasks spanning molecular property prediction, molecular representation transformations, and molecular design. Importantly, we formulate these tasks as reinforcement learning (RL) environments, enabling a unified approach for evaluation and post-training. Across three model families, we find that frontier models are increasingly proficient at chemical tasks, but that there is significant room for improvement, especially in experimental settings with low data. Critically, we show that RL-based post-training can substantially improve performance. A smaller model post-trained on our environments becomes competitive with state-of-the-art frontier models, despite a significantly weaker base model. This suggests a practical route toward employing LLMs in drug discovery; by combining carefully-designed evaluation tasks with targeted post-training, we can both elucidate and close critical capability gaps.

AI.tldr: LLMs show promise for drug design but need better real-world benchmarks. Authors create chemical tasks as RL environments, find frontier models improving but need work, and show RL post-training boosts smaller models to compete with larger ones.
AI.motivation: LLMs have potential for accelerating small molecule drug design due to their reasoning capabilities across diverse information, but practical utility is unclear due to lack of benchmarks reflecting real-world scenarios.
AI.method: Introduced a suite of chemically-grounded tasks spanning molecular property prediction, molecular representation transformations, and molecular design, formulated as reinforcement learning environments for unified evaluation and post-training. Tested across three model families and applied RL-based post-training.
AI.result: Frontier models show increasing proficiency at chemical tasks but significant room for improvement, especially in low-data experimental settings. RL-based post-training substantially improves performance - a smaller post-trained model becomes competitive with state-of-the-art frontier models despite weaker base model.
AI.conclusion: Combining carefully-designed evaluation tasks with targeted RL post-training provides a practical route for employing LLMs in drug discovery, enabling both elucidation and closure of critical capability gaps.

title: Geometric regularization of autoencoders via observed stochastic dynamics
summary: Stochastic dynamical systems with slow or metastable behavior evolve, on long time scales, on an unknown low-dimensional manifold in high-dimensional ambient space. Building a reduced simulator from short-burst ambient ensembles is a long-standing problem: local-chart methods like ATLAS suffer from exponential landmark scaling and per-step reprojection, while autoencoder alternatives leave tangent-bundle geometry poorly constrained, and the errors propagate into the learned drift and diffusion. We observe that the ambient covariance~$Λ$ already encodes coordinate-invariant tangent-space information, its range spanning the tangent bundle. Using this, we construct a tangent-bundle penalty and an inverse-consistency penalty for a three-stage pipeline (chart learning, latent drift, latent diffusion) that learns a single nonlinear chart and the latent SDE. The penalties induce a function-space metric, the $ρ$-metric, strictly weaker than the Sobolev $H^1$ norm yet achieving the same chart-quality generalization rate up to logarithmic factors. For the drift, we derive an encoder-pullback target via Itô's formula on the learned encoder and prove a bias decomposition showing the standard decoder-side formula carries systematic error for any imperfect chart. Under a $W^{2,\infty}$ chart-convergence assumption, chart-level error propagates controllably to weak convergence of the ambient dynamics and to convergence of radial mean first-passage times. Experiments on four surfaces embedded in up to $201$ ambient dimensions reduce radial MFPT error by $50$--$70\%$ under rotation dynamics and achieve the lowest inter-well MFPT error on most surface--transition pairs under metastable Müller--Brown Langevin dynamics, while reducing end-to-end ambient coefficient errors by up to an order of magnitude relative to an unregularized autoencoder.

AI.tldr: A method for learning low-dimensional manifolds and latent SDEs from short-burst ambient ensembles using covariance-based tangent bundle regularization, achieving better generalization and reduced error in simulating metastable dynamics.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for building reduced simulators from short-burst ambient ensembles have limitations: local-chart methods suffer from exponential landmark scaling, while autoencoders poorly constrain tangent-bundle geometry, leading to error propagation in learned drift and diffusion.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: 1) chart learning, 2) latent drift, 3) latent diffusion. Uses ambient covariance to construct tangent-bundle penalty and inverse-consistency penalty. Introduces ρ-metric for function-space regularization. Derives encoder-pullback target via Itô's formula to avoid systematic error in drift estimation.
AI.result: Achieves same chart-quality generalization rate as Sobolev H¹ norm up to logarithmic factors. Reduces radial MFPT error by 50-70% under rotation dynamics. Achieves lowest inter-well MFPT error on most surface-transition pairs under metastable Müller-Brown Langevin dynamics. Reduces end-to-end ambient coefficient errors by up to an order of magnitude compared to unregularized autoencoder.
AI.conclusion: The covariance-based regularization approach effectively learns low-dimensional manifolds and latent SDEs from short-burst data, overcoming limitations of existing methods and achieving superior performance in simulating metastable dynamical systems.
